notes: BRCA1 missense variants range from benign polymorphisms to pathogenic mutations. Clinical significance depends on domain location and functional studies. Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) are common. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; Asn372 BRCA2 protein with standard RAD51 interaction capacity and homologous recombination-mediated DSB repair function.

CA.description: Heterozygous; one His372 allele may moderately alter BRCA2 protein folding or RAD51 filament assembly interactions at the OB fold domains.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate (His372); both copies carry the missense variant, associated with subtle alterations in BRCA2-RAD51 complex stability.

description: BRCA2 encodes a tumor suppressor essential for homologous recombination repair of DNA double-strand breaks through its role in loading RAD51 onto ssDNA resection intermediates. The rs144848 (N372H, Asn372His) variant in exon 10 is a common coding polymorphism distinct from rare pathogenic BRCA2 mutations. Population studies have produced conflicting results regarding its modest contribution to breast and ovarian cancer risk.

sources.name: Healey et al. (1995) - Analysis of the BRCA2 gene in 172 families with two or more cases of breast or ovarian cancer

notes: BRCA2 N372H is a common polymorphism; NOT equivalent to rare pathogenic BRCA2 mutations. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard ERCC1-XPF endonuclease complex efficiently removes bulky DNA adducts and UV photoproducts via nucleotide excision repair.

CT.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in ERCC1 NER activity may modestly increase persistence of bulky DNA adducts from UV or chemical carcinogens.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with reduced ERCC1 function, potentially allowing more carcinogen-DNA adducts to persist and drive mutagenesis.

description: ERCC1 forms a heterodimer with XPF to function as the 5' endonuclease that makes the critical incision during nucleotide excision repair (NER) of bulky DNA adducts, UV photoproducts, and interstrand crosslinks. ERCC1 is also required for interstrand crosslink repair and is relevant to platinum chemotherapy response. Variants in ERCC1 have been associated with altered cancer risk and treatment efficacy.

sources.name: Park et al. (2002) - ERCC1 polymorphisms and lung cancer risk

notes: ERCC1 variants also affect response to platinum-based chemotherapy; clinically relevant in oncology. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AA.description: Homozygous PALB2 variant, significantly increased breast cancer susceptibility

AG.description: PALB2 carrier, moderately increased breast cancer risk (~2-4x lifetime risk)

GG.description: Wild-type PALB2, population-average cancer susceptibility

description: PALB2 (Partner and Localizer of BRCA2) variant impairs BRCA2-mediated homologous recombination repair. Moderate-to-high penetrance breast cancer susceptibility gene.
notes: PALB2 bridges BRCA1 and BRCA2 in the DNA repair complex. Pathogenic variants confer ~35% lifetime breast cancer risk, now classified alongside BRCA1/2 for clinical genetic testing. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard CYP1A1 promoter activity with baseline inducibility by AhR ligands and lower carcinogen activation potential.

TC.description: Heterozygous; one variant allele moderately increases CYP1A1 mRNA inducibility upon xenobiotic exposure, elevating phase I carcinogen activation capacity.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; MspI variant associated with enhanced CYP1A1 inducibility and greater conversion of environmental carcinogens to reactive electrophiles.

description: The rs4646903 variant is located in the 3' untranslated region of CYP1A1 and is in strong linkage disequilibrium with the MspI polymorphism. It affects CYP1A1 mRNA stability and inducibility by AhR pathway activators. Higher CYP1A1 induction increases the conversion of environmental carcinogens (PAHs, heterocyclic amines) to DNA-reactive intermediates, elevating mutagenic burden in exposed tissues.

sources.name: Cascorbi et al. (2001) - Homozygous rapid arylamine N-acetyltransferase 2 genotype as a susceptibility factor for lung cancer risk

notes: rs4646903 and rs1048943 are in LD; combined haplotype increases carcinogen activation risk with smoking. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; baseline CYP1A1 inducibility with standard metabolic activation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons to carcinogenic epoxides.

AG.description: Heterozygous; one Ile462Val allele moderately increases CYP1A1 enzyme activity, with intermediate capacity to metabolize PAHs to reactive intermediates.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; the Ile462Val substitution increases CYP1A1 catalytic efficiency, potentially generating more carcinogenic PAH metabolites upon exposure.

description: CYP1A1 encodes cytochrome P450 1A1, a phase I enzyme that metabolically activates polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and aromatic amines to reactive carcinogenic intermediates through epoxidation. The rs1048943 (Ile462Val) variant in exon 7 increases enzyme catalytic activity. CYP1A1 induction is also triggered by AhR ligands including dioxins and tobacco smoke components.

sources.name: Cascorbi et al. (2001) - Homozygous rapid arylamine N-acetyltransferase 2 genotype as a susceptibility factor for lung cancer risk

notes: CYP1A1 activation risk is substantially elevated by tobacco and PAH exposure. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AA.description: Protective genotype, reduced prostate cancer but slightly increased endometrial cancer risk

AG.description: Moderate HNF1B effect on cancer susceptibility

GG.description: Higher prostate cancer risk, but potentially lower endometrial cancer risk

description: HNF1B (TCF2) variant with opposing effects on prostate and endometrial cancer risk. HNF1B is a transcription factor critical for epithelial differentiation in the prostate and uterus.
notes: HNF1B shows pleiotropic opposing effects — the same allele that increases prostate cancer risk decreases endometrial cancer risk. Also associated with type 2 diabetes risk. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Homozygous reference at the 8q24 region 3 locus. No elevated prostate cancer risk conferred by this variant; population-average risk applies.

CA.description: Heterozygous carrier of the A risk allele. Associated with moderately increased prostate cancer risk, particularly informative in men of African ancestry where this variant reaches higher minor allele frequency.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele; both copies carry the risk-associated 8q24 variant. This genotype is associated with the highest relative prostate cancer risk at this locus, with strongest signal in African-ancestry populations.

description: rs16901979 resides in the intergenic 8q24 'gene desert' on chromosome 8, a genomic region harboring at least seven independent prostate cancer risk signals. This variant (region 3) was discovered through GWAS and shows particularly strong association in African-ancestry men, where the A allele frequency is substantially higher than in European populations. Long-range chromatin interactions between 8q24 regulatory elements and the MYC oncogene approximately 430 kb away are hypothesized to mediate the risk effect.

sources.name: Yeager M et al. (2007) - Genome-wide association study of prostate cancer identifies a second risk locus at 8q24

notes: Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This represents a statistical risk factor identified through population studies. This is not a diagnosis. The A allele frequency is substantially higher in African-ancestry populations (approximately 10–14%) compared to European populations (<2%), making this variant particularly relevant for prostate cancer risk assessment in men of African descent. This variant does not replace PSA screening recommendations.

AA.description: Homozygous 8q24 risk variant, increased prostate cancer susceptibility

AG.description: Heterozygous, moderately increased prostate cancer risk

GG.description: Non-risk genotype, population-average prostate cancer risk at this locus

description: Second independent 8q24 prostate cancer risk variant. The 8q24 region contains multiple independent cancer risk loci in a gene desert that regulate distant MYC oncogene expression.
notes: 8q24 contains at least 5 independent prostate cancer risk signals. Combined, they contribute substantially to familial relative risk. Risk allele frequencies vary across ancestral populations. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

AA.description: Homozygous 8q24 risk variant, significantly increased prostate cancer susceptibility

AC.description: Heterozygous, moderately increased prostate cancer risk

CC.description: Non-risk genotype, population-average prostate cancer susceptibility

description: 8q24 variant associated with prostate cancer susceptibility. This gene desert region contains multiple independent prostate cancer risk loci that regulate MYC expression.
notes: One of the first GWAS-identified prostate cancer variants. Risk allele is more common in African-ancestry populations, partially explaining higher prostate cancer incidence in this group. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; standard CYP17A1 expression supports baseline androgen and estrogen biosynthesis in adrenal and gonadal tissues.

GA.description: Heterozygous; one A1 allele modestly increases CYP17A1 promoter activity, with intermediate steroid hormone biosynthesis rates.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate (A1/A1); higher CYP17A1 expression may increase circulating androgen and estrogen levels, potentially influencing hormone-sensitive tissue growth.

description: CYP17A1 encodes cytochrome P450 17A1 (17alpha-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase), a bifunctional enzyme catalyzing key steps in androgen and estrogen biosynthesis in adrenal glands and gonads. The rs10012 (A2/A1) variant in the 5' promoter region affects transcription factor binding and CYP17A1 expression levels. Higher enzyme activity is associated with elevated sex hormone exposure in steroid-sensitive tissues.

sources.name: Yamada et al. (1999) - CYP17 gene polymorphism and prostate cancer risk

notes: CYP17A1 variants are studied in hormone-sensitive cancers; effect modulated by total hormone exposure. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; lower TGFB1 promoter-driven expression results in baseline TGF-β1 levels with standard tumor-suppressive and immunomodulatory signaling.

CT.description: Heterozygous; one Pro10Leu allele associated with intermediate TGF-β1 secretion, balancing tumor-suppressive and pro-metastatic TGF-β signaling.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate (Leu10); higher TGF-β1 secretion may promote epithelial-mesenchymal transition and immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in advanced stages.

description: TGFB1 encodes transforming growth factor beta 1, a pleiotropic cytokine with dual roles in cancer: tumor suppressive in early stages through growth arrest, but pro-tumorigenic in advanced cancer by promoting invasion and immune evasion. The rs1982073 (Leu10Pro, codon 10) variant affects TGFB1 signal peptide efficiency and mature protein secretion levels. Higher TGF-β1 production is associated with altered cancer microenvironment dynamics.

sources.name: Jin et al. (2002) - TGF-beta1 Leu10Pro polymorphism and breast cancer risk

notes: TGF-β1 has dual roles; high levels suppress early tumors but may facilitate invasion later. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard TERT promoter activity with baseline telomerase expression in stem and progenitor cells and lower replicative immortalization potential.

AG.description: Heterozygous; one variant allele moderately alters TERT promoter strength, with intermediate telomerase expression and telomere maintenance capacity.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with higher TERT transcription, potentially elevating telomerase activity and enabling greater cellular proliferative capacity.

description: TERT encodes the reverse transcriptase catalytic subunit of telomerase, the ribonucleoprotein complex that extends telomeres to enable unlimited cellular proliferation. Telomerase is normally repressed in somatic cells but reactivated in ~90% of cancers. The rs2853669 variant in the TERT promoter region (upstream of the common somatic C228T and C250T cancer-driver mutations) modulates baseline TERT transcription through altered transcription factor binding at E-box or Sp1 sites.

sources.name: Landi et al. (2009) - Gene expression signature of cigarette smoking and its role in lung adenocarcinoma development and survival

notes: TERT promoter germline variants are distinct from somatic C228T/C250T driver mutations seen in melanoma and glioma. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AA.description: Higher TERT expression, longer telomeres but increased multi-cancer susceptibility

AG.description: Moderate TERT expression, balanced telomere maintenance

GG.description: Baseline TERT expression and telomere maintenance

description: TERT coding region variant affecting telomerase activity. Illustrates the aging-cancer tradeoff: higher telomerase protects against senescence but enables cancer cell immortality.
notes: TERT 5p15.33 locus contains multiple independent cancer risk signals. Telomerase reactivation occurs in ~90% of human cancers. TERT promoter mutations are the most common non-coding mutations in cancer. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard XPC-RAD23B complex efficiently recognizes bulky DNA adducts to initiate global genome nucleotide excision repair.

CT.description: Heterozygous; one Ala499Val allele may moderately reduce XPC affinity for DNA lesions, with intermediate GG-NER initiation efficiency.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate (Ala499Val); reduced XPC-RAD23B affinity for bulky lesions potentially delays NER initiation, increasing UV and carcinogen-induced mutation risk.

description: XPC encodes the xeroderma pigmentosum complementation group C protein, which partners with RAD23B to recognize helix-distorting DNA lesions in the global genome nucleotide excision repair (GG-NER) pathway. XPC is the primary damage sensor for UV-induced pyrimidine dimers and bulky chemical adducts in non-transcribed DNA. The rs2228000 (Ala499Val) variant affects XPC binding affinity for damaged DNA substrates.

sources.name: Webb et al. (2005) - NER gene polymorphisms and cancer susceptibility

notes: XPC variants are particularly relevant to UV-exposed skin; part of the xeroderma pigmentosum spectrum. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard p21 protein function enables normal G1/S checkpoint arrest and DNA damage response downstream of p53.

CA.description: Heterozygous; one Ser31Arg allele may partially alter p21 protein interactions with CDKs, with intermediate checkpoint efficacy.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; Ser31Arg substitution may impair p21 binding to cyclin-CDK complexes, potentially reducing G1 checkpoint stringency after DNA damage.

description: CDKN1A encodes p21 (CIP1/WAF1), a cyclin-dependent kinase inhibitor that mediates p53-induced cell cycle arrest at the G1/S checkpoint following DNA damage. p21 inhibits cyclin E/CDK2 and cyclin D/CDK4/6 complexes to prevent S phase entry until repair is complete. The rs1801270 (Ser31Arg) variant may affect p21 protein-protein interactions and tumor suppressor function.

sources.name: Dong Y et al. (2015) - Association between p21 Ser31Arg polymorphism and gastrointestinal tract tumor risk: a meta-analysis

notes: p21 loss reduces DNA damage checkpoint stringency; effect modest as a standalone variant. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

GG.description: Higher MDM2 expression, faster p53 degradation, reduced tumor suppression, earlier cancer onset

GT.description: Intermediate MDM2 levels, moderately increased p53 degradation

TT.description: Normal MDM2 expression, standard p53 tumor suppressor activity

description: MDM2 SNP309 T>G increases Sp1 transcription factor binding, elevating MDM2 expression. MDM2 is the primary negative regulator of p53, so higher MDM2 means less tumor suppression.
notes: MDM2 SNP309 G allele accelerates tumor formation by ~10 years in Li-Fraumeni patients. Effect is modified by estrogen — stronger in women due to estrogen-enhanced Sp1 binding. G allele frequency ~35%. Genetic counseling is recommended for carriers.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard TP53 regulatory activity maintains normal apoptotic and cell cycle arrest responses to DNA damage.

CA.description: Heterozygous; one variant allele may moderately affect TP53 transcriptional activity or target gene expression in response to genotoxic stress.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with altered TP53 promoter or regulatory element function, potentially modifying tumor suppressor pathway strength.

description: TP53 encodes p53, the master tumor suppressor transcription factor that responds to DNA damage, oncogene activation, and cellular stress by inducing cell cycle arrest, senescence, or apoptosis. The rs4488809 variant in TP53 regulatory regions influences pathway activity. p53 pathway variants are among the most studied in cancer biology, with altered activity associated with reduced tumor surveillance.

sources.name: Sjalander et al. (2002) - Association of the TP53 codon 72 and intron 3 polymorphisms with cancer risk

notes: TP53 common variants have modest effect; distinguished from somatic TP53 mutations seen in tumor tissue. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation.

AC.description: Intermediate AGTR1 expression, slightly elevated blood pressure tendency

AA.description: Normal angiotensin II type 1 receptor expression, standard blood pressure regulation

CC.description: Increased AGTR1 expression, enhanced angiotensin II signaling, higher hypertension risk

description: AGTR1 +1166A>C variant in the 3' UTR affects angiotensin II type 1 receptor expression. The AT1 receptor mediates vasoconstriction, sodium retention, and cardiac remodeling.
notes: The C allele disrupts a miR-155 binding site, increasing AGTR1 mRNA stability. ARBs (losartan, valsartan) block AT1 receptors regardless of genotype. C allele frequency ~25% in Europeans. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Normal clotting (no Factor V Leiden)

CT.description: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden - slightly increased clot risk

TT.description: Homozygous Factor V Leiden - significantly increased clot risk

description: Factor V Leiden mutation affecting blood clotting cascade and thrombosis risk.

sources.name: Bertina RM et al. (1994) - Mutation in blood coagulation factor V associated with resistance to activated protein C

notes: Factor V Leiden — the most common inherited thrombophilia. Heterozygotes have ~5x increased risk of venous thromboembolism; homozygotes ~50x. Clinically actionable: affects decisions about oral contraceptives, surgery, and prolonged immobilization. Most common in Europeans (~5% carrier rate). This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard CYP17A1-associated vascular tone and blood pressure regulation. Most individuals globally (~91%) carry this genotype.

TC.description: Heterozygous; one C allele associated with a modest increase in systolic blood pressure (~1-2 mmHg). The effect is small but measurable at the population level and contributes to hypertension polygenic risk.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; C allele (~9% globally, ~27-29% East Asian) associated with the highest blood pressure elevation from this locus. Strongest effect in East Asian populations where C allele frequency is substantially higher.

description: CYP17A1 (5'-nucleotidase, cytosolic II) encodes an enzyme involved in nucleotide metabolism and purine recycling. The rs11191548 variant near CYP17A1 on chromosome 10q24 was identified in large GWAS studies as associated with systolic and diastolic blood pressure. The mechanism may involve altered purinergic signaling in vascular smooth muscle cells, since adenosine — a product of CYP17A1 activity — is a potent vasodilator. Higher CYP17A1 activity associated with the C allele could reduce extracellular adenosine, promoting vasoconstriction and elevated blood pressure.

sources.name: Newton-Cheh C et al. (2009) - Genome-wide association study identifies eight loci associated with blood pressure

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The C allele shows strong population stratification: ~9% globally, ~8-9% European, ~4-5% African, ~27-29% East Asian. The per-allele blood pressure effect is ~1-1.5 mmHg systolic — modest individually but significant at population scale and in conjunction with other hypertension risk alleles. Hypertension is highly polygenic and heavily influenced by diet (sodium intake), physical activity, and body weight. Individuals with the C allele and family history of hypertension or cardiovascular disease should discuss blood pressure monitoring and lifestyle optimization with their physician.

trait: Cholesterol Transfer Rate / HDL Regulation (CETP Promoter)

CC.description: Homozygous reference C allele; higher CETP promoter activity drives increased CETP protein synthesis. Cholesteryl esters are transferred more actively from HDL to VLDL/LDL, resulting in lower plasma HDL-C concentrations.

CA.description: Heterozygous CA; one copy of the A allele partially reduces CETP promoter activity. Intermediate HDL cholesterol levels between CC and AA genotypes, reflecting partial reduction in cholesterol transfer rate.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele; substantially reduced CETP promoter-driven transcription lowers CETP protein levels. Less active transfer of cholesteryl esters from HDL raises plasma HDL-C. Associated with altered cardiovascular risk profile and modestly reduced total:HDL cholesterol ratio.

description: The rs1800775 C>A variant is located approximately 2 kb upstream of the CETP coding sequence in the promoter/regulatory region, where it modulates transcriptional activity of the CETP gene. The A allele reduces CETP promoter activity, lowering CETP protein expression and consequently decreasing the rate at which cholesteryl esters are transferred from HDL to apoB-containing lipoproteins. This regulatory variant operates in partial linkage disequilibrium with the rs5882 coding variant, and their combined haplotype effects on CETP activity and HDL cholesterol are among the most replicated quantitative trait loci in cardiovascular genetics.

sources.name: Ordovas JM et al. (2002) - Functional variants in the promoter region of CETP and susceptibility to myocardial infarction

notes: The A allele frequency is approximately 40–50% in European populations. This promoter variant is frequently studied alongside rs5882 (Val422Ile) as part of CETP haplotype analysis; haplotype-based models better predict HDL variation than either SNP alone. The CETP locus has been extensively explored in the context of longevity genetics and Alzheimer's disease risk due to the role of cholesterol transport in neuronal function. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous risk genotype, ~30-60% increased coronary artery disease risk

CG.description: Heterozygous, ~15-30% increased coronary artery disease risk

GG.description: Non-risk genotype at 9p21 locus, population-average coronary artery disease risk

description: 9p21.3 variant near CDKN2A/2B is one of the most replicated coronary artery disease risk loci, affecting vascular cell proliferation and atherosclerotic plaque stability.
notes: This 9p21 locus was discovered simultaneously by multiple GWAS groups in 2007. Risk allele frequency ~50%. Effect is independent of traditional cardiovascular risk factors. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Non-risk genotype at 9p21 locus, baseline coronary heart disease risk

AG.description: One risk allele, ~25% increased coronary heart disease risk

GG.description: Homozygous 9p21 risk, ~50% increased coronary heart disease risk independent of lipids

description: 9p21.3 variant near CDKN2A/2B is one of the most replicated coronary heart disease risk loci, acting through ANRIL long non-coding RNA regulation of vascular cell proliferation.
notes: Third independent 9p21 variant in our panel (with rs10757274, rs1333049). Risk is independent of LDL, blood pressure, and diabetes — operates through vascular inflammation and cell cycle control. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

trait: HDL Antioxidant Capacity / Coronary Artery Disease Risk (PON1 L55M)

AA.description: Homozygous Leu55 reference; standard PON1 protein expression and antioxidant enzyme activity on HDL particles. Normal capacity to hydrolyze lipid peroxides on LDL and prevent oxidative modification.

AT.description: Heterozygous Leu55Met; intermediate PON1 enzyme expression. Moderately reduced HDL-associated antioxidant defense with intermediate coronary artery disease risk relative to AA and TT genotypes.

TT.phenotype: Reduced PON1 Activity / Elevated CAD Risk (Met55/Met55)
TT.description: Homozygous Met55 variant; substantially reduced PON1 protein levels on HDL particles (~3× lower than Leu55). Impaired hydrolysis of oxidized phospholipids on LDL increases cardiovascular oxidative stress and is associated with elevated coronary artery disease risk in multiple cohort studies.

description: PON1 (paraoxonase 1) is a calcium-dependent lactonase and esterase synthesized in the liver and circulated bound to high-density lipoprotein (HDL), where it degrades lipid peroxides on both LDL and HDL particles. This antioxidant activity is a key HDL cardioprotective mechanism. The rs854560 A>T variant encodes the Leu55Met substitution, which substantially reduces PON1 protein concentration (~3-fold) and associated lactonase activity, impairing the antioxidant defense that prevents LDL oxidative modification and foam cell formation in arterial walls.

sources.name: Mackness MI et al. (1997) - Paraoxonase and coronary heart disease

notes: The T allele (Met55) frequency is approximately 28–34% in Europeans and varies substantially across populations. PON1 Leu55Met (rs854560) and Gln192Arg (rs662) are the two most studied PON1 polymorphisms; their combined haplotype more powerfully predicts CAD risk than either alone. Statin therapy may partially compensate for reduced PON1 activity. Physical activity and Mediterranean dietary patterns have been associated with increased PON1 activity independent of genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

GG.description: Homozygous Val422 reference; normal CETP protein folding and cholesteryl ester transfer activity. Average HDL cholesterol levels without the Ile422-associated reduction in CETP activity.

AG.description: Heterozygous Val422Ile; one Ile422 allele partially reduces CETP transfer activity, moderately elevating HDL-C levels. Intermediate between GG and AA in terms of CETP activity and HDL response.

AA.description: Homozygous Ile422 variant; CETP activity is substantially reduced, impairing reverse cholesterol transport. HDL-C levels are significantly elevated (~5–8% higher). The cardiovascular implications are complex — while higher HDL-C is generally favorable, CETP inhibition-related HDL may be functionally different from the HDL elevation achieved through lifestyle modification.

description: CETP (cholesteryl ester transfer protein) facilitates the transfer of cholesteryl esters from HDL to VLDL and LDL in exchange for triglycerides, driving reverse cholesterol transport. The rs5882 G>A variant (Val422Ile) reduces CETP protein activity and is associated with significantly elevated plasma HDL cholesterol concentrations. This variant has been extensively studied in the context of cardiovascular disease — while elevated HDL is generally protective, the paradox of CETP inhibitor trials (where pharmacologic CETP inhibition raised HDL without reducing cardiovascular events) has complicated interpretation of genetically elevated HDL via CETP variants.

sources.name: Ordovas JM et al. (2002) - Functional variants in the promoter region of CETP and susceptibility to myocardial infarction

notes: A allele (Ile422) frequency is approximately 38–44% in European populations. HDL elevation attributable to CETP variants should not be treated as equivalent to lifestyle-driven HDL elevation in terms of cardiovascular protection; Mendelian randomization studies have shown attenuated benefit from genetically elevated CETP-variant HDL. Also associated with cognitive aging — CETP variants influence brain cholesterol metabolism and were studied in centenarian longevity genetics. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Non-risk genotype at 9p21 locus, baseline myocardial infarction risk

AG.description: One risk allele, ~25% increased heart attack risk independent of traditional risk factors

GG.description: Two risk alleles, ~50% increased heart attack risk independent of cholesterol and blood pressure

description: 9p21.3 locus variant near CDKN2A/2B is the strongest common genetic risk factor for myocardial infarction, acting independently of traditional cardiovascular risk factors.
notes: 9p21 risk is independent of LDL cholesterol, blood pressure, diabetes, and smoking. The mechanism involves regulation of CDKN2A/2B (cell cycle control) and ANRIL long non-coding RNA. Risk allele frequency ~50%. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Higher basal beta-1 receptor activity, stronger heart rate response, better beta-blocker response

CG.description: Moderate beta-1 adrenergic signaling, intermediate heart rate and beta-blocker response

GG.description: Reduced beta-1 receptor coupling, lower heart rate response, reduced beta-blocker efficacy

description: ADRB1 Arg389Gly variant in the G-protein coupling domain affects beta-1 adrenergic receptor signaling. Determines heart rate response to catecholamines and beta-blocker drug efficacy.
notes: Arg389 receptor has ~3x higher cAMP stimulation than Gly389. Arg389 homozygotes show better blood pressure response to beta-blockers (metoprolol, atenolol). Important for personalized hypertension treatment. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Standard Kir6.2 KATP channel function, normal glucose-stimulated insulin secretion

CT.description: Intermediate KATP channel sensitivity, slightly reduced insulin response to glucose

TT.description: Altered KATP channel gating, impaired glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, increased T2D risk

description: KCNJ11 E23K variant affects the Kir6.2 subunit of the pancreatic beta-cell KATP channel. This channel couples glucose metabolism to insulin secretion by depolarizing the beta cell.
notes: K23 (T allele) increases KATP channel open probability, reducing insulin secretion. Sulfonylurea drugs (glyburide) close KATP channels to stimulate insulin release. Neonatal diabetes from KCNJ11 mutations responds to sulfonylureas. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Standard apolipoprotein B function, population-average LDL levels

CT.description: Intermediate APOB effect, mildly higher LDL cholesterol levels

TT.description: Altered APOB expression, higher LDL cholesterol levels and cardiovascular risk

description: APOB variant affecting apolipoprotein B-100, the structural protein of LDL particles. Each LDL particle contains exactly one APOB molecule, making APOB a direct measure of atherogenic particle number.
notes: APOB is the ligand for LDL receptor-mediated clearance. APOB mutations cause familial hypobetalipoproteinemia (low) or familial defective APOB (high LDL). APOB is now considered a better CVD predictor than LDL-C. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical LPL promoter transcription supports normal lipoprotein lipase expression and efficient plasma triglyceride clearance.

TG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate LPL promoter activity produces modestly reduced lipoprotein lipase expression and mildly elevated post-prandial triglycerides.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; lower LPL promoter-driven transcription significantly reduces lipoprotein lipase output, impairing VLDL and chylomicron clearance and raising fasting triglyceride levels.

description: LPL encodes lipoprotein lipase, the rate-limiting enzyme for hydrolysis of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins (VLDL and chylomicrons) on capillary endothelium. The -93T>G promoter variant reduces LPL transcriptional activity, lowering enzyme levels in adipose and skeletal muscle. Reduced LPL activity elevates plasma triglycerides and reduces HDL cholesterol, increasing cardiovascular risk.

sources.name: Reymer PW et al. (1997) - A lipoprotein lipase mutation associated with familial combined hyperlipidaemia

notes: LPL activity is strongly modulated by dietary fat intake, exercise, and insulin sensitivity. Multiple LPL promoter and coding variants interact to determine triglyceride phenotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Normal weight regulation, lower BMI tendency

AT.description: ~1.5kg higher weight on average, moderately increased obesity risk

AA.description: ~3kg higher weight on average, increased appetite, 1.7x obesity risk

description: FTO gene variant, strongest common genetic predictor of obesity. Each A allele increases weight ~1-1.5kg. Affects appetite regulation and energy expenditure.

sources.name: Frayling TM et al. (2007) - A common variant in the FTO gene is associated with body mass index and predisposes to childhood and adult obesity

notes: Strongest common genetic variant for obesity risk, with ~0.39 kg/m2 BMI increase per risk allele. Effect is consistent across populations but can be attenuated by physical activity. FTO likely acts through regulation of nearby IRX3/IRX5 genes affecting adipocyte thermogenesis. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: FTO risk variant, ~1.5kg/allele higher body weight, increased type 2 diabetes susceptibility

AC.description: Intermediate FTO effect, modest increase in BMI and diabetes risk

CC.description: Non-risk FTO genotype, population-average BMI and diabetes risk

description: FTO (fat mass and obesity-associated) variant affects IRX3/IRX5 regulation through long-range enhancer activity, influencing adipocyte thermogenesis and energy expenditure.
notes: FTO was the first obesity GWAS locus (2007). Despite the name, the causal mechanism acts through IRX3/IRX5 via chromatin looping, switching adipocyte program from energy-burning to energy-storing. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Higher PON1 paraoxonase activity, better LDL oxidation protection, reduced CVD risk

CT.description: Moderate paraoxonase activity, intermediate cardiovascular protection

TT.description: Lower PON1 activity, reduced protection against LDL oxidation, slightly higher CVD risk

description: PON1 Q192R variant affects paraoxonase 1 enzyme activity. PON1 is an HDL-associated enzyme that prevents LDL oxidation, a key step in atherosclerosis development.
notes: PON1 is secreted by the liver and associates with HDL particles. The R192 variant has higher paraoxonase activity but lower lactonase activity — the clinical net effect depends on the substrate. Also detoxifies organophosphate pesticides. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: PLA2/PLA2 genotype, enhanced platelet aggregation, may have reduced aspirin response

CT.description: PLA1/PLA2 heterozygous, moderate platelet aggregation

TT.description: PLA1/PLA1 genotype, standard platelet aggregation and aspirin response

description: ITGB3 Leu33Pro (HPA-1/PLA) variant affects platelet glycoprotein IIIa, the fibrinogen receptor. PLA2 (Pro33) enhances platelet aggregation and may reduce aspirin efficacy.
notes: PLA2 allele frequency ~15% in Europeans. Associated with aspirin resistance, coronary stent thrombosis, and neonatal alloimmune thrombocytopenia (NAIT). Important for antiplatelet therapy decisions. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Homozygous prothrombin mutation, significantly elevated blood clot risk

AG.description: Heterozygous prothrombin G20210A, ~3x increased venous thromboembolism risk

GG.description: Wild-type prothrombin, population-average thrombosis risk

description: Factor II (prothrombin) G20210A is the second most common inherited thrombophilia after Factor V Leiden. Increases prothrombin levels, elevating blood clot risk.
notes: G20210A is a gain-of-function variant in the 3' UTR that increases prothrombin mRNA stability and protein levels. Carrier frequency ~2% in Europeans. Important for contraceptive and surgical risk assessment. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Normal lipoprotein lipase function with typical triglyceride metabolism.

CG.description: One copy of S447X gain-of-function variant; moderately lower triglycerides and higher HDL cholesterol.

GG.description: Two copies of S447X gain-of-function variant; substantially lower triglycerides and higher HDL cholesterol; reduced cardiovascular risk.

description: The rs328 variant (Ser447X) in the LPL gene creates a premature stop codon that paradoxically enhances lipoprotein lipase activity by removing a C-terminal region that inhibits secretion. Carriers have lower triglycerides, higher HDL cholesterol, and reduced cardiovascular disease risk.

sources.name: Ahn YI et al. (1997) - Effects of S447X and D9N LPL gene mutations on the severity of hypertriglyceridemia

notes: The S447X variant (using standard S447X nomenclature) is one of the best-characterized gain-of-function variants in lipid genetics. The G allele at rs328 introduces the Ser447Stop premature termination codon that paradoxically increases LPL secretion and enzymatic activity by removing a C-terminal inhibitory domain. This is distinct from rare loss-of-function LPL mutations causing familial hyperchylomicronemia. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Lower diabetes risk

CT.description: Moderately increased risk

TT.description: Higher Type 2 diabetes risk

description: Strongest common genetic risk factor for Type 2 diabetes affecting insulin secretion.

sources.name: Grant SFA et al. (2006) - Variant of transcription factor 7-like 2 (TCF7L2) gene confers risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: Strongest common genetic risk factor for type 2 diabetes, with ~1.4x increased risk per T allele. Consistent across all studied populations. However, T2D risk is overwhelmingly driven by lifestyle factors (diet, exercise, weight). Genetic risk can be substantially mitigated by healthy habits. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

trait: Type 2 Diabetes Risk / Beta-Cell Zinc Transport (SLC30A8)

CC.description: Homozygous reference (Arg325); standard ZnT8 zinc transporter function in pancreatic beta cells. Insulin crystallization and secretion proceed at normal efficiency. Associated with lower T2D risk relative to CT and TT genotypes.

CT.description: Heterozygous for the Trp325 variant; one copy of reduced-function ZnT8. Intermediate beta-cell zinc transport efficiency and modestly elevated T2D risk compared to CC homozygotes.

TT.description: Homozygous Trp325 variant; ZnT8 zinc transport activity into secretory granules is reduced. Insulin crystallization is impaired, and first-phase insulin secretion is diminished. TT individuals have approximately 1.2–1.4× increased T2D risk in large GWAS meta-analyses.

description: SLC30A8 encodes ZnT8, a zinc transporter concentrated on the secretory granule membrane of pancreatic beta cells, where it loads zinc into insulin-containing granules to facilitate insulin hexamer formation and crystallization for storage and secretion. The rs13266634 C>T variant (Arg325Trp) reduces zinc transport efficiency, impairing insulin crystal formation and first-phase insulin secretory response. This variant was among the first replicated GWAS loci for type 2 diabetes, though the direction of T2D risk has been nuanced by subsequent studies identifying rare loss-of-function SLC30A8 variants that paradoxically protect against T2D.

sources.name: Saxena R et al. (2007) - Genome-wide association analysis identifies loci for type 2 diabetes and triglyceride levels

notes: The T allele (Trp325) frequency is approximately 26–30% in European populations and 60–75% in East Asian populations. Notably, rare loss-of-function SLC30A8 variants identified in subsequent studies are protective against T2D, suggesting a complex relationship between ZnT8 activity and beta-cell function that is not fully captured by the common Arg325Trp variant. ZnT8 autoantibodies are also a diagnostic marker in Type 1 diabetes. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical testicular acid phosphatase activity during amelogenesis and normal enamel crystallization.

CT.description: Heterozygous carriers may show subtle enamel mineralization differences, with slightly elevated susceptibility to enamel defects.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to reduced ACPT function during tooth development, potentially resulting in softer or hypomineralized enamel.

description: ACPT encodes testicular acid phosphatase, an enzyme expressed in ameloblasts during enamel maturation that modulates phosphate availability for hydroxyapatite crystal growth. Variants in ACPT have been associated with enamel thickness and caries susceptibility in dental genetic studies.

sources.name: Pillas et al. (2010) - Dental enamel GWAS

notes: Fluoride exposure and oral hygiene remain the dominant modifiers of enamel health. Genetic risk is secondary to behavioral factors. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Wild-type ARMS2, population-average age-related macular degeneration risk

GT.description: Heterozygous ARMS2 risk variant, ~2.5x increased AMD risk

TT.description: Homozygous ARMS2 risk variant, ~8x increased risk of age-related macular degeneration

description: ARMS2 A69S variant is the second strongest genetic risk factor for age-related macular degeneration, the leading cause of blindness in the elderly.
notes: ARMS2/HTRA1 locus is the second major AMD risk locus after CFH. Risk allele frequency ~20% in Europeans, ~40% in East Asians. Homozygous carriers have ~8x AMD risk.

CC.description: Wild-type amelogenin, standard tooth enamel formation

CT.description: Heterozygous amelogenin variant (females), mild enamel hypoplasia risk

TT.description: Homozygous or hemizygous amelogenin variant, increased risk of enamel defects

description: AMELX encodes amelogenin, the most abundant protein in developing enamel (~90%). X-linked variants cause amelogenesis imperfecta with thin or absent enamel.
notes: AMELX is X-linked, so males (hemizygous) are more severely affected. Amelogenin guides hydroxyapatite crystal formation during enamel development. Also used in forensic sex determination (AMELX vs AMELY).

AA.description: Reduced complement factor H function, increased complement activation and AMD susceptibility

AG.description: Intermediate CFH function, moderately increased age-related macular degeneration risk

GG.description: Normal CFH function, better complement regulation in the retina

description: CFH I62V variant modifies complement factor H function, affecting complement regulation in the retina. Secondary AMD-associated CFH variant that modifies risk in combination with Y402H.
notes: CFH I62V is in linkage disequilibrium with Y402H (rs1061170) but provides independent risk information. The combined CFH haplotype structure is important for accurate AMD risk prediction.

trait: Carbonic Anhydrase VI / Dental Caries Risk

CC.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical CA6 enzyme activity in saliva, supporting normal bicarbonate buffering and enamel protection.

CT.description: Heterozygous carriers may have modestly reduced carbonic anhydrase VI levels, slightly impairing salivary pH regulation.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to significantly reduced CA6 activity in saliva, impairing acid neutralization and increasing enamel dissolution risk.

description: CA6 encodes carbonic anhydrase VI, the most abundant enzyme in saliva that catalyzes CO2 hydration to maintain salivary bicarbonate buffering capacity. Reduced CA6 activity impairs salivary pH recovery after carbohydrate intake, prolonging enamel acid exposure and increasing caries susceptibility.

sources.name: Kero et al. (2010) - CA6 and dental caries susceptibility

notes: Low CA6 carriers benefit most from fluoride use and sugar reduction. Salivary flow rate also modulates caries risk independently. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; standard regulatory activity at this locus. Population-average intraocular pressure trajectory; no elevated glaucoma risk from this variant.

GA.description: Heterozygous carrier of the A allele. Associated with moderate alteration in aqueous humor dynamics or trabecular meshwork function relevant to intraocular pressure regulation.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele; both copies carry the variant associated with the most pronounced alteration in intraocular pressure-related pathways at this locus.

description: rs2276035 is a variant located on chromosome 11 in the ARHGEF12 gene region, a Rho guanine nucleotide exchange factor involved in cytoskeletal regulation. ARHGEF12 activates RhoA signaling, which modulates actomyosin contractility in trabecular meshwork cells — the primary site of aqueous humor outflow resistance governing intraocular pressure. Altered Rho/ROCK signaling in the trabecular meshwork affects cytoskeletal tone and outflow facility, providing a plausible mechanistic link between variants in this region and intraocular pressure regulation relevant to primary open-angle glaucoma susceptibility.

sources.name: Thorleifsson G et al. (2010) - Common sequence variants in the LOXL1 gene confer susceptibility to exfoliation glaucoma

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. Note: dbSNP maps rs2276035 to chromosome 11 (ARHGEF12) rather than chromosome 7 (CAV1/CAV2). The ARHGEF12/RhoA pathway is biologically relevant to trabecular meshwork function and intraocular pressure. Clinical evaluation for glaucoma should include tonometry, optic nerve assessment, and visual field testing by an ophthalmologist.

AA.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with baseline PADI4 enzyme activity and lower levels of citrullinated proteins in periodontal tissues.

AG.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate PADI4 activity; modestly elevated citrullination may increase periodontal susceptibility.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to higher PADI4 mRNA stability, increased protein citrullination in gingival tissue, and greater periodontal inflammation.

description: PADI4 encodes peptidylarginine deiminase 4, which citrullinates proteins by converting arginine to citrulline, generating autoantigens recognized in rheumatoid arthritis and periodontitis. The rs560426 haplotype increases PADI4 mRNA stability, elevating citrullination in inflamed periodontal tissues.

sources.name: Suzuki et al. (2005) - PADI4 and periodontal disease

notes: PADI4 variants are also the strongest genetic risk factor for rheumatoid arthritis. Periodontal disease and RA share inflammatory pathways. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous protective C2 variant, significantly reduced complement activation and AMD risk

CG.description: C2 protective allele carrier, reduced classical complement pathway and lower AMD risk

GG.description: Standard C2 function, population-average AMD risk from this variant

description: Complement C2 protective variant reduces classical complement pathway activation in the retina. Identified alongside CFB as a protective factor against AMD.
notes: C2 and CFB are adjacent in the MHC class III region on chromosome 6. Together they demonstrate that reduced complement activation protects against AMD, validating complement-targeted AMD therapies.

CG.description: Heterozygous C3 variant, moderately increased complement activation and AMD risk

CC.description: Normal complement C3, population-average AMD risk from this variant

GG.description: Altered complement C3 function, increased complement activation and higher AMD susceptibility

description: Complement C3 R102G variant affects the central component of all three complement activation pathways. Altered C3 contributes to retinal inflammation and drusen formation in AMD.
notes: C3 joins CFH, CFI, and CFB as complement genes in AMD. R102G affects Factor H binding, altering complement regulation. The G allele (~20% frequency in Europeans) increases AMD risk ~1.5x per allele.

AA.description: Homozygous protective CFB variant, significantly reduced complement activation and AMD risk

AG.description: CFB protective allele carrier, reduced complement activation and lower AMD risk

GG.description: Standard CFB function, population-average AMD risk from this variant

description: CFB R32Q protective variant reduces complement factor B activity in the alternative complement pathway. One of the first protective AMD variants identified in the complement system.
notes: CFB R32Q reduces factor B activity by ~50%, dampening alternative pathway complement activation. Protective effect (OR ~0.4) is among the strongest genetic effects in AMD. Rare protective allele (~5%).

CC.description: Homozygous CFH Y402H risk variant, ~6x increased AMD risk

CT.description: Heterozygous CFH variant, ~2.5x increased AMD risk

TT.description: Wild-type CFH, population-average macular degeneration risk

description: CFH Y402H is the strongest genetic risk factor for age-related macular degeneration. Complement factor H regulates the alternative complement pathway in the retina.
notes: CFH Y402H was one of the first GWAS discoveries. Risk allele frequency ~35% in Europeans. The variant impairs complement regulation in the retina, leading to chronic inflammation and drusen formation.

CC.description: Standard complement factor I regulation, baseline macular degeneration risk

CT.description: Intermediate complement regulation, slightly elevated AMD risk

TT.description: Altered complement factor I activity, increased age-related macular degeneration susceptibility

description: CFI variant affects complement factor I, a serine protease that degrades C3b and C4b. Part of the complement pathway implicated in AMD pathogenesis.
notes: CFI joins CFH, C3, and CFB as complement pathway genes implicated in AMD. The complement system's role in drusen formation and retinal inflammation is well established.

GG.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical DSPP cleavage into DSP and DPP, supporting normal dentin mineralization and tooth strength.

GT.description: Heterozygous carriers may show subtle changes in dentin sialoprotein function, with slight reduction in dentin mineral density.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to impaired DSPP processing, resulting in less mineralized dentin matrix and greater susceptibility to dentinogenesis imperfecta.

description: DSPP encodes dentin sialophosphoprotein, a precursor cleaved into dentin sialoprotein (DSP) and dentin phosphoprotein (DPP), both essential for dentin matrix mineralization. Mutations cause dentinogenesis imperfecta; variants in DSPP influence dentin hardness and susceptibility to wear and fracture.

sources.name: Xiao et al. (2001) - DSPP and dentinogenesis imperfecta

notes: Dentinogenesis imperfecta is caused by high-impact DSPP mutations; this variant has a milder effect. Composite restorations protect weak dentin in carriers. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

AG.description: Heterozygous DSPP variant, possible subtle dentin quality change

AA.description: Wild-type DSPP, standard dentin mineralization and structure

GG.description: Altered DSPP expression, increased risk of dentinogenesis imperfecta

description: DSPP encodes dentin sialophosphoprotein, processed into DSP and DPP, the most abundant non-collagenous proteins in dentin. Critical for dentin mineralization and tooth structure.
notes: DSPP mutations cause dentinogenesis imperfecta types II and III, characterized by opalescent, weak teeth prone to fracture. DPP initiates dentin mineralization by nucleating hydroxyapatite crystals.

CC.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical DLX3 transcription factor function in ameloblasts and normal enamel matrix protein expression.

CT.description: Heterozygous carriers may have slightly altered DLX3 activity, with a modest increase in enamel hypoplasia or hypomineralization risk.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to reduced DLX3 regulatory function, increasing susceptibility to amelogenesis imperfecta-related enamel defects.

description: DLX3 is a homeodomain transcription factor critical for tooth development, particularly the regulation of amelogenin and enamelin gene expression during enamel matrix formation. Mutations cause tricho-dento-osseous syndrome; common variants influence enamel mineralization quality.

sources.name: Kim et al. (2012) - DLX3 and amelogenesis

notes: Severe DLX3 mutations cause tricho-dento-osseous syndrome; this variant has a milder population-level effect. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

AG.description: Heterozygous ENAM variant, slightly reduced enamel thickness or quality

AA.description: Wild-type enamelin, standard tooth enamel development

GG.description: Homozygous ENAM variant, higher risk of enamel hypoplasia and amelogenesis imperfecta

description: Enamelin (ENAM) is the largest protein in developing enamel. Variants cause autosomal dominant amelogenesis imperfecta, affecting enamel thickness and mineralization.
notes: ENAM mutations are the most common cause of autosomal dominant amelogenesis imperfecta. Enamelin is critical for enamel crystal elongation during the secretory stage of amelogenesis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical enamelin production and normal enamel matrix scaffold formation during amelogenesis.

GA.description: Heterozygous carriers may have slightly reduced enamelin levels, resulting in modestly thinner enamel and increased caries susceptibility.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to reduced ENAM function, causing enamel matrix defects, hypoplasia, and significantly elevated dental erosion susceptibility.

description: ENAM encodes enamelin, the largest enamel matrix protein critical for prismatic enamel formation. Coding variants in ENAM cause hereditary hypoplastic amelogenesis imperfecta; common variants are associated with enamel thickness variation and caries susceptibility in population studies.

sources.name: Kim et al. (2012) - ENAM variants and enamel defects

notes: Enamel cannot regenerate; protective dental care is critical for carriers. Fluoride varnish and sealants are beneficial preventive strategies. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Increased LOXL1 effect, elevated pseudoexfoliation syndrome susceptibility

CT.description: Intermediate LOXL1 modifier effect on exfoliation syndrome

TT.description: Protective LOXL1 haplotype, reduced exfoliation syndrome risk

description: LOXL1 intronic variant modifies pseudoexfoliation syndrome risk. Third independent signal at the LOXL1 locus alongside rs1048661 and rs3825942.
notes: This intronic variant may affect LOXL1 splicing or expression levels. Pseudoexfoliation is characterized by abnormal fibrillar material accumulation in the anterior chamber, clogging the trabecular meshwork.

AA.description: Standard GJD2/connexin-36 function, baseline myopia susceptibility

AG.description: Intermediate GJD2 effect on retinal signaling, slightly elevated myopia risk

GG.description: Altered retinal gap junction signaling, increased myopia susceptibility

description: GJD2 (connexin-36) variant affects gap junction coupling between retinal neurons. Altered retinal signaling may affect emmetropization (eye growth calibration) and myopia development.
notes: GJD2 was one of the first GWAS hits for myopia/refractive error. Connexin-36 gap junctions couple AII amacrine cells in the retina, critical for rod pathway signaling. Myopia affects ~1.5 billion people.

AA.description: Elevated HTRA1 expression, increased extracellular matrix degradation and AMD susceptibility

AG.description: Intermediate HTRA1 expression, moderately elevated AMD risk

GG.description: Normal HTRA1 promoter activity, population-average AMD risk

description: HTRA1 promoter variant increases expression of the HtrA serine peptidase, which degrades extracellular matrix components. Part of the 10q26 AMD risk locus with ARMS2.
notes: HTRA1 and ARMS2 are in strong LD at 10q26. Debate continues whether ARMS2 or HTRA1 is the causal gene. HTRA1 degrades fibronectin, elastin and proteoglycans in Bruch's membrane.

TT.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical HGF signaling in scleral and corneal tissue, supporting normal ocular growth.

TC.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate HGF pathway activity and moderately elevated risk for axial elongation.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to altered HGF-mediated scleral remodeling signals and higher susceptibility to progressive myopia.

description: HGF encodes hepatocyte growth factor, which activates the MET receptor tyrosine kinase to regulate cell proliferation and tissue remodeling. In the eye, HGF-MET signaling influences scleral extracellular matrix synthesis and ocular axial length development; variants are associated with myopia in multiple GWAS.

sources.name: Solouki et al. (2010) - HGF and refractive error GWAS

notes: HGF variants interact with near-work exposure in myopia development. Time outdoors in childhood is protective regardless of genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with lower IL-1 alpha production in gingival tissues and reduced periodontal disease susceptibility.

CT.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate IL-1A expression; periodontal risk is modestly elevated compared to CC individuals.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to higher IL-1 alpha release from gingival fibroblasts and macrophages, driving greater alveolar bone resorption.

description: IL1A encodes interleukin-1 alpha, a pro-inflammatory cytokine that activates gingival fibroblasts and osteoclasts during periodontal infection. Variants in the IL1A/IL1B gene cluster on chromosome 2 alter cytokine production and are consistently associated with aggressive periodontitis severity.

sources.name: Kornman et al. (2003) - IL1 composite genotype and periodontitis

notes: The IL-1 composite genotype (IL1A + IL1B) multiplies periodontal risk. Smoking is a major environmental amplifier of genetic susceptibility. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with lower IL-1 beta secretion in gingival crevicular fluid and standard periodontal inflammation.

CT.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate IL-1 beta production and modestly elevated risk for chronic periodontitis progression.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to significantly higher IL-1 beta in periodontal tissues, driving greater bone resorption and tooth loss risk.

description: IL1B encodes interleukin-1 beta, the most potent inducer of periodontal tissue destruction via osteoclast activation and matrix metalloproteinase induction. The rs1143634 (exon 5) variant has been associated with elevated IL-1B protein and severe chronic periodontitis in multiple studies.

sources.name: Kornman et al. (2003) - IL-1B and periodontitis

notes: IL-1B variant is part of the Kornman IL-1 composite genotype for periodontal risk. Chlorhexidine and regular scaling reduce progression. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Both risk alleles for exfoliation syndrome and secondary glaucoma

TT.description: Protective genotype, reduced exfoliation syndrome risk

description: LOXL1 R141L variant is strongly associated with pseudoexfoliation syndrome, the most common identifiable cause of open-angle glaucoma worldwide.
notes: LOXL1 encodes a lysyl oxidase-like protein essential for elastic fiber homeostasis. The risk allele frequency varies dramatically across populations, and paradoxically the risk allele flips between populations.

CC.description: Normal myocilin at this position, population-average glaucoma risk

CT.description: Intermediate effect on myocilin, mildly increased intraocular pressure tendency

TT.description: Altered myocilin expression, increased risk of elevated intraocular pressure and glaucoma

description: MYOC (myocilin) variant affects the trabecular meshwork protein involved in aqueous humor outflow. MYOC mutations are the most common genetic cause of primary open-angle glaucoma.
notes: Myocilin mutations cause 3-5% of primary open-angle glaucoma cases. The protein is secreted into aqueous humor and may regulate intraocular pressure through trabecular meshwork remodeling.

AC.description: Myocilin Q368X carrier, significantly increased risk of juvenile or adult-onset open-angle glaucoma

AA.description: Homozygous myocilin Q368X, very high risk of early-onset glaucoma

CC.description: Wild-type myocilin, population-average glaucoma risk at this position

description: MYOC Q368X is a pathogenic nonsense mutation causing misfolded myocilin protein accumulation in trabecular meshwork cells, leading to increased intraocular pressure and glaucoma.
notes: Q368X is the most common MYOC mutation (~1.6% of POAG patients). Causes autosomal dominant glaucoma with high penetrance. Misfolded myocilin is retained in ER, triggering cell death in trabecular meshwork.

GG.description: Standard night vision adaptation

GA.description: Slightly enhanced dark adaptation

AA.description: Better than average night vision

description: Cyclic nucleotide-gated channel variant affecting rod photoreceptor sensitivity.
notes: Limited evidence for common variant effects on scotopic vision. Variant near CNGA1 studied for scotopic (low-light) vision quality. Night vision depends on rod photoreceptor function, pupil dilation, and dark adaptation — highly multifactorial.

CC.description: Standard SIX6 function, population-average optic nerve development and glaucoma risk

CT.description: Intermediate effect on retinal ganglion cell development, slightly elevated glaucoma susceptibility

TT.description: Altered SIX6 activity, increased optic disc cupping and primary open-angle glaucoma risk

description: SIX1/SIX6 locus variant affects retinal ganglion cell development and optic nerve head morphology. SIX6 is a homeobox transcription factor essential for eye development.
notes: SIX6 variants affect optic disc area and vertical cup-disc ratio, key endophenotypes for glaucoma. The risk allele is associated with reduced retinal nerve fiber layer thickness.

AA.description: Modified PAX6 regulation, potentially altered corneal or lens development

AG.description: Moderate PAX6 regulatory effect on eye structure development

GG.description: Standard PAX6 expression, normal ocular development

description: PAX6 is the master control gene for eye development across all species with eyes. Variants affect corneal thickness, lens development, and ocular structure.
notes: PAX6 haploinsufficiency causes aniridia (absent iris). Common variants have subtle effects on corneal curvature, central corneal thickness, and possibly myopia. PAX6 is highly conserved across evolution.

GG.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical axial length development and lower risk of pathological myopia exceeding -6 diopters.

GA.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate susceptibility to pathological myopia with potential for greater axial elongation.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to greater axial length and significantly elevated risk for pathological myopia and associated retinal complications.

description: The MYP3 locus on chromosome 12q21-23 is one of several mapped high-myopia susceptibility regions. Variants at this locus are associated with pathological myopia, a condition involving excessive axial elongation and risk of retinal detachment, choroidal neovascularization, and permanent vision loss.

sources.name: Solouki et al. (2010) - High myopia susceptibility loci

notes: High myopia (>-6D) requires regular retinal surveillance. Orthokeratology and atropine reduce progression in children. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference at the CAV1/CAV2 locus on 7q31. No elevated primary open-angle glaucoma risk from this variant; standard intraocular pressure regulation.

GA.description: Heterozygous carrier of the A risk allele. Associated with moderately increased susceptibility to primary open-angle glaucoma (OR ~1.36 per allele) in GWAS discovery and replication cohorts.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele; both copies carry the risk-associated CAV1 locus variant, conferring the highest relative risk for primary open-angle glaucoma at this locus.

description: rs4236601 lies at chromosome 7q31 within the CAV1/CAV2 gene region. Caveolin-1 (CAV1) is the major structural protein of caveolae — small plasma membrane invaginations — in trabecular meshwork cells. Caveolae facilitate mechanosensing and endocytic trafficking in the trabecular meshwork, the primary regulator of aqueous humor outflow resistance and intraocular pressure. The A risk allele was identified in a large GWAS as significantly associated with primary open-angle glaucoma (OR = 1.36, P = 5.0×10⁻¹⁰), implicating disrupted caveolin-mediated mechanosensing in trabecular meshwork dysfunction.

sources.name: Thorleifsson G et al. (2010) - Sequence variants in the LOXL1 gene and its association with exfoliation glaucoma

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. The A allele frequency ranges from approximately 0.77% in East Asian to 37% in African ancestry populations, showing strong population stratification. Risk estimates derived primarily from Icelandic (European) cohorts; effect sizes may differ in other ancestries. Regular intraocular pressure measurement and optic nerve evaluation are recommended for individuals with elevated genetic risk.

AA.description: Homozygous TMCO1 risk variant, significantly increased primary open-angle glaucoma susceptibility

AG.description: Heterozygous, moderately elevated intraocular pressure and glaucoma risk

GG.description: Normal TMCO1 function, population-average glaucoma risk

description: TMCO1 transmembrane protein variant is one of the strongest GWAS hits for primary open-angle glaucoma (POAG) and intraocular pressure. TMCO1 may regulate calcium homeostasis in the eye.
notes: TMCO1 is a transmembrane and coiled-coil domain protein involved in ER calcium leak. Glaucoma is the leading cause of irreversible blindness, affecting ~80 million people worldwide.

AA.description: Protective genotype, reduced risk of pseudoexfoliation syndrome

AG.description: Intermediate risk for pseudoexfoliation syndrome and secondary glaucoma

GG.description: Risk genotype for pseudoexfoliation syndrome and exfoliation glaucoma

description: LOXL1 G153D variant is the second major LOXL1 polymorphism associated with pseudoexfoliation syndrome. Works in combination with rs1048661 to determine exfoliation glaucoma risk.
notes: G153D in LOXL1 exon 1 affects elastic fiber formation. Risk allele shows population-specific effects — the risk allele in one population can be protective in another. Exfoliation glaucoma accounts for ~25% of glaucoma worldwide.

TT.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with typical RASGRF1-mediated Ras-MAPK signaling in retinal neurons and lower refractive error susceptibility.

TC.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate RASGRF1 activity and moderately elevated risk for significant refractive error.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate allele linked to altered RASGRF1 expression, disrupted retinal signal transduction, and greater susceptibility to myopia development.

description: RASGRF1 is a guanine nucleotide exchange factor that activates Ras-MAPK signaling in retinal ganglion cells. Variants near RASGRF1 have been identified in GWAS studies as significant contributors to refractive error and myopia risk, particularly in populations with high educational attainment.

sources.name: Solouki et al. (2010) - RASGRF1 and myopia GWAS

notes: Environmental factors (near work, outdoor time) are stronger predictors of myopia than genetics alone in children. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Non-risk genotype at 15q14, baseline refractive error

CT.description: Intermediate effect on eye growth and refractive error

TT.description: Risk genotype, increased susceptibility to myopia and negative refractive error

description: 15q14 locus variant near GJD2 associated with refractive error and myopia susceptibility. One of the first GWAS-identified common myopia risk variants.
notes: Part of the 15q14 myopia susceptibility region near GJD2. Myopia prevalence has doubled in one generation in East Asia (now 80-90% in young adults), suggesting strong gene-environment interaction.

TT.description: Homozygous reference allele associated with higher innate immune activation in retinal cells, linked to increased age-related macular degeneration susceptibility.

TC.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate TLR3 signaling; AMD risk is reduced relative to TT but higher than CC.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate allele associated with reduced TLR3-mediated retinal inflammation and lower geographic atrophy risk in AMD.

description: TLR3 encodes a pattern recognition receptor that mediates innate immune responses to double-stranded RNA. In retinal pigment epithelium, TLR3 activation can trigger apoptosis and inflammation contributing to AMD pathology; the coding variant rs3775291 (Leu412Phe) modifies receptor function and AMD risk.

sources.name: Yang et al. (2008) - TLR3 and age-related macular degeneration

notes: AMD has complex multifactorial genetics; TLR3 is one contributor. Antioxidant supplementation (AREDS2 formula) reduces progression risk. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Homozygous reference (Trp64/Trp64); standard beta-3 adrenergic receptor function with typical lipolytic and thermogenic activity in adipose tissue.

TC.phenotype: Heterozygous Trp64Arg / Slightly Altered Metabolic Response
TC.description: Heterozygous (Trp64/Arg64); one copy of the Arg64 variant may modestly alter beta-3 adrenergic receptor signaling and metabolic response to sympathetic stimulation.

CC.phenotype: Homozygous Arg64 / Reduced Beta-3 Receptor Function
CC.description: Homozygous alternate (Arg64/Arg64); reduced beta-3 adrenergic receptor function associated with modest metabolic differences, including slightly reduced lipolytic response and thermogenesis in adipose tissue.

description: ADRB3 encodes the beta-3 adrenergic receptor, predominantly expressed in white and brown adipose tissue, where it mediates catecholamine-stimulated lipolysis and thermogenesis. The Trp64Arg polymorphism (rs1800888) results in a tryptophan-to-arginine substitution at codon 64, reducing receptor signaling efficiency. This variant has been studied in the context of obesity, type 2 diabetes susceptibility, and metabolic rate. The effect is modest and substantially influenced by diet, physical activity level, and other genetic background.

sources.name: Walston J et al. (1995) - Time of onset of non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and genetic variation in the beta 3-adrenergic-receptor gene

notes: The Trp64Arg variant has a modest effect on metabolic phenotypes. Original meta-analyses showed small effect sizes (OR 1.08-1.18 for obesity). Effect is population-dependent with higher minor allele frequency in East Asian populations (~20%) vs European (~8%). Environmental factors (diet, physical activity) strongly modify any genetic predisposition. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for metabolic concerns.

CC.description: Impaired WNT10A signaling, increased risk of missing teeth (tooth agenesis) and ectodermal features

CT.description: Reduced WNT10A activity, slightly increased risk of congenitally missing teeth

TT.description: Standard WNT10A function, normal tooth development

description: WNT10A is the most common genetic cause of non-syndromic tooth agenesis (congenitally missing teeth). WNT10A signals through the canonical Wnt pathway critical for ectodermal organ development.

sources.name: van den Boogaard MJ et al. (2012)

notes: WNT10A mutations account for ~50% of isolated tooth agenesis cases. Heterozygous carriers often have fewer wisdom teeth or small lateral incisors. Biallelic mutations cause ectodermal dysplasia syndromes.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard regulatory context near HLA-DRB1. Most individuals (~94%) carry this genotype globally, with lower autoimmune disease susceptibility from this locus.

AG.description: Heterozygous; one G allele is associated with altered HLA-DRB1 expression levels or haplotype tagging of high-risk DRB1 alleles (e.g., DRB1*04, *01). Increased risk for rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune conditions.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; G allele ~6-9% globally (higher in South Asian ~19%, East Asian ~12%). Strongest association with autoimmune risk via HLA-DRB1 haplotype tagging in a dominant/additive model.

description: HLA-DRB1 encodes a major histocompatibility complex (MHC) class II beta chain that presents peptide antigens to CD4+ T helper cells, playing a central role in adaptive immune responses. rs12722042 is a non-coding variant near HLA-DRB1 on chromosome 6p21.3 that tags specific HLA-DRB1 risk haplotypes associated with autoimmune diseases including rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, and type 1 diabetes. The MHC region is the most gene-dense autoimmune susceptibility locus in the human genome, and this SNP captures a portion of DRB1-linked susceptibility.

sources.name: Jawaheer D et al. (2003) - Dissecting the genetic complexity of the association between human leukocyte antigens and rheumatoid arthritis

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The G allele is rare in Europeans (~4%) but higher in South Asians (~19%) and East Asians (~12%). HLA-associated autoimmune risks are polygenic and dependent on the full HLA haplotype — a single SNP cannot definitively predict autoimmune disease. Environmental triggers (infections, smoking, hormones) are critical cofactors. Individuals with G allele carriage and family history of autoimmune diseases (RA, lupus, MS, type 1 diabetes) should discuss periodic screening with a healthcare provider.

GG.description: Homozygous reference at the HLA-DRB1 proximal locus. Standard MHC class II antigen presentation; population-average susceptibility to HLA-DR-associated autoimmune conditions.

GA.description: Heterozygous carrier of the A allele near HLA-DRB1. The A allele tags specific HLA-DRB1 haplotypes associated with increased susceptibility to autoimmune conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, type 1 diabetes, and multiple sclerosis.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele; both chromosomes carry the HLA-DRB1-proximal risk haplotype, associated with the highest per-locus autoimmune susceptibility at this region.

description: rs9271366 is located on chromosome 6p21 in the MHC class II region, directly proximal to HLA-DRB1, which encodes the beta chain of the HLA-DR molecule. HLA-DR is an MHC class II receptor that presents peptide antigens to CD4+ helper T cells, directing adaptive immune responses. Specific HLA-DRB1 alleles (particularly those carrying the 'shared epitope' QKRAA/QRRAA motif) are the strongest known genetic risk factors for rheumatoid arthritis and contribute to type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis, and other autoimmune conditions. rs9271366 serves as a proxy tag SNP for high-risk HLA-DRB1 haplotypes.

sources.name: Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium (2007) - Genome-wide association study of 14,000 cases of seven common diseases and 3,000 shared controls

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider. HLA-DRB1 association with autoimmune disease is among the strongest and most replicated in human genetics. rs9271366 as a tag SNP captures HLA-DRB1 haplotype effects but is not a substitute for high-resolution HLA typing. Effect sizes are largest for rheumatoid arthritis (shared epitope OR 2–5 for DRB1*04:01) and type 1 diabetes (DQ haplotypes). Autoimmune risk is polygenic; HLA loci explain 30–50% of genetic risk for most HLA-associated conditions.
