AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard DRD2 expression and dopamine D2 receptor density in striatal and limbic circuits. Typical dopaminergic reward and motor signaling.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; one G allele at this DRD2 intronic/regulatory position. Intermediate dopamine receptor density or signaling efficiency between the two homozygous states.

GG.description: Homozygous G allele; this DRD2 region variant is associated with altered D2 receptor availability and has been studied in the context of reward processing, addiction susceptibility, and neuropsychiatric conditions.

description: DRD2 (dopamine D2 receptor) is a G-protein-coupled receptor expressed in the striatum, nucleus accumbens, and prefrontal cortex that mediates postsynaptic and presynaptic dopaminergic signaling. rs2734838 maps to chromosome 11q23, within or near the DRD2 locus. Variants in DRD2 are among the most studied in psychiatric genetics, with associations reported for schizophrenia, Parkinson's disease, addiction phenotypes, and antipsychotic drug response. D2 receptor availability measured by PET imaging correlates with variants in this region.
notes: dbSNP maps rs2734838 to chromosome 11 near the DRD2 locus. Any prior association with DYRK1A or chromosome 21 was based on an incorrect assembly mapping and has been corrected in this entry. DRD2 variants are relevant to antipsychotic pharmacogenomics — D2 receptor density influences both therapeutic response and side-effect profiles of antipsychotic medications. This is a research-level association, not a clinical diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Enhanced hippocampal memory consolidation, better performance on episodic memory tasks

CT.description: Intermediate memory performance, moderate hippocampal activation

TT.description: Reduced hippocampal efficiency during memory tasks, lower episodic memory scores

description: KIBRA (WWC1) variant affects memory consolidation through its role in synaptic plasticity and long-term potentiation in the hippocampus.
notes: KIBRA interacts with PKMzeta and synaptopodin, proteins critical for long-term potentiation. Memory effect has been replicated across multiple cohorts. The T allele associated with less efficient hippocampal activation on fMRI. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Homozygous reference A allele; standard LINGO1 intronic variant context. LINGO1 expression in cerebellar and olivary neurons is at baseline, associated with standard essential tremor risk for the population.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; one G allele at this intronic LINGO1 position. Associated with modestly elevated essential tremor risk in GWAS meta-analyses. LINGO1 may influence myelination or cerebellar circuit integrity.

GG.description: Homozygous G allele; both LINGO1 alleles carry this intronic risk variant. Associated with approximately 1.5–2× elevated odds of essential tremor versus AA genotype in multiple GWAS studies across European and North American cohorts. LINGO1 is the most replicated common genetic risk factor for essential tremor.

description: LINGO1 (leucine-rich repeat and immunoglobulin-like domain-containing nogo receptor-interacting protein 1) is expressed in neurons of the cerebellum, inferior olivary nucleus, and other brain regions, where it functions as a negative regulator of axonal myelination and neurological circuit formation. LINGO1 inhibits myelination by blocking EGFR and ErbB2 signaling and interacts with the Nogo receptor to suppress axon regeneration and myelination. The rs9652490 intronic variant was identified in one of the first GWAS for essential tremor, and the LINGO1 locus represents the most consistently replicated genetic risk factor for this common movement disorder affecting ~1% of the general population.

sources.name: Stefansson H et al. (2009) - Variant in the sequence of the LINGO1 gene confers risk of essential tremor

notes: G allele frequency is approximately 20–25% in European populations. Essential tremor is the most common adult movement disorder; LINGO1 variants contribute a modest odds ratio (~1.5) representing one of several contributing genetic factors. LINGO1 is also under investigation as a therapeutic target for multiple sclerosis (remyelination) and Parkinson's disease. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This represents a statistical risk factor identified through population studies. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard GAD67 enzyme expression supports normal cortical GABA biosynthesis and inhibitory neurotransmission.

CT.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in GAD1 expression may subtly affect cortical GABAergic tone and excitation-inhibition balance.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with lower GAD67 expression in parvalbumin interneurons, potentially affecting cortical inhibitory circuits.

description: GAD1 encodes glutamate decarboxylase 67 kDa isoform (GAD67), the primary enzyme responsible for synthesizing GABA from glutamate in cortical interneurons. GAD67 is particularly important in fast-spiking parvalbumin-positive interneurons that coordinate cortical oscillations. Variants in GAD1 have been associated with altered cortical excitation-inhibition balance.

sources.name: Addington et al. (2005) - Association of GAD1 with schizophrenia and cognitive performance

notes: GAD1 variants are studied in cortical E/I balance and neurodevelopment research. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

CT.description: GBA variant carrier, ~3-5x increased PD risk, earlier onset and more rapid cognitive decline

CC.description: Homozygous GBA variant, Gaucher disease risk and very high Parkinson's susceptibility

TT.description: Wild-type GBA, population-average Parkinson's disease risk

description: GBA variant linking Gaucher disease and Parkinson's disease. Demonstrates the connection between lysosomal dysfunction and alpha-synuclein aggregation in neurodegeneration.
notes: GBA mutations impair glucocerebrosidase, causing glucocerebroside accumulation that promotes alpha-synuclein aggregation. GBA-targeted therapies (ambroxol, venglustat) are in clinical trials for Parkinson's. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CT.description: GBA carrier, ~5x increased Parkinson's disease risk, earlier onset tendency

CC.description: Wild-type GBA, population-average Parkinson's disease risk

TT.description: Homozygous GBA variant, Gaucher disease and very high Parkinson's risk

description: GBA encodes glucocerebrosidase, a lysosomal enzyme. GBA mutations are the most common genetic risk factor for Parkinson's disease, linking lysosomal dysfunction to alpha-synuclein aggregation.
notes: GBA mutations are found in 5-15% of PD patients. Homozygous mutations cause Gaucher disease. GBA-PD has earlier onset and faster cognitive decline. GCase activation is a therapeutic strategy. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard GluN2A subunit expression maintains normal NMDA receptor function, LTP, and synaptic maturation.

CT.description: Heterozygous; intermediate GluN2A expression may modestly affect NMDA receptor decay kinetics and synaptic plasticity thresholds.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; altered GRIN2A expression associated with changes in NMDA receptor gating and excitatory synaptic transmission.

description: GRIN2A encodes the GluN2A subunit of NMDA receptors, which become the predominant GluN2 isoform in mature cortical synapses through a developmental switch from GluN2B. GluN2A-containing receptors have faster deactivation kinetics and are critical for experience-dependent synaptic refinement. Variants in GRIN2A have been implicated in glutamate signaling differences.

sources.name: Bhouri et al. (2012) - Association study of GRIN2A polymorphisms in neurodevelopmental disorders

notes: GRIN2A variants are studied in epilepsy and neurodevelopment research contexts. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

CC.description: Standard FKBP5 expression, normal cortisol feedback and HPA axis regulation

CT.description: Moderately increased FKBP5 expression, slightly prolonged cortisol response to stress

TT.description: Increased FKBP5 expression, impaired cortisol feedback, prolonged stress response, PTSD vulnerability

description: FKBP5 co-chaperone protein regulates glucocorticoid receptor sensitivity. Variants alter HPA axis feedback, affecting cortisol response duration and susceptibility to stress-related disorders.
notes: FKBP5 T allele increases protein expression, which inhibits glucocorticoid receptor nuclear translocation, creating ultra-short negative feedback loop dysfunction. Gene x environment interaction with childhood trauma for PTSD. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Resistant to brain freeze

CT.description: Moderate brain freeze susceptibility

TT.description: Highly susceptible to ice cream headaches

description: Cold-sensing receptor variant affecting susceptibility to cold-stimulus headaches.

sources.name: Anttila V et al. (2013) - Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies new susceptibility loci for migraine

notes: TRPM8 variant primarily associated with migraine susceptibility. The link to 'brain freeze' (cold-stimulus headache) is indirect — TRPM8 is a cold-sensing receptor. Migraine genetics are highly polygenic with hundreds of loci. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AT.description: Moderate CNTNAP2 effect on language development and neuronal connectivity

AA.description: Standard CNTNAP2 function, typical language and social communication development

TT.description: Altered CNTNAP2 expression, affecting neuronal connectivity in frontal and temporal language circuits

description: CNTNAP2 (contactin-associated protein-like 2) variant affects neuronal connectivity and language development. CNTNAP2 is one of the largest genes in the human genome and plays a key role in clustering potassium channels at nodes of Ranvier in cortical circuits involved in language processing.
notes: CNTNAP2 is one of the largest genes in the human genome (2.3Mb). It clusters potassium channels at nodes of Ranvier. Disruptions affect language lateralization, frontal lobe connectivity, and social cognition. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous ALAD*1 allele (Lys59); standard delta-aminolevulinic acid dehydratase protein with typical lead-binding capacity. Plasma lead is distributed normally across blood compartments without enhanced tissue sequestration.

CG.description: Heterozygous for ALAD*2 (Asn59); ALAD enzyme has higher affinity for lead ions. Blood lead levels may be maintained higher relative to tissue lead burden, altering the apparent toxicokinetics of lead exposure.

GG.phenotype: High Lead-Binding ALAD / Redistributed Lead Toxicity (ALAD*2/ALAD*2)
GG.description: Homozygous ALAD*2 (Asn59/Asn59); substantially higher ALAD protein affinity for lead at its zinc-binding active site. Blood lead concentrations may paradoxically remain elevated (less released to tissues), but this may modify the tissue distribution and neurological impact of cumulative lead exposure.

description: ALAD (aminolevulinate dehydratase) catalyzes the condensation of two molecules of delta-aminolevulinic acid in the heme biosynthesis pathway and is also a major inorganic lead-binding protein in erythrocytes. The rs1800435 C>G variant (Lys59Asn) defines the ALAD*1 vs ALAD*2 alleles and confers higher affinity of the ALAD enzyme for lead ions due to the Asn59 substitution altering the zinc-binding site. ALAD*2 carriers exposed to environmental lead show altered lead toxicokinetics — higher blood lead retention and potentially modified distribution to bone and neurological tissue — with complex and context-dependent effects on neurodevelopmental and cognitive outcomes.

sources.name: Onalaja AO & Claudio L (2000) - Genetic susceptibility to lead poisoning

notes: G allele (ALAD*2) frequency is approximately 10–16% in European and South Asian populations and approximately 1–3% in African populations. Lead neurotoxicity risk depends on both genotype and cumulative environmental lead exposure; this variant is most clinically relevant in occupationally exposed populations (battery workers, painters, plumbers) or individuals raised in pre-1978 housing with leaded paint. Chelation therapy may be indicated for high blood lead levels regardless of genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Homozygous LRRK2 G2019S, very high lifetime Parkinson's disease risk

AG.description: LRRK2 G2019S carrier, ~30-75% lifetime risk of Parkinson's disease (age-dependent penetrance)

GG.description: Wild-type LRRK2, population-average Parkinson's disease risk

description: LRRK2 G2019S is the most common genetic cause of familial Parkinson's disease, causing gain-of-function kinase activity that damages dopaminergic neurons.
notes: LRRK2 G2019S is found in ~1% of sporadic and 4% of familial Parkinson's cases. Penetrance is age-dependent (~30% by age 60, ~75% by age 80). Common in Ashkenazi Jewish and North African Berber populations. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.phenotype: Standard MAPT Haplotype — Reference Tau Regulation
AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard MAPT haplotype context on chromosome 17q21. Normal tau protein expression, splicing, and microtubule binding. Baseline population risk for tauopathy-associated neurodegenerative conditions.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; one G allele at this MAPT/SPPL2C region position. Intermediate haplotype context between the H1 and H2 MAPT haplotype blocks, with corresponding intermediate tau expression and splicing patterns.

GG.phenotype: Alternate MAPT Haplotype — Altered Tau Regulation
GG.description: Homozygous G allele; alternative MAPT region haplotype associated with altered tau protein isoform balance and expression levels. The MAPT locus H1 haplotype is associated with increased risk for progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), corticobasal degeneration (CBD), and Parkinson's disease.

description: rs12185268 maps to chromosome 17q21 near MAPT (microtubule-associated protein tau) and SPPL2C. MAPT encodes tau, the microtubule-stabilizing protein that forms neurofibrillary tangles in Alzheimer's disease and other tauopathies. The chromosome 17q21 region contains two major MAPT haplotype blocks (H1 and H2) associated with differential risk for progressive supranuclear palsy, corticobasal degeneration, Parkinson's disease, and Alzheimer's disease. Common variants in this region serve as proxies for MAPT haplotype membership and associated tau expression and splicing differences.

sources.name: Baker M et al. (1999) - Association of an extended haplotype in the tau gene with progressive supranuclear palsy

notes: dbSNP maps rs12185268 to chromosome 17 near SPPL2C/MAPT-AS1, adjacent to the MAPT locus. Prior annotation linking this rsID to ATXN2 or chromosome 12 was based on an incorrect mapping and has been corrected. The MAPT H1 haplotype is one of the best-established genetic risk factors for tauopathies including PSP and CBD (OR ~5 for PSP). ALS associations at this locus are not established — the ATXN2/ALS connection involves chromosome 12 and is a separate gene/locus. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CT.description: TREM2 R47H carrier, ~2-4x increased late-onset Alzheimer's disease risk via impaired microglial function

CC.description: Normal TREM2 function, standard microglial phagocytic capacity

TT.description: Homozygous TREM2 R47H, significantly impaired microglial function and elevated Alzheimer's risk

description: TREM2 R47H variant impairs microglial phagocytosis and clearance of amyloid-beta and apoptotic neurons. The strongest Alzheimer's risk variant discovered since APOE e4.
notes: TREM2 R47H is rare (~0.5% carrier frequency) but has strong effect (OR 2-4 for Alzheimer's). Discovered 2012-2013, confirmed the neuroinflammation hypothesis of Alzheimer's. TREM2 is now a therapeutic target. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Lower migraine risk

CT.description: Moderate migraine susceptibility

TT.description: Higher migraine with aura risk

description: Astrocyte glutamate transporter variant affecting cortical excitability and migraine.

sources.name: Anttila V et al. (2010) - Genome-wide association study of migraine implicates a common susceptibility variant on 8q22.1

notes: First GWAS-identified migraine susceptibility locus near MTDH/AEG-1. Effect is modest (OR ~1.2). Migraine with aura has a complex genetic architecture with dozens of confirmed loci. Environmental triggers (stress, sleep, diet) remain the primary modifiable risk factors. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AG.description: HLA-DRB1*1501 tag carrier, ~3x increased multiple sclerosis risk

AA.description: No HLA-DRB1*1501 risk allele, population-average MS risk

GG.description: Homozygous HLA-DRB1*1501 tag, ~6x increased multiple sclerosis risk

description: HLA-DRB1*1501 tag SNP is the strongest genetic risk factor for multiple sclerosis. The MHC class II molecule presents myelin-derived peptides, potentially triggering autoimmune demyelination.
notes: HLA-DRB1*1501 confers the largest single-gene effect on MS risk. Interacts with EBV infection (nearly all MS patients are EBV+), vitamin D deficiency, and smoking. Risk allele frequency ~14% in Europeans. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard NRG1 signaling through ErbB receptors promotes normal Schwann cell and oligodendrocyte-mediated myelination.

CG.description: Heterozygous; partial alteration in NRG1 expression may moderately affect ErbB2/ErbB4-driven myelination in central and peripheral nervous system.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with altered NRG1-ErbB myelination signaling, potentially affecting white matter integrity.

description: NRG1 encodes neuregulin-1, which signals through ErbB2 and ErbB4 receptors on Schwann cells and oligodendrocytes to regulate myelination in both the peripheral and central nervous systems. This NRG1 5' haplotype region (rs1006131) has been studied for its role in modulating myelination-related phenotypes distinct from the rs2241165 variant.

sources.name: Stefansson et al. (2004) - Neuregulin 1 and susceptibility to schizophrenia

notes: rs1006131 tags the NRG1 HAP(ICE) haplotype studied in myelination research. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard GluN2B subunit expression supporting normal NMDA receptor-mediated synaptic plasticity and LTP.

CT.description: Heterozygous; partial alteration in GluN2B expression or function may modestly affect NMDA receptor kinetics and synaptic plasticity.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with altered GluN2B-containing receptor properties, potentially affecting long-term potentiation mechanisms.

description: GRIN2B encodes the GluN2B subunit of NMDA-type glutamate receptors, which are central to synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory consolidation. GluN2B-containing receptors have distinct kinetic properties and are critical during neurodevelopment. Variants in GRIN2B have been studied in relation to neurodevelopmental and cognitive phenotypes.

sources.name: Martucci et al. (2003) - Association of GRIN2B variants with neurodevelopmental and cognitive phenotypes

notes: GRIN2B variants are studied in neurodevelopment and cognitive research; not diagnostic. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

CT.description: HLA-DQB1*0602 tag carrier, significantly increased narcolepsy type 1 susceptibility

CC.description: No HLA-DQB1*0602 risk allele, very low narcolepsy risk

TT.description: Homozygous HLA-DQB1*0602 tag, highest genetic risk for narcolepsy type 1

description: HLA-DQB1*0602 tag SNP is the strongest genetic determinant of narcolepsy type 1 (with cataplexy). Present in >98% of narcolepsy type 1 patients, enabling autoimmune destruction of hypocretin neurons.
notes: HLA-DQB1*0602 is necessary but not sufficient for narcolepsy (~25% of population carries it, but narcolepsy prevalence is ~0.05%). Narcolepsy type 1 is now considered autoimmune destruction of hypocretin/orexin neurons. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard neuregulin-1 expression supports normal ErbB4 receptor activation and GABAergic interneuron development.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate NRG1 expression may modestly affect ErbB4-mediated synaptic maturation and myelination in cortical circuits.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced or dysregulated NRG1-ErbB4 signaling may impact GABAergic interneuron function and cortical myelination.

description: NRG1 encodes neuregulin-1, a growth factor that activates ErbB receptor tyrosine kinases to regulate neuronal migration, myelination, and synaptogenesis. It plays a critical role in establishing cortical inhibitory interneuron circuits. Variants at rs2241165 have been studied in the context of neurodevelopmental signaling differences.

sources.name: Stefansson et al. (2004) - Neuregulin 1 and susceptibility to schizophrenia

notes: NRG1 variants are research markers for cortical signaling differences; not diagnostic. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; lower IL-1β production at the -511 promoter position, associated with reduced cytokine-mediated neuroinflammatory signaling.

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate IL-1β production levels with moderate neuroinflammatory potential in response to CNS injury or infection.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; higher IL-1β promoter activity associated with increased cytokine production and amplified neuroinflammatory cascades.

description: IL1B encodes interleukin-1 beta, a pro-inflammatory cytokine produced by microglia and astrocytes in the central nervous system. IL-1β signaling modulates microglial activation, synaptic pruning, and neuroinflammatory responses to injury or infection. The rs16944 variant at position -511 in the IL1B promoter affects transcription factor binding and cytokine production levels.

sources.name: Bhatt et al. (2003) - IL-1β promoter polymorphisms and neurological inflammation

notes: IL1B neuroinflammation markers are studied in neurodegeneration and cognitive aging research. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard norepinephrine transporter expression and reuptake kinetics maintain normal synaptic norepinephrine clearance.

TC.description: Heterozygous; partial alteration in NET expression may modestly affect norepinephrine reuptake efficiency and synaptic catecholamine levels.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with altered norepinephrine transporter levels, potentially affecting catecholamine signaling in the locus coeruleus circuit.

description: SLC6A2 encodes the norepinephrine transporter (NET), which mediates synaptic reuptake of norepinephrine and is the primary mechanism for terminating noradrenergic neurotransmission. NET is also a target for norepinephrine-dopamine reuptake inhibitors. Variants at rs2242446 have been studied in relation to attentional and autonomic phenotypes.

sources.name: Kim et al. (2006) - Association of norepinephrine transporter gene polymorphisms with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder

notes: SLC6A2 variants are studied in ADHD and autonomic regulation research. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; associated with higher TPH1 enzyme activity in peripheral tissues such as gut enterochromaffin cells and platelets.

AC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate TPH1 activity resulting in moderate peripheral serotonin production in enterochromaffin and blood cells.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with lower TPH1 catalytic activity and reduced peripheral serotonin biosynthesis capacity.

description: TPH1 encodes tryptophan hydroxylase 1, the rate-limiting enzyme for serotonin biosynthesis in peripheral tissues including gut enterochromaffin cells and pineal gland. It catalyzes the conversion of tryptophan to 5-hydroxytryptophan. Variants at rs1799913 have been associated with differences in peripheral serotonin levels and platelet serotonin content.

sources.name: Jonsson et al. (2004) - A variant in the tryptophan hydroxylase-1 gene is associated with stress-related autonomic responses

notes: TPH1 primarily affects peripheral serotonin; TPH2 governs CNS serotonin. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

AA.description: Homozygous reference A allele; standard copper-zinc superoxide dismutase expression supporting antioxidant defense in motor neurons and all cell types.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; one G allele at this intronic SOD1 position. May modulate SOD1 expression or splicing, with intermediate effects on cellular antioxidant capacity.

GG.description: Homozygous G allele; intronic position may influence SOD1 alternative splicing or transcript stability, with potential effects on superoxide dismutase activity and cellular protection against oxidative stress.

description: SOD1 (superoxide dismutase 1) encodes the copper-zinc superoxide dismutase enzyme, the primary cytosolic antioxidant enzyme in motor neurons and all human cells. SOD1 converts superoxide radicals (O2-) to hydrogen peroxide, protecting against oxidative damage. Dominant gain-of-function mutations in SOD1 cause approximately 20% of familial ALS cases, but common intronic variants like rs2070424 may influence SOD1 expression levels and thus modulate the threshold for oxidative motor neuron injury in sporadic ALS. This intronic variant has been studied in ALS cohorts as a potential modifier of disease susceptibility rather than a direct causal mutation.

sources.name: Conforti FL et al. (2006) - A novel SOD1 mutation in an Italian family with ALS and a common SOD1 variant analysis

notes: SOD1 dominantly inherited gain-of-toxic-function mutations (such as A4V, D90A) cause approximately 20% of familial ALS cases — these are separate, pathogenic variants distinct from this common intronic SNP. The rs2070424 intronic variant has been examined in sporadic ALS cohorts as a potential expression modifier (Conforti et al. 2006). G allele frequency varies substantially by ancestry (~51% in Asian vs ~7% in European populations). This common variant does not cause ALS. Genetic counseling is recommended for interpretation. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: Standard ZNF804A expression, population-average brain connectivity

AC.description: Intermediate ZNF804A effect, modestly altered brain connectivity

CC.description: Altered ZNF804A expression, affecting brain connectivity and prefrontal-hippocampal coupling

description: ZNF804A was the first gene to reach genome-wide significance for schizophrenia. The zinc finger protein affects brain connectivity, white matter integrity, and Theory of Mind processing.
notes: ZNF804A risk allele affects inter-hemispheric connectivity and prefrontal-hippocampal coupling. The variant also increases bipolar disorder risk, supporting the schizophrenia-bipolar genetic overlap. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

TT.description: Lower ZBTB20 circadian expression variance, typical seasonal mood regulation

TC.description: Intermediate ZBTB20 effect on circadian mood regulation

CC.description: Altered ZBTB20 circadian expression, increased sensitivity to seasonal light changes

description: Circadian rhythm gene variant affecting seasonal mood and energy regulation.

sources.name: Levitan RD et al. (2004) - Seasonality-associated serotonin transporter gene polymorphisms and seasonal affective disorder

notes: Tentatively associated with seasonal affective disorder. Evidence is limited — SAD genetics are poorly understood and most studies have been underpowered. The condition is strongly influenced by latitude, light exposure, and serotonin pathway function. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard SNAP25 protein function in SNARE complex assembly supports normal neurotransmitter exocytosis.

TC.description: Heterozygous; partial alteration in SNAP25 function may modestly affect SNARE-mediated neurotransmitter release efficiency at synapses.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; variant associated with altered SNAP25 expression or SNARE complex kinetics, potentially affecting synaptic transmission.

description: SNAP25 encodes synaptosomal-associated protein 25, a core component of the SNARE complex essential for synaptic vesicle docking and neurotransmitter exocytosis at the presynaptic terminal. It partners with syntaxin and VAMP to mediate calcium-triggered vesicle fusion. Variants at rs362691 have been associated with differences in synaptic transmission efficiency.

sources.name: Barr CL et al. (2000) - Identification of DNA variants in the SNAP-25 gene and linkage study of these polymorphisms and ADHD

notes: SNAP25 variants are studied in ADHD and neurodevelopmental research contexts. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

AA.description: Increased alpha-synuclein levels, elevated Parkinson's disease and Lewy body risk

AG.description: Moderate alpha-synuclein levels, slightly elevated PD risk

GG.description: Normal alpha-synuclein expression, population-average Parkinson's risk

description: SNCA 3' UTR variant modulates alpha-synuclein mRNA stability and expression. Gene dosage is critical — duplications and triplications cause familial Parkinson's with increasing severity.
notes: Alpha-synuclein aggregation into Lewy bodies is the hallmark of PD pathology. Even modest expression increases (~1.5x) from common variants contribute to population PD risk. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

AA.description: H1/H1 MAPT haplotype, slightly increased risk of progressive supranuclear palsy and tauopathies

AG.description: Mixed MAPT haplotype, intermediate tauopathy risk

GG.description: H2/H2 MAPT haplotype, reduced risk of tauopathies and Parkinson's disease

description: MAPT H1/H2 haplotype tag SNP affects tau protein expression and splicing. H1 is associated with increased risk of tauopathies including progressive supranuclear palsy and Parkinson's disease.
notes: The H1 haplotype spans a 1.8Mb inversion on chromosome 17. H2 frequency ~20% in Europeans but very rare in East Asian and African populations. H1 increases tau expression and 4-repeat tau isoforms. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard CNTNAP2 expression supports typical myelination and axonal organization in frontal cortex circuits.

CT.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in CNTNAP2-mediated axonal clustering may subtly influence frontal lobe white matter integrity.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; both copies carry the variant, associated with measurable reductions in frontal white matter fractional anisotropy in neuroimaging studies.

description: CNTNAP2 encodes contactin-associated protein-like 2, a neurexin superfamily member essential for clustering potassium channels and organizing myelinated axons. It is highly expressed in frontal cortex circuits. Variants in CNTNAP2 have been linked to differences in white matter connectivity and language-related neural networks.

sources.name: Arking et al. (2008) - A common genetic variant in the neurexin superfamily member CNTNAP2 increases familial risk of autism

notes: CNTNAP2 variants are research markers for connectivity differences; not diagnostic. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider.

CC.description: Reduced hippocampal efficiency during memory encoding, lower working memory scores

CT.description: Intermediate hippocampal activation and memory performance

TT.description: Enhanced hippocampal memory consolidation, better working memory and learning rate

description: KIBRA (WWC1) variant affects working memory and learning rate through modulation of synaptic plasticity in the hippocampus. Replicated memory-associated gene.
notes: KIBRA T allele carriers show better episodic memory performance across multiple studies. The protein interacts with PKMzeta and dendrin at postsynaptic densities, modulating long-term potentiation. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions.

GG.description: Normal ALDH2 enzyme function, no flush reaction

GA.description: Reduced ALDH2 activity, facial flushing with alcohol, increased hangover, higher cancer risk

AA.description: Very low ALDH2 activity, severe facial flushing, nausea with small amounts of alcohol, strongly protective against alcoholism

description: ALDH2*2 variant (Glu504Lys) causing aldehyde dehydrogenase deficiency. Common in East Asians (~40% carriers). Leads to acetaldehyde accumulation and 'Asian flush' reaction.
notes: ALDH2*2 variant causing the 'Asian flush' reaction to alcohol. Nearly 40% of East Asians carry at least one copy. Homozygotes have severe alcohol intolerance. Also associated with increased esophageal cancer risk with alcohol consumption.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard CYP11B2 upstream regulation supports moderate aldosterone synthesis, typical salt handling and blood pressure response.

AC.description: Heterozygous; moderate CYP11B2 expression shift yielding intermediate aldosterone levels and blood pressure sensitivity to dietary sodium.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; increased CYP11B2 expression drives higher aldosterone production, promoting renal sodium retention and elevated blood pressure on high-salt diets.

description: CYP11B2 encodes aldosterone synthase, the final enzyme in the mineralocorticoid biosynthesis pathway. The rs1799998 upstream variant influences CYP11B2 transcription, modulating aldosterone production. Higher aldosterone promotes renal sodium retention, expanding plasma volume and raising blood pressure in response to dietary salt.

sources.name: Yoshimura M et al. (1998) - CYP11B2 variants and blood pressure phenotypes

notes: Salt sensitivity of blood pressure is influenced by multiple genes (ACE, AGT, ADD1) plus kidney function and BMI. Dietary sodium reduction benefits individuals with this genotype more than average. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis of hypertension.

CC.description: Normal melanocortin-4 receptor function and appetite regulation.

CT.description: One copy of V103I gain-of-function variant; associated with ~18% reduced obesity risk.

TT.description: Two copies of V103I protective variant; associated with further reduced obesity risk.

description: The rs2229616 variant (Val103Ile) in the MC4R gene is a gain-of-function polymorphism that enhances melanocortin receptor signaling. Carriers are protected against obesity, with meta-analyses showing an approximately 18% reduced risk per copy of the Ile103 allele.

sources.name: Heid IM et al. (2005) - Association of the MC4R V103I polymorphism with the metabolic syndrome

notes: This is a protective variant, not a risk factor. The T allele (Ile103) is rare (~1.9% globally) but consistent in its protective effect across multiple large meta-analyses. Unlike the loss-of-function MC4R mutations that cause severe monogenic obesity, this gain-of-function variant enhances hypothalamic satiety signaling. Discuss with a healthcare provider for personalized interpretation.

CC.description: Normal BCO1 cleavage activity, efficient conversion of beta-carotene to retinal

CT.description: ~30% reduced BCO1 activity, less efficient beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion

TT.description: ~50% reduced BCO1 activity, poor beta-carotene conversion, may need preformed vitamin A sources

description: BCMO1 (BCO1) A379V variant reduces beta-carotene oxygenase activity. Combined with rs12934922, determines overall efficiency of plant-derived vitamin A production.
notes: Combined with rs12934922, up to 69% reduction in beta-carotene conversion in double homozygotes. Important for vegans and populations relying on plant-based vitamin A. ~25% carry at least one reduced-function allele.

AA.description: Rapid caffeine breakdown, can drink coffee late without sleep issues, may need more for effects

AC.description: Moderate caffeine metabolism rate

CC.description: Slow caffeine breakdown, caffeine effects last longer, may have sleep issues with afternoon coffee, increased hypertension risk with high intake

description: CYP1A2*1F variant determining caffeine metabolism speed. AA (fast) metabolizers clear caffeine ~4x faster than CC (slow). Affects coffee tolerance and cardiovascular effects.

sources.name: Sachse C et al. (1999) - Functional significance of a C→A polymorphism in intron 1 of the cytochrome P450 CYP1A2 gene

notes: CYP1A2 variant determining fast vs slow caffeine metabolism. Fast metabolizers (AA genotype) clear caffeine ~4x faster. Clinically relevant — slow metabolizers may have increased cardiovascular risk with high coffee intake. Affects drug metabolism beyond caffeine.

TT.description: Lower habitual coffee intake

TC.description: Moderate coffee consumption

CC.description: Higher habitual coffee consumption tendency

description: Aryl hydrocarbon receptor variant influencing caffeine reward pathway and consumption habits.

sources.name: Cornelis MC et al. (2011) - Genome-wide meta-analysis identifies regions on 7p21 (AHR) and 15q24 (CYP1A2) as determinants of habitual caffeine consumption

notes: AHR variant associated with habitual coffee consumption in large GWAS. AHR regulates CYP1A2 expression, linking this to caffeine metabolism. Effect is consistent across populations. Coffee consumption is also heavily influenced by cultural and socioeconomic factors.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; higher CD36 expression in taste receptor cells supports robust oral fat detection, enabling perception of low-fat concentrations.

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate CD36-mediated oral fat sensing with moderate taste threshold for dietary lipids.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced CD36 expression lowers oral long-chain fatty acid detection, requiring higher fat concentrations for taste perception.

description: CD36 (fatty acid translocase) is expressed in taste bud cells of the tongue where it serves as a receptor for long-chain fatty acids. CD36-mediated oral fat sensing triggers cephalic phase lipid responses and influences dietary fat preference. The A allele reduces CD36 expression, blunting fat taste sensitivity and potentially increasing fat intake to compensate.

sources.name: Pepino MY et al. (2012) - Taste perception and BMI in the context of CD36

notes: Fat taste sensitivity influences food choices but does not determine diet quality; conscious dietary choices remain the primary driver. CD36 is also expressed in enterocytes and macrophages with roles in lipid absorption and atherosclerosis. A allele frequency is approximately 35-45% in various populations.

TT.description: Normal alcohol metabolism

TC.description: Faster alcohol to acetaldehyde conversion - mild flush

CC.description: Rapid conversion - strong facial flushing

description: Alcohol dehydrogenase fast-metabolizer variant causing acetaldehyde accumulation.

sources.name: Li H et al. (2009) - Refined geographic distribution of the East Asian ALDH2*504Lys (nee 487Lys) variant

notes: ADH1B variant causing rapid alcohol metabolism (Arg48His). Common in East Asian populations (~70-90% frequency). Carriers experience acetaldehyde accumulation causing facial flushing, nausea, and headache with alcohol. Protective against alcoholism but associated with esophageal cancer risk in drinkers.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard GCKR inhibitory activity on glucokinase. Associated with typical fasting glucose levels and lower triglycerides. T allele frequency ~39% globally.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate GCKR regulatory activity. Fasting glucose and triglyceride levels fall between the TT and CC extremes, with modest elevation in hepatic glucose uptake.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; the C allele reduces GCKR inhibitory binding to glucokinase, increasing hepatic glucose uptake and glycolysis. Results in lower fasting glucose but elevated fasting triglycerides due to increased de novo lipogenesis. C allele is more common (~61% globally).

description: GCKR (glucokinase regulatory protein) encodes an inhibitory protein that sequesters hepatic glucokinase in the nucleus, preventing glucose phosphorylation when glucose is low. The rs780094 variant (in strong LD with the functional rs1260326 variant) modulates this inhibitory activity: the C allele reduces GCKR binding to glucokinase, allowing more active hepatic glucose uptake and glycolysis. This produces a paradoxical phenotype — lower fasting glucose but higher fasting triglycerides — due to redirected carbon flux toward de novo lipogenesis.

sources.name: Sparso T et al. (2008) - The GCKR rs780094 polymorphism is associated with elevated fasting serum triacylglycerol, reduced fasting and OGTT-related insulinaemia, and reduced risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The CC genotype confers a paradoxical metabolic effect: marginally lower type 2 diabetes risk (due to lower fasting glucose) but elevated triglycerides (mild cardiovascular risk factor). African populations show lowest T allele frequency (~18%), East Asians the highest (~52%). Individuals with CC genotype and family history of hypertriglyceridemia or pancreatitis should discuss lipid monitoring with a healthcare provider. Dietary fat modification can partially offset triglyceride elevation.

CC.description: Higher FADS2 delta-6-desaturase activity, efficient omega-3 and omega-6 processing

CT.description: Moderate FADS2 activity, intermediate fatty acid processing

TT.description: Lower FADS2 activity, less efficient fatty acid conversion, may benefit from direct EPA/DHA intake

description: FADS2 delta-6-desaturase variant affects the rate-limiting step in long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid synthesis from dietary precursors (ALA to EPA, LA to GLA).
notes: FADS2 performs the first desaturation step in both omega-3 and omega-6 pathways. The FADS1/FADS2 gene cluster shows strong signals of positive selection in populations with plant-based diets.

CC.description: Full MTHFR enzyme activity, normal folate and homocysteine metabolism

CT.description: ~65% MTHFR enzyme activity, mildly elevated homocysteine levels

TT.description: ~30% MTHFR enzyme activity, elevated homocysteine, increased folate supplementation needs

description: MTHFR C677T (Ala222Val) reduces methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase activity, impairing folate metabolism and raising homocysteine levels. One of the most clinically relevant nutrigenomic variants.
notes: TT genotype has ~30% residual enzyme activity. Elevated homocysteine is a cardiovascular risk factor. Adequate folate intake can compensate. Important during pregnancy for neural tube defect prevention.

AA.description: Full enzyme activity at A1298C position, normal folate metabolism

AC.description: Slightly reduced MTHFR activity (~85%), mild effect on folate metabolism

CC.description: Reduced MTHFR activity (~60-70%), may compound with C677T for elevated homocysteine

description: MTHFR A1298C (Glu429Ala) variant mildly reduces enzyme activity. Clinically significant mainly when compound heterozygous with C677T, impairing folate-mediated one-carbon metabolism.

sources.name: van der Put NM et al. (1998)

notes: A1298C alone has mild effect. Compound heterozygosity (677CT + 1298AC) can reduce MTHFR activity to ~50%, similar to 677TT alone. The C allele frequency is ~30% in most populations.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; GCKR Pro446 isoform with normal inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Standard fasting glucose regulation and lower de novo lipogenesis. T allele ~40% globally.

TC.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the Leu446 variant partially reduces GCKR's inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Intermediate effect on hepatic glucose metabolism and triglyceride synthesis.

CC.description: Homozygous Leu446; the C allele encodes a GCKR protein (Pro446Leu) with markedly reduced inhibitory binding to glucokinase. Hepatic glucokinase is more constitutively active, driving lower fasting glucose but elevated triglycerides. C allele ~60% globally.

description: rs1260326 encodes the functional GCKR Pro446Leu amino acid variant, the primary causal SNP at this locus. The Leu446 (C allele) isoform of glucokinase regulatory protein has substantially reduced affinity for glucokinase, resulting in persistent hepatic glucokinase activity even during fasting. This increases hepatic glucose phosphorylation, glycolysis, and downstream de novo lipogenesis — lowering fasting plasma glucose while elevating fasting triglycerides. This variant is one of the most replicated metabolic GWAS hits, with consistent effects across diverse populations.

sources.name: Sparso T et al. (2008) - The GCKR rs780094 polymorphism is associated with elevated fasting serum triacylglycerol, reduced fasting and OGTT-related insulinaemia, and reduced risk of type 2 diabetes

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. rs1260326 is the functional causal variant at the GCKR locus (Pro446Leu), while rs780094 is a proxy marker in high LD. The C allele prevalence is ~60% globally (~58% European, ~85% African, ~47% East Asian). The elevated triglyceride association is most pronounced in the context of high carbohydrate or high-fat diets. Individuals with CC genotype and elevated fasting triglycerides should discuss dietary optimization and monitoring with a healthcare provider.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Thr110); normal GLUT2 function in pancreatic beta cells and gut epithelium, typical glucose-stimulated insulin release and sugar preference.

GA.description: Heterozygous; one Ile110 allele modestly alters GLUT2 transport kinetics with intermediate glucose sensing and insulin secretion dynamics.

AA.phenotype: Altered Glucose Sensing / Higher Sugar Preference
AA.description: Homozygous alternate (Ile110); reduced GLUT2-mediated glucose sensing in pancreas and gut, associated with blunted sweet taste inhibition and higher dietary sugar preference.

description: SLC2A2 encodes GLUT2, a high-capacity facilitative glucose transporter expressed in pancreatic beta cells, hepatocytes, and intestinal epithelium. GLUT2 in taste receptor cells and gut mediates sweet taste signaling and gut glucose sensing that normally suppresses sugar intake. The Thr110Ile (rs5400) missense variant reduces GLUT2 transport efficiency, impairing glucose-triggered satiety signals and increasing sugar preference.

sources.name: Eny KM et al. (2008) - Genetic variant in the glucose transporter type 2 is associated with higher intakes of sugars in two distinct populations

notes: Sugar preference is highly modifiable by dietary habits and food environment. The A allele is associated with higher sugar intake in population studies but does not determine food choices. GLUT2 mutations causing Fanconi-Bickel syndrome are severe loss-of-function events distinct from this common variant.

AA.description: Homozygous HFE C282Y, high risk of iron overload and hemochromatosis requiring monitoring/treatment

AG.description: HFE C282Y carrier, mildly elevated iron stores but typically asymptomatic

GG.description: Wild-type HFE, normal iron absorption and regulation

description: HFE C282Y is the primary cause of hereditary hemochromatosis type 1, causing excessive iron absorption that can damage the liver, heart, and pancreas if untreated.
notes: C282Y homozygosity is present in ~0.5% of Northern Europeans. Penetrance is incomplete (~28% of male and ~1% of female homozygotes develop clinical hemochromatosis). Regular phlebotomy prevents complications.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical CBS-mediated transsulfuration pathway activity, normal homocysteine to cystathionine conversion.

GA.description: Heterozygous; minor variation in CBS splicing, modest effect on transsulfuration capacity with generally normal plasma homocysteine.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; synonymous CBS variant affecting mRNA stability or splicing, associated with modestly altered homocysteine metabolism in some studies.

description: CBS encodes cystathionine beta-synthase, a pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (vitamin B6)-dependent enzyme that converts homocysteine to cystathionine in the transsulfuration pathway. The rs234706 synonymous variant influences CBS mRNA expression and enzyme activity, affecting plasma homocysteine levels. Homocysteine is an independent cardiovascular risk factor when elevated.

sources.name: Tsai MY et al. (2000) - Cystathionine beta-synthase variants and hyperhomocysteinemia

notes: Homocysteine levels are strongly influenced by dietary B6, B12, and folate intake, which can override genetic predisposition. This variant should be interpreted alongside MTHFR genotype and nutritional status. Not diagnostic for hyperhomocysteinemia.

CC.description: Normal PPARgamma activity, standard adipogenesis and insulin sensitivity

CG.description: Mildly reduced PPARgamma activity, improved insulin sensitivity, lower type 2 diabetes risk

GG.description: Reduced PPARgamma activity, better insulin sensitivity, significantly lower type 2 diabetes risk

description: PPARG Pro12Ala variant modulates the master regulator of adipocyte differentiation. The Ala allele slightly reduces receptor activity, paradoxically improving insulin sensitivity and reducing diabetes risk.
notes: Pro12Ala was one of the first validated T2D susceptibility variants. Ala allele frequency ~12% in Europeans. PPARgamma is the target of thiazolidinedione drugs (pioglitazone). Ala reduces T2D risk by ~20%.

AA.description: Higher hepcidin levels from TMPRSS6 variant, reduced intestinal iron absorption, lower iron stores

AG.description: Moderate TMPRSS6 function, intermediate hepcidin and iron regulation

GG.description: Full TMPRSS6/matriptase-2 function, normal hepcidin suppression and iron absorption

description: TMPRSS6 A736V variant affects matriptase-2, a protease that cleaves hemojuvelin to suppress hepcidin (the master iron regulatory hormone). Reduced TMPRSS6 means higher hepcidin and lower iron absorption.
notes: TMPRSS6 is the strongest GWAS hit for serum iron, hemoglobin, and transferrin saturation. The A allele raises hepcidin, reducing iron absorption. Relevant for iron deficiency anemia susceptibility.

CC.description: Lactase production stops after childhood, high likelihood of lactose intolerance (>90%)

CT.description: Lactase persistence, can digest lactose in adulthood

TT.description: Strong lactase persistence, very likely can digest lactose throughout life

description: MCM6 regulatory variant controlling LCT (lactase) gene expression. The -13910 C>T variant enables lactase persistence into adulthood in ~35% of world population, mostly Europeans and some African/Middle Eastern groups.

sources.name: Enattah NS et al. (2002) - Identification of a variant associated with adult-type hypolactasia

notes: The primary lactase persistence variant in European populations (-13910C>T). T allele allows continued milk digestion into adulthood. Different lactase persistence mutations exist in African and Middle Eastern populations. One of the best examples of recent human adaptation.

CC.description: Higher FADS1 activity, efficient conversion of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA/DHA

CT.description: Moderate FADS1 activity, intermediate omega-3/omega-6 conversion efficiency

TT.description: Lower FADS1 activity, less efficient omega-3 conversion, may benefit more from direct fish oil supplementation

description: FADS1 fatty acid desaturase 1 variant affects the conversion efficiency of plant-based omega-3 (ALA) to EPA and DHA, and omega-6 (LA) to arachidonic acid.
notes: FADS1/FADS2 cluster variants show strong signals of recent natural selection. The efficient-desaturation allele was selected for in populations with plant-based diets. Affects blood lipid profiles.

CC.description: Normal BCMO1 activity, efficient conversion of beta-carotene to retinal (vitamin A)

CT.description: ~30% reduced BCMO1 activity, less efficient beta-carotene to vitamin A conversion

TT.description: ~50% reduced BCMO1 activity, significantly less efficient provitamin A conversion, may need preformed vitamin A

description: BCMO1 (BCO1) variant reduces beta-carotene 15,15'-oxygenase activity, the enzyme that cleaves beta-carotene into retinal. Poor converters may not get adequate vitamin A from plant sources alone.
notes: ~45% of the population carries at least one reduced-function allele. Combined with rs7501331, up to 69% reduction in conversion. Important for vegans relying on plant-based vitamin A (beta-carotene).

CG.description: Intermediate effect, moderately elevated postprandial triglyceride response to dietary fat

CC.description: Standard triglyceride metabolism, normal postprandial lipemia

GG.description: Significantly higher triglyceride response to fat intake, increased cardiovascular risk

description: ZPR1/APOA5 variant affects triglyceride metabolism and postprandial lipemia. APOA5 regulates triglyceride-rich lipoprotein clearance by activating lipoprotein lipase.
notes: This locus has one of the largest effects on triglyceride levels from GWAS. Risk allele carriers show ~20% higher fasting triglycerides. APOA5 activates LPL-mediated triglyceride clearance from VLDL and chylomicrons.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Gly258); functional FUT2 produces H-antigen on intestinal mucosa, facilitating haptocorrin-mediated B12 uptake and supporting higher serum B12.

GA.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in intestinal H-antigen expression, with moderately lower B12 absorption efficiency.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate (Ser258); reduced FUT2 activity lowers intestinal H-antigen density, impairing haptocorrin attachment and reducing ileal B12 absorption.

description: FUT2 (secretor gene) encodes alpha(1,2)fucosyltransferase 2, which adds fucose to glycoproteins on intestinal mucosal cells creating H-antigen. H-antigen facilitates the binding of intrinsic factor-B12 complex and haptocorrin during ileal cobalamin absorption. The A allele reduces FUT2 activity, lowering mucosal H-antigen density and B12 absorption efficiency.

sources.name: Hazra A et al. (2008) - Common variants of FUT2 are associated with plasma vitamin B12 levels

notes: FUT2 non-secretor status (A allele) is also associated with reduced susceptibility to norovirus and H. pylori infection, illustrating pleiotropic effects. Dietary B12 or supplementation effectively compensates for lower absorption efficiency. Vegans with the A allele have heightened risk of B12 deficiency.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; baseline pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) plasma levels associated with standard ALPL tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase activity. Most common in African populations (~87% C frequency).

CT.description: Heterozygous; one T allele associated with moderately elevated plasma PLP levels due to partially reduced alkaline phosphatase activity that normally degrades circulating PLP.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with the highest plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate levels. The T allele reduces alkaline phosphatase-mediated PLP catabolism. T allele frequency is ~45-53% globally, with highest frequency in East Asians (~53%).

description: The NBPF3/ALPL locus on chromosome 1 harbors rs4654748, a variant associated with circulating plasma pyridoxal-5'-phosphate (PLP) levels — the active form of vitamin B6. ALPL (tissue-nonspecific alkaline phosphatase) degrades circulating PLP; reduced ALPL activity from the T allele leads to higher plasma B6 levels. Vitamin B6 is essential for over 100 enzymatic reactions including amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and one-carbon metabolism. Genome-wide association studies consistently identify this locus as the top hit for circulating B6 levels.

sources.name: Tanaka T et al. (2013) - Genome-wide association study of plasma B6 levels identifies novel loci in the NBPF3/ALPL region

notes: Strong population stratification: T allele ~49% in Europeans, ~13% in Africans, ~53% in East Asians. Elevated plasma B6 from TT genotype is generally not harmful and may be beneficial for those with marginally low dietary intake. However, very high-dose B6 supplementation (>100 mg/day) can cause peripheral neuropathy regardless of genotype. The clinically actionable insight is that TT individuals may maintain adequate B6 status with lower dietary intake. Discuss supplementation strategies with a healthcare provider.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical SVCT1 transporter expression in intestinal epithelium supporting efficient dietary vitamin C uptake.

CA.description: Heterozygous; moderate reduction in SVCT1 activity, potentially lowering intestinal vitamin C absorption efficiency.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced SLC23A1-encoded transporter function lowers intestinal ascorbate uptake, increasing dietary vitamin C requirements for adequate plasma levels.

description: SLC23A1 encodes sodium-dependent vitamin C transporter 1 (SVCT1), the primary intestinal transporter mediating dietary ascorbate absorption. Variants in SLC23A1 alter transporter expression and function, influencing how efficiently dietary vitamin C is absorbed into circulation. Lower absorption efficiency raises dietary requirements to achieve sufficient plasma ascorbate.

sources.name: Cahill L et al. (2009) - Vitamin C transport gene variants and plasma ascorbic acid

notes: Plasma vitamin C is strongly determined by dietary intake; genetic differences in absorption are most relevant at low-to-moderate intake levels. Smokers have substantially higher vitamin C requirements regardless of genotype. Supplementation effectively compensates for reduced absorption efficiency.

GG.description: Higher vitamin D binding protein, more total 25(OH)D but similar bioavailable vitamin D

GT.description: Moderate vitamin D binding protein levels

TT.description: Lower vitamin D binding protein, lower total 25(OH)D but similar bioavailable vitamin D

description: GC (group-specific component) encodes vitamin D binding protein, the primary carrier of 25(OH)D in blood. Variants affect total vitamin D measurements but may not change bioavailable vitamin D.
notes: GC is the strongest genetic determinant of total serum 25(OH)D. However, if free/bioavailable vitamin D is the active form, GC genotype may be less clinically relevant. The Gc2 allele is more common in darker-skinned populations.

AA.description: Higher vitamin D levels

AC.description: Moderate vitamin D levels

CC.description: Lower vitamin D levels - may need supplementation

description: Vitamin D binding protein variant affecting circulating vitamin D levels.

sources.name: Wang TJ et al. (2010) - Common genetic determinants of vitamin D insufficiency: a genome-wide association study

notes: GC (vitamin D binding protein) variant — one of the strongest genetic determinants of circulating 25(OH)D levels. Clinically relevant: carriers of certain alleles may need higher vitamin D supplementation. Effect is consistent across populations. Vitamin D levels also strongly depend on sun exposure and diet.

AA.description: Reduced CYP2R1 25-hydroxylase activity, lower circulating 25(OH)D levels

AG.description: Moderate CYP2R1 activity, intermediate vitamin D hydroxylation

GG.description: Full CYP2R1 activity, efficient conversion of vitamin D to 25(OH)D

description: CYP2R1 is the major vitamin D 25-hydroxylase that converts vitamin D to its circulating form 25(OH)D. Variants directly affect serum vitamin D levels.
notes: CYP2R1 performs the first hydroxylation step in vitamin D activation. GWAS confirmed it as one of the top genetic determinants of serum 25(OH)D levels, along with GC and DHCR7.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; standard TFAP2B-mediated adipogenesis regulation associated with better long-term weight maintenance after caloric restriction.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate adipocyte differentiation signaling with moderately elevated weight regain tendency after dieting.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; enhanced TFAP2B-driven adipogenesis and fat storage signaling associated with greater weight regain following caloric restriction.

description: TFAP2B encodes transcription factor AP-2 beta, which regulates adipocyte differentiation and lipid metabolism genes. The rs987237 G allele enhances TFAP2B transcriptional activity in adipose tissue, promoting adipogenesis and fat storage. Individuals carrying the G allele show greater tendency to regain adipose mass following dietary weight loss, a major challenge in long-term obesity management.

sources.name: Vogels N et al. (2009) - Genetic variant in TFAP2B is associated with weight maintenance

notes: Weight regain after dieting is influenced by many factors including diet quality, physical activity, sleep, and metabolic adaptation. The G allele suggests a modest but actionable predisposition toward adipogenesis rebound. Dietary composition (higher protein, lower glycemic index) may partially offset this tendency.

CC.description: Full DPD enzyme activity, standard 5-fluorouracil chemotherapy dosing appropriate

CT.description: ~50% DPD activity, increased risk of severe 5-FU toxicity, dose reduction recommended

TT.description: Complete DPD deficiency, 5-fluorouracil can cause life-threatening toxicity including neutropenia and mucositis

description: DPYD IVS14+1G>A (DPYD*2A) splice site variant eliminates dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity, causing potentially fatal toxicity from 5-fluorouracil and capecitabine chemotherapy.
notes: DPYD*2A causes exon 14 skipping. Carrier frequency ~1% in Europeans. DPD degrades >80% of administered 5-FU. Pre-treatment DPYD testing is now recommended/mandated in EU and increasingly in US. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical ABCB1 intronic regulation supports normal P-gp expression at the blood-brain barrier, maintaining efficient CNS drug exclusion.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate ABCB1 expression produces modestly reduced P-gp activity at the BBB, allowing slightly increased CNS penetration of substrate drugs.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced ABCB1 intronic regulatory activity lowers P-gp levels at the blood-brain barrier, significantly increasing CNS exposure to P-gp substrate drugs.

description: This intronic ABCB1 variant influences expression of P-glycoprotein specifically at the blood-brain barrier endothelium. Reduced BBB P-gp activity increases CNS penetration of substrate drugs including antidepressants, antipsychotics, opioids, and chemotherapy agents. Greater CNS penetration can enhance therapeutic efficacy but also increase neurotoxic side-effect risk, depending on the drug.

sources.name: Hoffmeyer S et al. (2000) - Functional polymorphisms of the human multidrug-resistance gene

notes: rs2032583 and rs2032582 are co-inherited in a common ABCB1 haplotype. BBB P-gp function influences CNS drug therapy outcomes for epilepsy, depression, and cancer. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone. Consult a healthcare provider or pharmacist before making any medication decisions.

AA.description: Homozygous Lys262 reference; standard CYP2B6 enzyme activity. Bupropion, efavirenz, and cyclophosphamide are metabolized at expected rates, yielding typical plasma levels and standard therapeutic/toxicity profiles.

AG.description: Heterozygous Lys262Arg; one copy of the Arg262 variant moderately modulates CYP2B6 expression or activity. Intermediate metabolism of bupropion and cyclophosphamide with mild variation in plasma drug concentrations.

GG.description: Homozygous Arg262 variant; both CYP2B6 alleles carry the K262R substitution. Altered metabolism of substrates including bupropion (antidepressant/smoking cessation), efavirenz (HIV antiretroviral), and cyclophosphamide (chemotherapy/immunosuppressant), potentially shifting therapeutic windows.

description: CYP2B6 (cytochrome P450 family 2 subfamily B member 6) is the primary hepatic enzyme responsible for metabolism of bupropion to its active hydroxybupropion metabolite, efavirenz to its major phase I metabolite, and cyclophosphamide to its active alkylating species. The rs2279343 A>G variant encodes the Lys262Arg (K262R) amino acid substitution, which affects CYP2B6 protein expression and catalytic efficiency. This SNP defines part of the CYP2B6*4 haplotype and contributes to the well-documented inter-individual variability in CYP2B6-mediated drug metabolism.

sources.name: Hesse LM et al. (2004) - Influence of CYP2B6 genetic polymorphisms on drug metabolism

notes: G allele frequency is approximately 22–28% in European populations. CYP2B6 is one of the most polymorphic CYP enzymes, with multiple variants (CYP2B6*2 through *38) contributing to a 20-250-fold range of inter-individual activity. Clinical significance is highest for efavirenz (NNRTI for HIV) where poor metabolizers experience dose-related neurotoxicity and virologic response differences. Bupropion smoking-cessation efficacy may also vary by genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: No *4 allele, standard CYP2C19 metabolizer status

AG.description: One *4 allele, reduced CYP2C19 function, may affect drug metabolism

GG.description: Homozygous *4, loss of CYP2C19 function, clopidogrel and PPI dose adjustment needed

description: CYP2C19*4 is a rare loss-of-function allele affecting a splice site. Contributes to poor metabolizer status for clopidogrel, proton pump inhibitors, and antidepressants.
notes: CYP2C19*4 is rare (<1% in most populations) but clinically significant. Together with *2 and *3, accounts for >95% of CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles worldwide. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2C19, severely impaired metabolism of clopidogrel, PPIs, and antidepressants

AG.description: One *3 allele, reduced CYP2C19 activity, consider alternative drugs or dose adjustment

GG.description: No *3 allele at this position, normal CYP2C19 metabolizer status

description: CYP2C19*3 introduces a premature stop codon (W212X), completely eliminating enzyme activity. Most common loss-of-function allele in East Asian populations.
notes: CYP2C19*3 is rare in Europeans (<1%) but found in 5-10% of East Asians. Combined with *2, these are the two most important CYP2C19 loss-of-function alleles for clopidogrel pharmacogenomics. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Standard CYP2C19 metabolizer status, normal drug activation and metabolism

CT.description: Increased CYP2C19 activity, faster metabolism of PPIs and clopidogrel activation

TT.description: Significantly increased CYP2C19 activity, may need higher PPI doses, enhanced clopidogrel response

description: CYP2C19*17 promoter variant increases gene expression, producing ultra-rapid metabolism of proton pump inhibitors, antidepressants, and enhanced clopidogrel activation.
notes: *17 carriers metabolize PPIs too quickly, reducing efficacy. For clopidogrel, *17 enhances activation (beneficial). Frequency ~20-25% in Europeans, 3-5% in East Asians. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Homozygous Ile105 wild-type; GSTP1 enzyme maintains optimal substrate binding and standard detoxification activity toward chemotherapy agents including cyclophosphamide and platinum compounds.

AG.description: Heterozygous for the Val105 variant; intermediate detoxification capacity. Moderate reduction in enzyme thermostability and activity toward electrophilic chemotherapy substrates.

GG.description: Homozygous Val105 variant; substantially reduced GSTP1 enzyme activity and thermal stability. Impaired glutathione conjugation of cyclophosphamide, chlorambucil, and platinum-DNA adducts results in altered chemotherapy efficacy and toxicity profiles.

description: GSTP1 (glutathione S-transferase pi 1) catalyzes the conjugation of glutathione to electrophilic compounds as a Phase II detoxification mechanism. The rs1695 A>G substitution encodes the Ile105Val amino acid change, which destabilizes the H-site hydrophobic drug-binding pocket and reduces catalytic activity toward bulky aromatic substrates. This polymorphism modulates detoxification of many clinical chemotherapeutics — including alkylating agents, platinum compounds, and anthracyclines — altering both therapeutic response and organ toxicity patterns in cancer patients.

sources.name: Harries LW et al. (2001) - The effect of glutathione S-transferase genotype on outcomes in chemotherapy

notes: The G allele (Val105) frequency is approximately 32–38% in European populations and 16–24% in East Asian populations. GG individuals may exhibit altered toxicity profiles when treated with platinum-based regimens (cisplatin, oxaliplatin) or cyclophosphamide. GSTP1 activity reduction can increase oxidative stress burden during chemotherapy. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2C19 (*2/*2), clopidogrel ineffective, high risk of cardiovascular events on standard therapy

AG.description: Reduced CYP2C19 activity, diminished clopidogrel activation, consider alternative antiplatelet therapy

GG.description: Full CYP2C19 activity, normal clopidogrel activation and antiplatelet response

description: CYP2C19*2 is the most common loss-of-function allele, preventing activation of the prodrug clopidogrel. FDA black box warning mandates genotyping consideration before clopidogrel use.
notes: CYP2C19*2 aberrant splice site eliminates enzyme activity. 15-30% of most populations carry at least one *2 allele. FDA black box warning on clopidogrel labels regarding CYP2C19 poor metabolizers. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Reduced drug efflux, higher brain and tissue drug concentrations

AG.description: Moderate drug efflux pump function, intermediate drug levels

GG.description: Increased drug efflux, lower intracellular drug concentrations, potential treatment resistance

description: ABCB1/MDR1 C3435T variant affects P-glycoprotein expression, a key drug efflux transporter at the blood-brain barrier and intestinal epithelium affecting bioavailability of many drugs.
notes: P-glycoprotein transports hundreds of drugs including chemotherapeutics, antivirals, immunosuppressants, and cardiac drugs. The T allele (A) reduces P-gp expression, increasing drug absorption and brain penetration. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme activity, standard drug metabolism

CT.description: Reduced CYP2D6 activity, may need dose adjustments for codeine, antidepressants

TT.description: Significantly reduced CYP2D6 activity, codeine ineffective, altered antidepressant response

description: CYP2D6*10 variant causes reduced enzyme activity affecting metabolism of ~25% of all drugs including codeine, tramadol, and many antidepressants.
notes: CYP2D6 metabolizes approximately 25% of clinically used drugs. The *10 allele (P34S) is especially common in East Asian populations (40-70%). Poor metabolizers cannot convert codeine to morphine. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Gln141/Gln141); full ABCG2 protein function. Standard rosuvastatin and topotecan efflux activity — plasma drug levels fall within expected ranges.

AG.description: One copy of the Lys141 variant (Gln141Lys); ABCG2 surface expression and efflux activity are moderately reduced. Intermediate plasma concentrations of ABCG2 substrates including rosuvastatin are expected.

AA.description: Homozygous Lys141 variant; ABCG2 protein is substantially misfolded and degraded via ERAD, reducing membrane-level efflux. Plasma rosuvastatin concentrations are approximately 1.7-fold higher than in GG individuals, increasing both therapeutic effect and adverse event risk.

description: ABCG2 (ATP-binding cassette subfamily G member 2) is a half-transporter expressed at the intestinal epithelium, liver, kidney, and blood–brain barrier that mediates efflux of xenobiotics into luminal spaces. The rs2231142 G>A variant (Gln141Lys) reduces ABCG2 protein surface expression by approximately 50% through endoplasmic reticulum-associated degradation. Affected substrates include rosuvastatin (statin), topotecan (chemotherapy), methotrexate, sulfasalazine, and multiple tyrosine kinase inhibitors; the A allele is also the primary genetic determinant of gout susceptibility via reduced urate excretion.

sources.name: Morisaki K et al. (2008) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms modify the transporter activity of ABCG2

notes: The A allele (Lys141) reaches ~30% frequency in East Asian populations versus ~6–9% in Europeans. Clinical implications extend beyond rosuvastatin: this variant significantly affects topotecan bioavailability in cancer chemotherapy regimens and oral bioavailability of sulfasalazine for inflammatory bowel disease. Gout associations are driven by reduced renal urate secretion. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Standard CYP2B6 activity, normal efavirenz and methadone metabolism

GT.description: Reduced CYP2B6 activity, slightly elevated drug levels, may need dose adjustment

TT.description: Significantly reduced CYP2B6 activity, high risk of efavirenz CNS toxicity, slow methadone metabolism

description: CYP2B6*6 (Q172H + K262R) is the most common reduced-function allele. Critical for metabolism of efavirenz (HIV), methadone (opioid maintenance), and bupropion (smoking cessation).
notes: CYP2B6*6 frequency ~25% globally, up to 50% in some African populations. TT genotype causes efavirenz plasma levels 3-4x higher than GG, increasing CNS side effects (vivid dreams, dizziness). Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Normal bilirubin glucuronidation, standard irinotecan metabolism

AT.description: Mildly reduced glucuronidation, slightly elevated bilirubin and irinotecan toxicity risk

TT.description: Significantly reduced UGT1A1 activity, elevated bilirubin (Gilbert syndrome), high irinotecan toxicity risk

description: UGT1A1*28 TA repeat variant reduces UGT1A1 expression, causing Gilbert syndrome (benign unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia) and increasing irinotecan chemotherapy toxicity risk.
notes: UGT1A1*28 (7 TA repeats vs 6 in *1) reduces transcription by ~70%. Gilbert syndrome affects 5-10% of population. FDA label for irinotecan recommends UGT1A1 genotyping. *28/*28 patients need 30% dose reduction. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Homozygous reference (Ser893); typical P-glycoprotein activity at intestinal and blood-brain barrier membranes supports standard drug absorption and CNS exclusion.

AT.description: Heterozygous (Ser/Ala); partially reduced P-gp efflux pump efficiency produces intermediate drug absorption and modest increase in CNS drug exposure.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate (Ala893); lower P-glycoprotein pump activity increases oral bioavailability and CNS penetration of substrate drugs including digoxin, cyclosporine, and certain statins.

description: ABCB1 encodes P-glycoprotein (P-gp), an ATP-dependent efflux pump expressed at intestinal epithelium, blood-brain barrier, and kidney tubule cells. P-gp actively expels hundreds of xenobiotics and drugs back into the gut lumen or blood, limiting absorption and CNS entry. The Ser893Ala missense variant alters P-gp transport efficiency, affecting bioavailability and drug clearance for numerous therapeutic agents.

sources.name: Hoffmeyer S et al. (2000) - Functional polymorphisms of the human multidrug-resistance gene: multiple sequence variations and correlation of one allele with P-glycoprotein expression

notes: ABCB1 rs2032582 is in linkage disequilibrium with rs1045642 (C3435T). P-gp activity affects response to anticancer drugs, immunosuppressants, antiepileptics, and HIV antiretrovirals. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone. Consult a healthcare provider or pharmacist before making any medication decisions.

AG.description: One non-functional CYP2D6 allele, reduced metabolism of CYP2D6 substrates

AA.description: Non-functional CYP2D6 (*4/*4), unable to metabolize many common drugs including codeine, tamoxifen

GG.description: Normal CYP2D6 enzyme function, standard drug metabolism

description: CYP2D6*4 is the most common non-functional allele in Europeans, causing a splicing defect that eliminates enzyme activity. Affects metabolism of ~25% of prescription drugs.
notes: CYP2D6*4 (G>A at intron 3/exon 4 boundary) is the most common PM allele in Europeans (20-25%). Homozygotes are poor metabolizers (~7% of Europeans). FDA recommends CYP2D6 testing for multiple drugs. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Impaired hepatic statin uptake, 15-20x increased risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy

CT.description: Reduced hepatic statin uptake, ~5x increased risk of statin myopathy

TT.description: Normal OATP1B1 transporter function, standard statin myopathy risk
