description: SLCO1B1*5 (V174A) reduces hepatic uptake of statins via the OATP1B1 transporter, increasing plasma statin levels and risk of myopathy. FDA-relevant pharmacogenomic marker.
notes: SLCO1B1*5 carrier frequency ~15% in Europeans. The SEARCH trial showed CC genotype had 16.9% vs 0.6% myopathy rate on simvastatin 80mg. CPIC recommends avoiding simvastatin 80mg in C allele carriers. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Full CYP3A5 expression, rapid tacrolimus metabolism, may need higher doses

CT.description: Reduced CYP3A5 expression, intermediate tacrolimus metabolism

TT.description: No CYP3A5 expression (cryptic splice site), slower tacrolimus metabolism, lower doses needed

description: CYP3A5*3 splice variant determines CYP3A5 expression. Critical for transplant immunosuppression dosing — tacrolimus dose requirements differ 1.5-2x between expressors and non-expressors.
notes: *3 creates a cryptic splice site that abolishes CYP3A5 expression. ~90% of Europeans are *3/*3 non-expressors, vs ~30% of Africans. CPIC recommends genotype-guided tacrolimus dosing in transplant patients. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal thiopurine methyltransferase function, standard dosing appropriate

CT.description: Reduced TPMT enzyme stability, dose reduction recommended for thiopurines

TT.description: Severely reduced TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening toxicity at standard thiopurine doses

description: TPMT*3B component (Y240C) causes enzyme instability and rapid degradation. Part of the TPMT*3A haplotype when combined with *3C, the most common deficient allele in Europeans.
notes: TPMT*3A (combination of *3B and *3C) is the most common deficiency allele in Europeans (~5%). The Y240C substitution causes misfolding and rapid proteasomal degradation of the enzyme. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Full TPMT enzyme activity, standard azathioprine/6-MP dosing appropriate

AG.description: Reduced TPMT activity, dose reduction recommended to prevent myelosuppression

GG.description: Very low TPMT activity, high risk of life-threatening myelosuppression at standard doses

description: TPMT*3C variant reduces thiopurine methyltransferase activity. Critical for dosing azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine, and thioguanine to prevent potentially fatal bone marrow toxicity.
notes: TPMT deficiency affects ~0.3% of population (homozygous) and ~10% are intermediate metabolizers. FDA mandates TPMT testing before thiopurine therapy. TPMT*3C is the most common variant in East Asian and African populations. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (GG haplotype H7/H7); VKORC1 is expressed at high levels. Higher warfarin doses are typically required to achieve therapeutic anticoagulation (INR 2–3). Common in African-ancestry individuals.

AG.description: Heterozygous AG; intermediate VKORC1 expression. Moderate warfarin dose required. This genotype is the most common in European populations and requires individualized dose adjustment.

AA.description: Homozygous A allele (haplotype H1/H1); VKORC1 promoter activity is markedly reduced, lowering enzyme expression. Significantly lower warfarin doses needed — typically 30–40% below average. AA individuals face substantially elevated bleeding risk if dosed at standard levels.

description: VKORC1 (vitamin K epoxide reductase complex subunit 1) catalyzes the reduction of vitamin K epoxide back to the active hydroquinone form required for activation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X. The rs9934438 G>A variant is an intronic SNP that tags the H1 VKORC1 haplotype, which is associated with a -1639G>A promoter variant reducing VKORC1 transcription by ~40%. This is one of the two primary genetic determinants of warfarin dose (alongside CYP2C9), and is incorporated into FDA pharmacogenomic dosing guidelines for warfarin, acenocoumarol, and phenprocoumon.

sources.name: Rieder MJ et al. (2005) - Effect of VKORC1 haplotypes on transcriptional regulation and warfarin dose

notes: A allele frequency ranges from ~9% in African populations to ~37% in Europeans to ~93% in East Asians — this extreme population differentiation explains why East Asian patients require substantially lower warfarin doses on average. Algorithms such as IWPC and WarfarinDosing.org incorporate this variant alongside CYP2C9 genotype and clinical factors for precise dosing. Also affects sensitivity to acenocoumarol and phenprocoumon (coumarin anticoagulants). This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider before making medical decisions. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Low VKORC1 sensitivity, requires higher warfarin doses (5-7 mg/day typical)

CT.description: Moderate VKORC1 sensitivity, intermediate warfarin dose requirement (3-5 mg/day typical)

TT.description: High VKORC1 sensitivity, requires lower warfarin doses (1.5-3 mg/day typical), higher bleeding risk at standard doses

description: VKORC1 -1639G>A promoter variant is the single strongest predictor of warfarin dose requirement, accounting for ~25% of dose variability. Affects vitamin K epoxide reductase expression.
notes: VKORC1 genotype is the strongest single predictor of warfarin dose. The T allele reduces VKORC1 expression, making patients more sensitive to warfarin. Very common in East Asian populations (>90% TT). Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

CC.description: Normal CYP2C9*1 activity, standard warfarin dosing

CT.description: Intermediate CYP2C9 activity (~60%), may require lower warfarin dose

TT.description: Poor CYP2C9 activity (~30%), significantly lower warfarin dose needed

description: CYP2C9*2 variant (R144C) reduces warfarin metabolism. Combined with CYP2C9*3 and VKORC1 status, guides pharmacogenomic anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*2 reduces enzyme activity by ~30-40%. Common in European populations (10-15%). Together with *3 and VKORC1 genotype, accounts for ~40% of warfarin dose variability. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

AA.description: Normal CYP2C9 enzyme activity, standard warfarin dosing appropriate

AC.description: Reduced CYP2C9 activity (~50%), lower warfarin dose may be needed

CC.description: Significantly reduced CYP2C9 activity, substantially lower warfarin dose required

description: CYP2C9*3 variant (I359L) causes significantly reduced metabolism of warfarin and other CYP2C9 substrates. Critical pharmacogenomic marker for anticoagulant dosing.
notes: CYP2C9*3 allele reduces enzyme activity by ~80%. Combined with VKORC1 genotyping, enables pharmacogenomic warfarin dosing. FDA-recommended testing before warfarin initiation. Do not adjust medications based on this result alone.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; standard lysosomal trafficking supports normal melanosome maturation and eumelanin output, yielding darker blond or brown shades.

GA.description: Heterozygous; partial shift in melanosome biogenesis associated with medium-blond coloring.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; altered lysosomal trafficking reduces eumelanin loading in melanosomes, contributing to lighter blond hair color.

description: LYST (lysosomal trafficking regulator) governs vesicle fusion and melanosome biogenesis. Subtle variants in LYST modulate melanosome size and melanin content, contributing to variation in blond hair intensity along the spectrum from platinum to dark blond.

sources.name: Jacobs LC et al. (2015) - Genetics of skin color variation in Europeans

notes: Hair color is highly polygenic; this variant contributes a modest fraction of blond intensity variance. Effect replicated in European GWAS. Childhood hair color often lightens and then darkens again with age, modifying observed phenotype.

CC.description: Smooth chin

CT.description: Mild chin cleft

TT.description: Prominent cleft chin

description: Variation in jaw bone fusion creating a visible Y-shaped dimple in the chin.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with cleft chin morphology in consumer genetics studies. Limited peer-reviewed evidence. Facial morphology is highly polygenic and influenced by developmental environment. Treat prediction with caution.

TT.description: Free (detached) earlobes

TC.description: Partially attached earlobes

CC.description: Attached earlobes

description: Ectodysplasin receptor variant influencing earlobe attachment pattern.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2015) - A genome-wide association study identifies multiple loci for variation in human ear morphology

notes: EDAR region variant associated with earlobe attachment in multi-ethnic GWAS. Earlobe morphology is polygenic — not the simple dominant/recessive trait described in textbooks. Multiple loci contribute to the continuous spectrum of earlobe attachment.

AA.description: No cheek dimples

AG.description: Likely to have dimples on one or both cheeks

GG.description: Strong tendency for cheek dimples

description: Facial muscle variation creating visible indentations in cheeks when smiling.

sources.name: Shaffer JR et al. (2016) - Genome-wide association study reveals multiple loci influencing normal human facial morphology

notes: Weakly associated with cheek dimples in consumer genetics datasets. Evidence is limited to proprietary GWAS from direct-to-consumer companies; no peer-reviewed replication in independent cohorts. Dimple genetics remain poorly understood.

GG.description: Very high likelihood of brown eyes (>95%)

AG.description: Intermediate pigmentation, likely green or hazel eyes (70-80%)

AA.description: Very high likelihood of blue eyes (>90%)

description: Primary determinant of eye color variation in Europeans. This SNP in the HERC2 gene regulates OCA2 expression, controlling melanin production in the iris.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene determines human blue-brown eye color

notes: Strongest single genetic predictor of eye color in Europeans, explaining ~75% of blue vs brown variation. Effect is weaker in non-European populations where brown eyes are nearly universal. Works in concert with OCA2 and other pigmentation loci.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, contributes to darker eye color

CT.description: Slightly reduced melanin, may lighten eye color

TT.description: Reduced melanin production, contributes to lighter eye colors

description: OCA2 gene variant that modifies eye color by affecting melanin production. Works in combination with rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Modifier of eye color that works alongside rs12913832 (HERC2). Most impactful in individuals with intermediate pigmentation — can shift hazel eyes toward blue or green. Effect size is moderate compared to the primary HERC2 variant.

GG.description: Higher melanin, contributes to darker eye colors

TT.description: Lower melanin, contributes to lighter eye colors (especially green)

description: SLC24A4 gene variant associated with green eye color variation. Secondary modifier of eye pigmentation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Contributing locus for eye color identified in European GWAS. Has a smaller effect than HERC2/OCA2 variants but adds predictive power in multi-SNP models. Also weakly associated with hair color variation.

AA.description: Contributes to darker eye pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate eye pigmentation effect

GG.description: Contributes to lighter eye colors (blue/green)

description: Additional OCA2 region variant modifying eye color. Secondary modifier with smaller effect than rs12913832.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Part of a three-SNP OCA2 haplotype associated with blue/brown eye color. Most studied in European-descent populations. Works additively with HERC2 rs12913832 to fine-tune iris pigmentation.

AA.description: Reduced tyrosinase activity, contributes to lighter/bluer eye color

AG.description: Intermediate tyrosinase function, subtle influence toward lighter eye color

GG.description: Normal tyrosinase activity at this position, standard melanin contribution to eye color

description: TYR (tyrosinase) variant modifies eye color by affecting the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin biosynthesis. Contributes to the blue eye phenotype in combination with HERC2/OCA2.
notes: Tyrosinase catalyzes the first two steps of melanin synthesis. This variant is one of several TYR polymorphisms affecting pigmentation. Also associated with freckling and tanning response.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; TYRP1 activity is standard. Brown pigmentation expression is at baseline levels without the A-allele modifier effect.

CA.description: One copy of the A allele provides partial TYRP1 activity modulation. Eye color is influenced in an additive direction toward deeper or lighter brown depending on other pigmentation loci.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; both copies carry the A allele associated with modified TYRP1 enzymatic activity. In European populations, the A allele reaches ~66% frequency, influencing brown-eye shade and saturation.

description: TYRP1 (tyrosinase-related protein 1) encodes an enzyme involved in the eumelanin synthesis pathway, oxidizing 5,6-dihydroxyindole-2-carboxylic acid (DHICA) during melanin production. The rs683 variant modulates TYRP1 activity and acts as a modifier of brown eye color intensity, particularly in concert with OCA2 and HERC2 variants. Population studies demonstrate strong European enrichment of the A allele (~66%), with very low frequency in East Asians (~0.4%).

sources.name: Kayser M et al. (2008) - Three genome-wide association studies and a linkage analysis identify HERC2 as a human iris color gene

notes: The A allele frequency varies dramatically across ancestries: ~66% in Europeans, ~0.4% in East Asians, ~18% in Africans. This extreme population stratification means eye color predictions using this variant are most informative in European-ancestry individuals. Pronounced population-frequency differences make this a useful ancestry-informative marker. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic trait only.

AA.description: Modified OCA2 expression favoring green eye color, particularly in light-eyed individuals

AG.description: Intermediate OCA2 modification, subtle influence toward green eye tones

GG.description: Standard OCA2 expression at this position, baseline eye color contribution

description: OCA2 regulatory variant that modifies eye color, particularly associated with green eye color phenotype. Works as part of the multi-gene eye color determination system.
notes: Green eye color is genetically complex, involving multiple loci. This OCA2 variant contributes to the green eye phenotype alongside HERC2, IRF4, and other pigmentation genes. Green eyes are found in ~2% of the global population.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; minimal disruption to OCA2 regulation, producing uniform iris pigmentation in most individuals.

TC.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in OCA2 regulatory signal, modest increase in likelihood of sectoral iris color variation.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; strongest reduction in HERC2-mediated OCA2 regulation, increasing risk of sectoral or complete heterochromia.

description: HERC2 contains a regulatory element in intron 86 that controls OCA2 expression in melanocytes. Variants here modulate the distribution of melanin across iris sectors, contributing to heterochromia iridis. Effect is additive and interacts with other OCA2/HERC2 variants.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene

notes: Heterochromia has multiple causes including somatic mosaicism; this variant represents a germline predisposition modifier. Effect size for heterochromia specifically is modest. Most studied in European-ancestry cohorts.

AA.description: Reduced OCA2/HERC2 expression, lighter iris pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate melanin in iris, intermediate eye color intensity

GG.description: Higher OCA2 expression, more intense iris pigmentation

description: OCA2/HERC2 regulatory variant affecting melanin density in the iris. Part of the multi-SNP eye color determination system on chromosome 15.
notes: Works in concert with rs12913832 (HERC2) to determine overall eye color intensity. The OCA2/HERC2 region on 15q contains the strongest eye color determinants.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; this HERC2 regulatory region allele is associated with reduced OCA2 promoter activity, favoring lighter eye colors. European populations show ~78% A allele frequency.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate effect on HERC2-mediated OCA2 regulation. Eye color is likely hazel, green, or light brown depending on compound genotype across other pigmentation loci.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; the G allele is enriched in African (~63-70%) and East Asian (~69-73%) populations and is associated with higher OCA2 expression and darker iris pigmentation.

description: rs2238289 lies within the HERC2 gene (chromosome 15) and functions as a cis-regulatory variant modulating the promoter activity of the adjacent OCA2 (P protein) gene, which encodes a melanosomal transporter critical for melanin synthesis. Multiple HERC2 intronic variants, including rs2238289, form haplotypes that finely tune OCA2 expression levels in melanocytes. The A allele predominates in Europeans (~78%) while the G allele predominates in African and East Asian populations.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region within intron 86 of the HERC2 gene determines human blue-brown eye color

notes: This variant works synergistically with rs12913832 (the canonical HERC2 eye color SNP). European populations show very high A allele frequency (~78%), while African and East Asian populations predominantly carry G (~63-73%). Eye color predictions are most accurate when a multi-SNP panel spanning HERC2, OCA2, and other loci is used. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic pigmentation trait.

AA.description: Thinner eyebrows

AG.description: Medium eyebrow thickness

GG.description: Thicker/bushier eyebrows

description: Forkhead transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair density and thickness.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Associated with eyebrow thickness in admixed Latin American populations. Effect may vary across ethnic groups. Hair morphology traits are polygenic with substantial environmental influence (grooming habits).

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard SLC45A2-mediated melanin production and UV response, typical age-related skin appearance trajectory.

TC.description: Heterozygous; modest shift in pigmentation and skin characteristics associated with slightly younger perceived facial age in some cohorts.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; altered SLC45A2 activity linked to melanin distribution patterns associated with younger perceived facial appearance.

description: SLC45A2 encodes a melanocyte-specific transporter essential for melanosome function and pigmentation. Variants in this gene influence melanin distribution in skin, which affects UV photoprotection and perceived skin aging. Pigmentation patterns contribute to how facial age is perceived by observers.

sources.name: Liu F et al. (2016) - Genetics of skin aging

notes: Perceived facial age is influenced by many lifestyle factors (sun exposure, smoking, BMI) that substantially outweigh genetic effects. This genetic association was identified in European-ancestry cohorts and may not generalize across all populations.

TT.description: Fewer freckles

GG.description: Dense freckling tendency

description: Interferon regulatory factor variant influencing melanocyte-driven freckle formation.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: IRF4 regulatory variant associated with freckle density and sun sensitivity in European populations. Pleiotropic effects include contributions to hair color and melanoma risk. Effect size is moderate; freckling is influenced by many loci plus UV exposure.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; baseline melanocyte density and sun-responsiveness, low tendency for distributed ephelides.

TC.description: Heterozygous; mildly enhanced melanocyte proliferative response to UV, producing scattered freckling especially with sun exposure.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; greater KITLG signaling boosts melanocyte activation under UV, contributing to widespread freckle distribution.

description: KITLG (stem cell factor / KIT ligand) regulates melanocyte survival, migration, and proliferation via the KIT receptor. Variants in the KITLG region influence melanocyte density and UV-triggered pigmentation responses, affecting how freckles (ephelides) form and distribute across skin.

sources.name: Jacobs LC et al. (2015) - Genetics of skin color variation in Europeans: genome-wide association studies with functional follow-up

notes: Freckling is strongly UV-dependent; genetic predisposition manifests most with childhood sun exposure. Effect replicated in multiple European cohorts. Less studied in non-European populations. Freckle tendency is not a measure of skin cancer risk in isolation.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, lower freckling tendency

GT.description: Increased freckling tendency, especially with sun exposure

TT.description: Strong freckling tendency, many freckles likely

description: MC1R variant (D84E) strongly associated with freckling, even in non-redheads. Also associated with fair skin and sun sensitivity.

sources.name: Bastiaens M et al. (2001) - Association of the melanocortin-1 receptor gene with freckling and solar lentigines in children

notes: MC1R variant (V60L) associated with freckling and fair skin but rarely causes red hair alone. One of the milder MC1R variants. Freckling tendency is polygenic — MC1R explains the largest fraction but sun exposure is a major environmental modifier.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; higher SLC24A4 activity supports stronger eumelanin deposition, associated with darker blond or brown hair.

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate melanin production yields medium-blond to light-brown hair tone.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced eumelanin deposition associated with lighter blond hair, particularly in childhood.

description: SLC24A4 encodes a cation exchanger active in melanosomes that regulates calcium and potassium balance during pigment synthesis. The A allele at rs2402130 reduces melanosomal eumelanin output, shifting hair color toward lighter blond shades. Effect is strongest in individuals of European ancestry.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: This variant is most informative in European-ancestry populations where hair color GWAS were conducted. A allele frequency is approximately 30-40% in Europeans. Hair color darkens with age in many individuals regardless of genotype.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; normal TPCN2 function supports standard melanosomal acidification and eumelanin production, favoring darker hair.

AG.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in TPCN2-mediated melanin modulation, associated with medium-brown to light-brown hair.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; altered melanosomal ion channel activity reduces eumelanin output, contributing to lighter brown or dark-blond hair.

description: TPCN2 encodes a two-pore segment channel involved in lysosomal/melanosomal calcium and pH regulation. Variants affecting TPCN2 function alter the acidic environment of melanosomes required for optimal tyrosinase activity, shifting the eumelanin/phaeomelanin ratio and resulting in lighter hair color.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: TPCN2 variants have a modest but replicated effect on hair color in European populations. G allele frequency is approximately 25-35% in Europeans. Hair color is polygenic; TPCN2 explains only a fraction of variance.

CC.description: Higher melanin production, darker hair color

CT.description: Intermediate melanin, light brown or dark blond hair

TT.description: Reduced melanin, increased likelihood of blond hair in Europeans

description: KITLG gene variant strongly associated with blond hair in European populations. Each T allele increases blond hair likelihood.

sources.name: Sulem et al. (2008) - Genetic determinants of hair, eye and skin pigmentation in Europeans

notes: Associated with blond hair in Europeans via regulation of KITLG expression in hair follicles. Effect is population-specific — rare outside European-descent populations. Explains only a fraction of blond hair variation; many other loci contribute.

CC.description: Normal MC1R function, no red hair from this variant

CT.description: Carrier of red hair variant, may have subtle red tints or increased freckling

TT.description: High likelihood of red or strawberry blonde hair (80-90%)

description: Most common MC1R variant associated with red hair. Loss of function mutation in melanocortin receptor, shifting eumelanin to pheomelanin production.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: One of the strongest MC1R red hair variants (R151C). Highly penetrant in homozygotes — ~80% chance of red hair. Also increases melanoma risk independently of hair color. Most common in Northern European populations (Ireland, Scotland).

CC.description: Normal MC1R function

CT.description: Carrier status, possible subtle red tones

TT.description: High likelihood of red or auburn hair (75-85%)

description: Another common MC1R red hair variant (R160W mutation). Often found in combination with other MC1R variants.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (R160W) associated with red hair, though less penetrant than R151C. Compound heterozygosity with other MC1R variants increases red hair probability. Also linked to increased sun sensitivity and freckling.

AA.description: Straight hair tendency

TT.description: Curly hair tendency

description: Trichohyalin protein variant affecting hair fiber shape and curliness.

sources.name: Medland SE et al. (2009) - Common variants in the trichohyalin gene are associated with straight hair in Europeans

notes: TCHH variant strongly associated with straight vs curly hair in Europeans. Effect is specific to European populations where the variant is common (~10% frequency). Hair curliness is highly polygenic — this SNP captures only part of the variation.

TT.description: Typical European/African hair characteristics, thinner hair strands

CT.description: Thicker hair strands, straighter hair

CC.description: Very thick hair strands, very straight hair (common in East Asians)

description: EDAR gene variant (370A allele) prevalent in East Asian populations. Affects hair follicle development, leading to thicker, straighter hair shafts.

sources.name: Fujimoto A et al. (2008) - A scan for genetic determinants of human hair morphology: EDAR is associated with Asian hair thickness

notes: EDAR V370A variant at near-fixation in East Asian and Native American populations but rare elsewhere. Affects hair thickness, tooth morphology, sweat gland density, and breast tissue. One of the strongest examples of recent positive selection in humans.

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker hair and skin

CT.description: Intermediate pigmentation levels

TT.description: Reduced melanin, lighter hair (especially blond/red) and skin, more freckling

description: IRF4 transcription factor variant affecting melanocyte function. Associated with lighter hair, skin, and increased freckling.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: IRF4 variant with pleiotropic effects on skin, hair, and eye pigmentation. Strongly associated with freckling, sun sensitivity, and lighter hair color in Europeans. Also linked to melanoma susceptibility independently of pigmentation.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.1cm to head circumference on average

CT.description: Intermediate head circumference contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced head circumference

description: HMGA2 variant associated with head circumference variation. Same gene region affects overall height. Small individual effect.

sources.name: Ikram MA et al. (2012) - Common variants at 6q22 and 17q21 are associated with intracranial volume

notes: Associated with intracranial volume and head circumference in large GWAS meta-analyses. Effect size is small. Head circumference is highly polygenic and influenced by nutrition and prenatal environment. Not clinically predictive for any individual.

TT.description: Each T allele adds ~0.4cm to height on average

CT.description: Intermediate height contribution

CC.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: HMGA2 gene variant, one of the strongest single-SNP associations with adult height. However, height is polygenic with 700+ variants.

sources.name: Weedon MN et al. (2007) - A common variant of HMGA2 is associated with adult and childhood height in the general population

notes: HMGA2 variant associated with height variation, though effect size is small (~0.3-0.4 cm per allele). Height is extremely polygenic (thousands of variants). This is one of the more replicated individual loci but explains <0.1% of height variation.

AA.description: Minor positive contribution to height (~0.3cm per allele)

AG.description: Intermediate height contribution

GG.description: Neutral or slightly reduced height contribution

description: GDF5 growth factor variant associated with height and osteoarthritis. Small effect size as height is highly polygenic.

sources.name: Sanna S et al. (2008) - Common variants in the GDF5-UQCC region are associated with variation in human height

notes: GDF5 variant associated with height and osteoarthritis risk. Small effect on height (~0.2 cm per allele). Also implicated in joint development and skeletal morphology. Height prediction requires hundreds of SNPs for meaningful accuracy.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; lower OCA2 expression contributes to reduced melanin deposition and less complex iris stromal architecture.

AC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate OCA2-driven pigmentation with moderate variation in crypts and furrows across the iris.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; increased OCA2 activity supports richer melanin layering and more elaborate iris stromal patterns.

description: OCA2 encodes the P-protein, a melanosomal transporter critical for eumelanin synthesis and melanosome maturation. Variants at rs4778241 influence OCA2 transcript levels, modulating pigment density in the iris stroma. Higher melanin content correlates with greater apparent iris textural complexity.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2007) - A three-single-nucleotide polymorphism haplotype in intron 1 of OCA2 explains most human eye-color variation

notes: Iris pattern complexity (crypts, furrows, collarette structure) has multiple determinants including stromal fiber arrangement. OCA2 variants primarily drive pigment-related complexity. Most research conducted in European-ancestry cohorts.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; SLC45A2 Glu272 variant associated with normal/higher melanin production. Most common in African (~95%) and South Asian (~80%) populations.

CT.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the derived T allele (Lys272) associated with intermediate SLC45A2 activity. Pigmentation level is intermediate between darker and lighter extremes.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; the T allele (p.Glu272Lys) is strongly enriched in East Asian populations (~36-39%) and associated with lighter skin pigmentation adaptation. Lower frequency in Europeans (~1-2%) and Africans (~5%).

description: SLC45A2 (membrane-associated transporter protein, MATP) is a melanosomal transporter involved in melanin synthesis regulation. The rs26722 variant encodes a missense change (Glu272Lys) that modulates transporter function and melanin output. Unlike the European skin-lightening allele at SLC24A5, the SLC45A2 rs26722 T allele shows highest frequency in East Asian populations (~37%), reflecting a distinct adaptive pigmentation lineage. The variant exemplifies convergent evolution of reduced pigmentation across geographically separated populations.

sources.name: Graf J et al. (2007) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms in the MATP gene are associated with normal human pigmentation variation

notes: Strong population stratification: T allele ~37% in East Asians and Koreans, ~1-2% in Europeans, ~5% in Africans. This suggests the light-skin adaptation in East Asian populations used SLC45A2 as one mechanism, while European populations primarily used SLC24A5 rs1426654 — a classic case of convergent evolution. Not clinically actionable; cosmetic pigmentation trait. Ancestry-informative for East Asian vs. other populations.

AA.description: Increased androgen receptor sensitivity, higher risk of male pattern baldness

AG.description: Intermediate risk (only in females, males have one copy on X chromosome)

GG.description: Reduced androgen receptor sensitivity, lower baldness risk

description: Androgen receptor gene variant on X chromosome. Males inherit from mother. Affects sensitivity to DHT, primary driver of male pattern baldness.

sources.name: Hillmer AM et al. (2005) - Genetic variation in the human androgen receptor gene is the major determinant of common early-onset androgenetic alopecia

notes: Androgen receptor variant associated with male pattern baldness risk. One of multiple contributing loci — androgenetic alopecia is polygenic. The X-linked pattern means maternal family history is more informative. Environmental factors (stress, nutrition) also play a role.

AA.description: Lower baldness risk

AC.description: Moderate baldness risk

CC.description: Higher male pattern baldness risk

description: Histone deacetylase variant associated with androgenetic alopecia susceptibility.

sources.name: Richards JB et al. (2008) - Male-pattern baldness susceptibility locus at 20p11

notes: Associated with androgenetic alopecia at the 20p11 locus. Effect is moderate — male pattern baldness involves multiple loci including AR (X-linked) and this autosomal variant. Prediction accuracy improves substantially with multi-SNP models.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; standard TYR enzyme activity. No increased risk for oculocutaneous albinism type 1 (OCA1). Most common genotype globally (~75-87% depending on ancestry).

TC.description: Heterozygous carrier of the C allele, which encodes a tyrosinase variant with reduced but not absent activity. Carriers typically display normal or slightly lighter pigmentation without clinical albinism.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; substantially reduced TYR enzymatic activity. In combination with other TYR variants, homozygous CC is associated with oculocutaneous albinism type 1 (OCA1). The C allele frequency is ~22% in African populations and ~5-7% in Europeans.

description: TYR encodes tyrosinase, the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin biosynthesis, catalyzing the oxidation of L-tyrosine to DOPA and DOPA-quinone. The rs2240203 variant results in an amino acid change that reduces tyrosinase enzymatic activity in a dose-dependent manner. Compound heterozygosity involving this and other TYR pathogenic variants underlies many OCA1 cases. This SNP is classified as a hypomorphic allele rather than a fully null mutation.

sources.name: Hutton SM & Spritz RA (2008) - Comprehensive analysis of oculocutaneous albinism among non-Hispanic caucasians shows that OCA1 is the most prevalent OCA type

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. A CC genotype alone is not sufficient to diagnose OCA1 — clinical albinism typically requires compound heterozygosity with a second pathogenic TYR variant. Discuss with a healthcare provider or genetic counselor if concerned. Allele frequencies show strong population stratification: C allele ~22% in Africans vs ~5-7% in Europeans. The C allele is a hypomorphic variant, not a null allele, and its clinical significance depends heavily on the compound genotype.

CC.phenotype: No Red Hair from This Variant
CC.description: Normal MC1R signaling at this position, eumelanin production unaffected

CT.description: One copy of D84E, slight shift toward pheomelanin, may lighten hair color

TT.description: Homozygous D84E, reduced eumelanin switching, contributes to red hair and fair skin phenotype

description: MC1R D84E is a loss-of-function variant reducing melanocortin-1 receptor signaling, shifting melanin production from eumelanin (brown/black) to pheomelanin (red/yellow).
notes: MC1R D84E is one of several MC1R variants contributing to red hair phenotype. Usually requires compound heterozygosity with another MC1R variant (R151C, R160W, D294H) for full red hair expression.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Arg163); fully functional MC1R promotes eumelanin synthesis, associated with brown or black hair in most backgrounds.

GA.description: Heterozygous (Arg163/Gln163); one reduced-function MC1R allele partially shifts pigment toward phaeomelanin, may produce auburn, strawberry-blond, or reddish highlights.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate (Gln163/Gln163); strong shift toward phaeomelanin synthesis, major contributor to red or auburn hair coloring.

description: MC1R encodes the melanocortin 1 receptor, the master switch between eumelanin (black/brown) and phaeomelanin (red/yellow). The R163Q variant (Arg163Gln) reduces MC1R signal transduction, shifting melanocytes toward phaeomelanin production. It is one of the strongest single-locus contributors to red hair in Europeans.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variants including R163Q are most prevalent in Celtic and Northern European populations. Red hair expression requires compound heterozygosity or homozygosity at MC1R. MC1R loss-of-function also increases UV sensitivity and skin cancer risk independently of hair color.

AA.description: Reduced melanin production, light skin pigmentation (European-type)

AG.description: Intermediate melanin levels, medium skin tone

GG.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: Major determinant of light skin in European and South Asian populations. The A allele (derived) reduces melanin ~30-40%.

sources.name: Lamason RL et al. (2005) - SLC24A5, a putative cation exchanger, affects pigmentation in zebrafish and humans

notes: Major determinant of light skin in Europeans and South Asians. The derived A allele is nearly fixed in European populations and at high frequency in South Asia. One of the strongest known examples of convergent evolution for light skin pigmentation.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; associated with lower propensity for freckling and lighter pigmentation saturation in sun-exposed skin. Most common in European populations (~34% G allele frequency).

GA.description: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate freckling and pigmentation saturation. The A allele at ~66% global frequency reflects derived European enrichment for modified BNC2 regulation.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with increased tendency to develop freckles and higher skin pigmentation saturation in UV-exposed areas. The A allele predominates in European (~66%) populations.

description: BNC2 (basonuclin 2) encodes a zinc-finger transcription factor expressed in keratinocytes and melanocytes. Genome-wide association studies have identified BNC2 variants, including rs10756819, as associated with freckling propensity and pigmentation saturation traits. The mechanism involves BNC2 regulation of melanocyte differentiation and UV-response gene networks. The A allele shows notable enrichment in European populations (~66%) relative to African populations (~16%).

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Significant population stratification: A allele ~66% in Europeans, ~16% in Africans, ~54% in East Asians. The freckling association is strongest in individuals of Northern European descent who have lower baseline melanin. Freckling reflects a UV-response phenotype and is considered a surrogate marker of cumulative sun damage risk. Not a direct melanoma predictor; UV protection habits remain the primary preventive measure.

AA.description: Lower melanin production, lighter skin pigmentation

AG.description: Moderate melanin, intermediate skin tone

GG.description: Higher melanin production, darker skin pigmentation

description: SLC45A2 L374F variant strongly associated with skin pigmentation differences across populations.
notes: L374F variant in SLC45A2 melanin transporter gene. Major effect in European populations. The A allele frequency is >95% in Europeans, <5% in sub-Saharan Africans.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; reduced UV-induced eumelanin upregulation, associated with minimal tanning and higher burn tendency.

CA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate melanin production response to UV, yielding moderate tanning in most individuals.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; stronger UV-stimulated eumelanin synthesis, supporting a pronounced and lasting tan.

description: Tanning ability reflects the capacity to upregulate eumelanin synthesis in response to UV radiation. This intergenic variant near pigmentation-pathway loci modulates constitutive and facultative pigmentation. Tanning is a protective photoresponse reducing UV-induced DNA damage in keratinocytes.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: Tanning ability is one of many factors in UV skin response; baseline skin tone, MC1R genotype, and sun exposure history all modulate the outcome. Most GWAS data from European-ancestry cohorts. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Reduced melanin, lighter skin and eyes (European-type)

CG.description: Moderate melanin levels

CC.description: Normal melanin production, darker skin and eyes

description: SLC45A2 variant affecting melanin synthesis. The G allele is nearly fixed in Europeans and contributes to light skin and eyes.

sources.name: Graf J et al. (2005) - Single nucleotide polymorphisms in the MATP gene are associated with normal human pigmentation variation

notes: SLC45A2 variant strongly associated with light skin and eye color in Europeans. Nearly fixed for the derived allele in Europeans but rare in African and East Asian populations. Has a stronger effect on skin color than eye color.

CC.description: Separated eyebrows

CT.description: Some eyebrow connectivity

TT.description: Tendency for connected eyebrows (unibrow)

description: Transcription factor variant influencing eyebrow hair growth pattern and density.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: PAX3 variant associated with unibrow (synophrys) tendency, identified in a Latin American admixed population GWAS. Effect may differ across populations. Eyebrow morphology is influenced by multiple genetic and hormonal factors.

GG.description: Straight hairline

GA.description: Subtle V-shaped hairline

AA.description: Distinct widow's peak hairline

description: Characteristic V-shaped point in the center of the hairline.

sources.name: Adhikari K et al. (2016) - A genome-wide association scan in admixed Latin Americans identifies loci influencing facial and scalp hair features

notes: Linked to widow's peak hairline in facial morphology GWAS. The genetic architecture of hairline shape is complex and not well characterized. Limited evidence for this specific SNP-trait association.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; normal SRD5A2 enzymatic activity converts testosterone to DHT at typical rates, supporting standard androgenic effects in target tissues.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderately altered SRD5A2 activity produces intermediate DHT levels in prostate, skin, and hair follicles.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; modified enzyme activity changes DHT production rate, potentially affecting prostate growth, scalp hair follicle miniaturization, and male pattern baldness.

description: SRD5A2 encodes steroid 5-alpha reductase type 2, the enzyme that irreversibly converts testosterone to dihydrotestosterone (DHT) in prostate, skin, and hair follicles. DHT is a more potent androgen receptor agonist than testosterone. Variants influencing SRD5A2 activity affect DHT-driven processes including prostate size, male pattern baldness, and acne severity.

sources.name: Makridakis N et al. (1997) - A common missense mutation in SRD5A2 and its relationship to prostate cancer risk

notes: SRD5A2 is the pharmacological target of finasteride (Propecia/Proscar). Variants in this gene have been associated with differential prostate cancer risk across ancestries. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

AA.description: LIN28B variant associated with later onset of puberty and menarche by several months

AC.description: Intermediate LIN28B effect on pubertal timing

CC.description: Standard LIN28B expression, tendency toward earlier menarche onset

description: LIN28B variant affects pubertal timing through regulation of the let-7 microRNA family. LIN28B controls the transition from juvenile to adult gene expression programs.
notes: LIN28B was among the first GWAS hits for menarche timing. Each allele shifts menarche by ~0.1-0.2 years. LIN28B also affects adult height through its role in growth tempo regulation.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; baseline CYP17A1 transcriptional activity supports normal DHEA and androstenedione production in the adrenal glands and gonads.

AG.description: Heterozygous; increased CYP17A1 5' UTR activity modestly raises 17α-hydroxylase and 17,20-lyase activity, elevating androgen precursor output.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; highest CYP17A1 promoter activity drives elevated DHEA, androstenedione, and downstream testosterone biosynthesis.

description: CYP17A1 encodes the bifunctional enzyme 17α-hydroxylase/17,20-lyase, catalyzing key steps in glucocorticoid and androgen synthesis in adrenal and gonadal tissue. The 5' UTR variant modifies transcriptional activity, affecting the balance between cortisol and androgen precursor production. Higher androgen synthesis is associated with androgenic traits, acne, and sex-hormone dependent cancer risk.

sources.name: Carey AH et al. (1999) - Polycystic ovaries and the risk of cardiovascular disease

notes: CYP17A1 rs743572 has been studied in relation to polycystic ovary syndrome, prostate cancer risk, and hormone-dependent breast cancer. Effect is modulated by body composition and insulin levels. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

AA.description: Increased aromatase activity, higher estradiol production from androgens

AG.description: Moderate aromatase activity, intermediate estrogen synthesis

GG.description: Reduced aromatase activity, lower estrogen conversion from androgens

description: CYP19A1 (aromatase) variant affects the enzyme that converts androgens to estrogens. Critical for reproductive function, bone health, and breast cancer risk.
notes: Aromatase inhibitors (letrozole, anastrozole) are first-line endocrine therapy for ER+ breast cancer. CYP19A1 variants may affect response to these drugs and natural estrogen levels.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; baseline CYP19A1 (aromatase) expression and estrogen synthesis from androgens. Standard circulating estradiol and estrone levels in both sexes (~51% G globally).

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate CYP19A1 expression level. Estrogen levels are between GG and AA extremes. May influence estrogen-dependent phenotypes such as bone mineral density and menopausal symptom severity.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; A allele (~49% globally, ~51% European) associated with modified CYP19A1 expression and altered estradiol/estrone ratios. Associated in some studies with altered bone density, hormone-sensitive cancer risk, and reproductive phenotypes.

description: CYP19A1 encodes aromatase, the enzyme responsible for the final step in estrogen biosynthesis — converting androgens (testosterone, androstenedione) to estrogens (estradiol, estrone). rs10046 is a 3'UTR variant that influences CYP19A1 expression levels, affecting circulating estradiol concentrations in both sexes. Estrogen plays critical roles in bone density maintenance, cardiovascular protection, reproductive function, and breast/endometrial tissue proliferation. Variations at this locus have been associated with estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer risk, endometriosis, bone mineral density, and hormone replacement therapy outcomes.

sources.name: Haiman CA et al. (2007) - A common genetic variant in the CYP19A1 locus is associated with postmenopausal estrogen levels

notes: This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. The G and A alleles are nearly equally common (~50/50) globally, with European populations showing ~51% A frequency. The association with estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer risk from this locus confers modest effect sizes (OR ~1.05-1.15 per allele); it is one of dozens of breast cancer susceptibility loci. Bone density associations are more robust. Individuals with AA genotype and family history of estrogen-receptor-positive breast cancer or osteoporosis should discuss screening intervals and hormone therapy decisions with their physician.

AA.description: More sensitive FSH receptor, lower FSH needed for follicular response, better IVF response

AG.description: Moderate FSH receptor sensitivity, standard ovarian response to gonadotropins

GG.description: Less sensitive FSH receptor, higher FSH levels needed, may need higher IVF gonadotropin doses

description: FSHR Asn680Ser variant affects FSH receptor sensitivity in ovarian granulosa cells. Determines ovarian response to gonadotropins and is critical for IVF protocol optimization.
notes: Ser680 variant increases FSH threshold for follicular recruitment. Clinically used to personalize IVF gonadotropin dosing — Ser/Ser women typically need higher doses. Also affects age at menopause.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; associated with modestly lower circulating IGF-1 levels, potentially influencing linear growth and anabolic tissue response.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate IGF-1 expression from the IGF1 promoter region, with variable effects on height and muscle mass.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with elevated circulating IGF-1, linked to greater linear growth potential and enhanced anabolic response to growth hormone.

description: IGF1 encodes insulin-like growth factor 1, the primary mediator of growth hormone action in peripheral tissues. This intronic variant in the IGF1 gene region influences promoter activity and circulating IGF-1 levels. IGF-1 drives skeletal growth, muscle protein synthesis, and cell proliferation, with strong effects during adolescent development.

sources.name: Rosen CJ et al. (2004) - Serum insulin-like growth factors and insulin-like growth factor-binding proteins: clinical implications

notes: IGF-1 levels are highly influenced by nutrition, sleep, and exercise in addition to genetics. Elevated IGF-1 has dual associations with growth benefits and cancer risk (prostate, breast). This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical LH beta-subunit structure produces normal receptor binding affinity and standard mid-cycle LH surge amplitude.

GA.description: Heterozygous; partial shift in LH beta-chain glycosylation pattern modestly changes bioactivity-to-immunoreactivity ratio and circulating LH half-life.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; modified LH beta-subunit structure alters receptor binding kinetics, associated with changed LH bioactivity and potential effects on folliculogenesis.

description: LHB encodes the beta subunit of luteinizing hormone, which combines with the alpha subunit to form bioactive LH. Variants near LHB influence the ratio of bioactive to immunoreactive LH in circulation, affecting the amplitude and duration of the LH surge governing ovulation timing and testosterone synthesis in Leydig cells.

sources.name: Liao WX et al. (2005) - Functional implications of mutations and polymorphisms in the LH beta-subunit gene

notes: LH bioactivity variants can affect reproductive outcomes in both sexes. Anovulatory cycles and male hypogonadism have been associated with extreme LHB variants. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical CYP3A4 expression maintains normal hepatic testosterone catabolism rate and circulating androgen levels.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderately altered CYP3A4 activity produces intermediate testosterone catabolism with modest effects on circulating androgen levels.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; modified CYP3A4 expression changes hepatic testosterone clearance rate, potentially affecting circulating androgen concentrations.

description: CYP3A4 is the major hepatic enzyme responsible for oxidative catabolism of testosterone and other androgens. Variants in or near CYP3A4 alter enzyme expression levels, modifying the rate at which testosterone is converted to inactive metabolites. This influences steady-state testosterone and androgen-sensitive phenotypes including libido, muscle mass, and bone density.

sources.name: Higashi MK et al. (2002) - Association between CYP3A5 genotype and the dose requirement for vitamin K antagonists

notes: CYP3A4 metabolizes >50% of all clinically used drugs in addition to endogenous steroids. Testosterone clearance rate interacts with dietary factors and medications. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard IL-4 promoter activity supports balanced Th1/Th2 immune responses and lower IgE-mediated allergy risk.

CT.description: Heterozygous; intermediate IL-4 upregulation modestly skews immune responses toward Th2, increasing susceptibility to allergic sensitization.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; highest IL-4 promoter activity drives stronger Th2 polarization and elevated IgE production, significantly raising risk for atopic conditions.

description: IL4 encodes interleukin-4, a cytokine that drives Th2 immune differentiation, IgE class switching, and mast cell activation. The -589C>T promoter variant increases IL-4 transcription, promoting Th2 skewing associated with allergic rhinitis, asthma, and atopic dermatitis. Effect is additive and interacts with IL13 and FcεRI variants.

sources.name: Borish L et al. (2001) - IL-4 promoter polymorphisms and asthma

notes: The IL4 -589T allele is among the best-replicated atopy risk variants. Risk is substantially modulated by environmental exposures (hygiene hypothesis, farm exposure). This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Modified OR7D4 receptor, androstenone perceived as pleasant or undetectable

CT.description: Mixed perception, androstenone has intermediate intensity

TT.description: Fully functional OR7D4, androstenone perceived as intensely unpleasant 'sweaty/urinous'

description: OR7D4 olfactory receptor variant determines perception of androstenone, a steroid pheromone found in pork (boar taint), sweat, and truffles. Classic example of human olfactory genetic variation.
notes: OR7D4 R88W variant dramatically alters androstenone perception. ~50% of people find androstenone offensive, ~35% cannot smell it, and ~15% find it pleasant — largely determined by OR7D4 genotype.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; reduced olfactory receptor sensitivity yields inability to detect androstenone, the musky-urine compound in sweat and pork.

GA.description: Heterozygous; partial receptor sensitivity produces weak or inconsistent androstenone perception, often described as faintly pleasant or neutral.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; high receptor sensitivity enables vivid androstenone detection, perceived as urine-like or intensely musky by most carriers.

description: OR11H7P is a pseudogene-region variant within the olfactory receptor cluster on chromosome 14. Variation here modulates sensitivity of functional OR paralogs to androstenone, a pheromone-like steroid abundant in male sweat. Detection ability is strongly heritable and bimodal across populations.

sources.name: Keller A et al. (2007) - Genetic variation in a human odorant receptor alters odour perception

notes: Androstenone perception is one of the best-studied olfactory phenotypes with a Mendelian-like component. Non-detectors represent ~30% of Europeans. Perception can also be trained over time regardless of genotype.

AA.description: Homozygous PTPN22 1858T, strongly increased risk of multiple autoimmune diseases

AG.description: PTPN22 1858T carrier, ~1.5-2x increased risk of type 1 diabetes, RA, and thyroid autoimmunity

GG.description: Wild-type PTPN22, population-average autoimmune disease susceptibility

description: PTPN22 R620W is the strongest non-HLA autoimmune risk variant. Affects T-cell receptor signaling and negative regulation of T-cell activation, increasing susceptibility to multiple autoimmune diseases.
notes: PTPN22 1858T is a gain-of-function variant that increases phosphatase activity, reducing T-cell activation threshold. Shared risk variant for type 1 diabetes, RA, SLE, thyroid autoimmunity, and vitiligo.

AA.description: Non-functional OR5A1 receptor, unable to detect violet/berry aroma (beta-ionone)

AG.description: Intermediate OR5A1 function, can detect beta-ionone at higher concentrations

GG.description: Fully functional OR5A1, highly sensitive to violet, floral, and berry aromas (beta-ionone)

description: OR5A1 variant determines ability to perceive beta-ionone, the characteristic scent of violets, berries, and many flowers. One of the best-characterized human olfactory receptor variants.
notes: Beta-ionone is found in roses, violets, raspberries, and many foods. ~50% of people are sensitive to beta-ionone. The variant explains why some people love floral perfumes while others find them imperceptible.

CC.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds strongly (supertaster possible)

CG.description: Can taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds (medium sensitivity)

GG.description: Cannot taste PTC/PROP bitter compounds or very weakly

description: TAS2R38 bitter taste receptor variant (PAV/AVI haplotype). Determines ability to taste phenylthiocarbamide (PTC) and PROP bitterness.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: One of three TAS2R38 SNPs forming the PAV/AVI haplotype that determines PTC/PROP bitter taste sensitivity. PAV homozygotes are 'supertasters,' AVI homozygotes are non-tasters. The trait follows near-Mendelian inheritance with ~75% accuracy.

TT.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

description: Another TAS2R38 variant that combines with rs713598 to form PAV/AVI haplotypes determining bitter taste sensitivity.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Part of the TAS2R38 haplotype for bitter taste perception. Works in combination with rs713598 and rs10246939. The three SNPs together determine taster/non-taster status with high accuracy across all studied populations.

TT.description: Enhanced bitter taste sensitivity (part of PAV haplotype)

CT.description: Intermediate bitter taste sensitivity

CC.description: Reduced bitter taste sensitivity (part of AVI haplotype)

description: Third key TAS2R38 variant completing the PAV/AVI haplotype. Works with rs713598 and rs1726866.

sources.name: Kim UK et al. (2003) - Positional cloning of the human quantitative trait locus underlying taste sensitivity to phenylthiocarbamide

notes: Third SNP in the TAS2R38 bitter taste haplotype. The combined PAV/AVI haplotype is one of the best-characterized taste genetics examples. Non-taster frequency varies by population: ~30% in Europeans, ~3% in sub-Saharan Africans.

CC.description: More sensitive TRPV1 receptor, lower capsaicin tolerance, perceives spicy food as more intense

CT.description: Intermediate TRPV1 sensitivity, moderate capsaicin perception

TT.description: Less sensitive TRPV1 receptor, higher capsaicin tolerance, better handles spicy food

description: TRPV1 I585V variant affects the transient receptor potential vanilloid 1 channel, the primary capsaicin (chili pepper) and heat-pain receptor. Determines spice sensitivity and pain threshold.
notes: TRPV1 is activated by capsaicin, heat (>43C), and acidic pH. The I585V variant affects receptor sensitivity and desensitization kinetics. Also influences thermal pain perception and migraine susceptibility.

CC.description: Normal cilantro taste - perceives herb flavor

AA.description: Strong soapy/unpleasant taste perception

description: Genetic variation affecting olfactory receptor determining whether cilantro tastes like herbs or soap.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2012) - A genetic variant near olfactory receptor genes influences cilantro preference

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS as associated with perceiving cilantro as soapy-tasting. Located near olfactory receptor gene OR6A2. Effect is moderate — not all carriers dislike cilantro, and cultural exposure can modify preference. Well-replicated finding.

AA.description: Higher pain sensitivity

AG.description: Average pain tolerance

GG.description: Lower pain sensitivity - higher tolerance

description: COMT haplotype variant modulating catecholamine levels and pain perception thresholds.

sources.name: Diatchenko L et al. (2005) - Genetic basis for individual variations in pain perception and the development of a chronic pain condition

notes: COMT haplotype variant influencing pain sensitivity through altered enzyme activity and mRNA structure. Part of a multi-SNP COMT haplotype — individual effect is modest. Pain perception is highly complex with major environmental and psychological components.

TT.description: Lower celiac/gluten sensitivity risk

TC.phenotype: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 Celiac Risk Allele
TC.description: Carrier for HLA-DQ2 celiac risk allele

CC.description: Higher celiac disease and gluten sensitivity risk

description: HLA-DQ2 haplotype tag SNP strongly associated with celiac disease susceptibility.

sources.name: van Heel DA et al. (2007) - A genome-wide association study for celiac disease identifies risk variants in the region harboring IL2 and IL21

notes: HLA-DQ2.5 haplotype tag SNP — primary genetic risk factor for celiac disease. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 is necessary but not sufficient (~30-40% of the population carries it, but only ~1% develops celiac). Environmental trigger is gluten exposure. Gold standard for celiac genetic risk assessment.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; normal connexin 26 expression maintains cochlear potassium recycling and typical auditory threshold sensitivity.

AG.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in GJB2 expression or function may modestly elevate auditory thresholds, especially in noisy environments.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced connexin 26 gap-junction function impairs endocochlear potential maintenance, raising susceptibility to noise-induced and sensorineural hearing loss.

description: GJB2 encodes connexin 26, a gap-junction protein critical for potassium ion recycling in the cochlear epithelium. Variants in the 3' UTR region influence GJB2 expression levels and are associated with variable auditory thresholds and susceptibility to noise-induced hearing loss. GJB2 mutations are the most common cause of congenital recessive hearing impairment.

sources.name: Kelsell DP et al. (1997) - Connexin 26 mutations in hereditary non-syndromic sensorineural deafness

notes: GJB2 encodes connexin 26; biallelic loss-of-function mutations cause autosomal recessive nonsyndromic hearing loss. This 3' UTR variant is a modifier, not a direct cause. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference (Thr469); typical TRPV1 channel activation threshold yields normal perception of noxious heat and capsaicin.

GA.description: Heterozygous (Thr/Ile); intermediate TRPV1 function produces modestly altered heat pain threshold, often toward reduced sensitivity.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate (Ile469); lower TRPV1 channel responsiveness raises heat pain threshold, reducing perceived pain from hot stimuli.

description: TRPV1 is the primary thermosensor for noxious heat (>43°C) and is activated by capsaicin and protons. The Thr469Ile missense variant alters channel gating kinetics and desensitization rate, modifying both heat pain detection threshold and capsaicin sensitivity. TRPV1 also plays a role in inflammatory pain signaling.

sources.name: Kim H et al. (2012) - Genetic predictors for pain and analgesic drug treatment

notes: TRPV1 variants contribute to individual differences in spicy food tolerance, thermal pain threshold, and analgesic efficacy. Multiple TRPV1 SNPs interact. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Low sensitivity to trigger sounds

GA.description: Moderate sound sensitivity

AA.description: High sensitivity to chewing/repetitive sounds

description: Genetic predisposition to strong emotional reactions triggered by specific sounds like chewing.

sources.name: Sanchez-Roige S et al. (2017) - Genome-wide association studies of impulsive personality traits and misophonia

notes: TENM2 variant linked to misophonia (sound sensitivity) in a single GWAS. Evidence is preliminary — misophonia genetics is poorly understood. The condition likely involves multiple genes and neurological pathways. Replication in larger cohorts is needed.

CC.description: Low motion sickness risk

CT.description: Moderate motion sickness risk

TT.description: High susceptibility to motion sickness

description: Genetic variation affecting vestibular-visual conflict processing and nausea response.

sources.name: Hromatka BS et al. (2015) - Genetic variants associated with motion sickness point to roles for inner ear development, neurological processes and glucose homeostasis

notes: Identified in a 23andMe GWAS for motion sickness susceptibility. Effect is modest. Motion sickness is influenced by vestibular function, visual processing, and psychological factors. Genetic contribution is polygenic.

AA.description: Normal opioid receptor, higher baseline pain sensitivity, better response to opioid medications

AG.description: Intermediate pain sensitivity and opioid response

GG.description: Altered opioid receptor (Asn40Asp), reduced pain sensitivity, may need higher opioid doses

description: OPRM1 mu-opioid receptor variant (A118G) affecting pain perception and opioid medication response. G allele reduces receptor expression.

sources.name: Fillingim RB et al. (2005) - The A118G single nucleotide polymorphism of the mu-opioid receptor gene (OPRM1) is associated with pressure pain sensitivity in humans

notes: OPRM1 A118G variant affecting mu-opioid receptor function. Associated with altered pain sensitivity and variable analgesic response to opioids. Clinical relevance for pain management is being studied but is not yet standard of care. Effect varies by ancestry.

AA.description: Gain-of-function SCN9A variant, lower pain threshold, heightened nociception

AG.description: Heterozygous SCN9A variant, mildly modified pain perception

GG.description: Standard SCN9A function, population-average pain sensitivity

description: SCN9A encodes the Nav1.7 sodium channel, a key pain signaling molecule. Variants cause phenotypes ranging from congenital insensitivity to pain to extreme pain disorders.
notes: SCN9A/Nav1.7 is a validated analgesic drug target. Loss-of-function mutations cause congenital insensitivity to pain, while gain-of-function causes erythromelalgia and paroxysmal extreme pain disorder.

GG.description: Typical pitch perception

GA.description: Enhanced pitch memory

AA.description: Predisposition for absolute pitch ability

description: HLA region variant associated with rare absolute pitch ability in musicians.

sources.name: Theusch E et al. (2009) - Genome-wide study of families with absolute pitch reveals linkage to 8q24.21 and locus heterogeneity

notes: HLA-A region variant associated with absolute pitch in a linkage study. Evidence is preliminary — absolute pitch has a strong genetic component (~80% heritability) but the specific loci remain poorly characterized. Early musical training is a required environmental cofactor.

CC.description: Normal light tolerance

CT.description: Mild light sensitivity

TT.description: Increased light sensitivity (photophobia)

description: NAD(P)H dehydrogenase variant affecting retinal sensitivity to bright light.

sources.name: Siegel D et al. (1999) - NAD(P)H:quinone oxidoreductase 1: role as a superoxide scavenger

notes: NQO1 Pro187Ser variant reducing enzyme activity to near zero. Originally studied for cancer susceptibility. Association with photophobia is indirect and based on limited evidence. NQO1 deficiency affects oxidative stress response in multiple tissues.

GG.description: Susceptible to norovirus

GA.description: Partial resistance

AA.description: Resistant to most norovirus strains (non-secretor)

description: Fucosyltransferase variant determining secretor status and norovirus binding.

sources.name: Thorven M et al. (2005) - A homozygous nonsense mutation (428G→A) in the human secretor (FUT2) gene provides resistance to symptomatic norovirus (GGII) infections

notes: FUT2 nonsense variant conferring near-complete resistance to norovirus infection in homozygotes (~20% of Europeans). Non-secretors (AA genotype) lack the gut surface receptor the virus uses for entry. One of the clearest examples of infection resistance genetics.

GG.description: Standard umami perception

GA.description: Enhanced umami sensitivity

AA.description: Highly sensitive to umami/savory taste

description: Taste receptor variant determining sensitivity to glutamate and savory flavors.

sources.name: Shigemura N et al. (2009) - Genetic and molecular basis of individual differences in human umami taste perception

notes: TAS1R1 variant associated with sensitivity to umami (savory) taste. Less well-characterized than bitter taste genetics. Umami perception is influenced by multiple receptor variants and dietary experience.

CC.description: Standard sweet taste perception

CT.description: Slightly enhanced sweet sensitivity

TT.description: Enhanced sweet taste sensitivity - prefers less sugar

description: Sweet taste receptor variant affecting sensitivity to sugars and sweet taste preference.

sources.name: Fushan AA et al. (2009) - Allelic polymorphism within the TAS1R3 promoter is associated with human taste sensitivity to sucrose

notes: TAS1R2 variant influencing sweet taste perception and sugar intake. Effect is moderate — sweet preference is heavily influenced by environment, age, and dietary habits. Some evidence links this variant to differences in sucrose detection thresholds.

AA.description: Homozygous reference (Ser90); higher CA6 enzymatic activity enhances zinc delivery to taste buds, supporting normal bitter and sour perception.

AG.description: Heterozygous (Ser/Gly); mixed CA6 activity produces intermediate taste sensitivity, with modest reduction in bitter-sour discrimination.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate (Gly90); lower CA6 protein stability reduces zinc availability at taste receptor cells, associated with reduced sensitivity to bitter and sour compounds.

description: CA6 encodes carbonic anhydrase VI, the primary enzyme secreted in saliva. It catalyzes CO₂ hydration and supplies zinc ions critical for gustin (the zinc-binding taste protein) function at taste buds. The Ser90Gly missense variant reduces enzyme stability and has been associated with altered bitter and sour taste perception thresholds.

sources.name: Padiglia A et al. (2010) - Sensitivity to 6-n-propylthiouracil is associated with gustin (carbonic anhydrase VI) gene polymorphism

notes: CA6 Ser90Gly is one of few missense variants with a demonstrated functional impact on taste receptor sensitivity. The G allele frequency varies widely, reaching ~65% in some populations. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical TRPA1 channel activity yields normal pungency response to allyl isothiocyanate in wasabi and mustard.

TA.description: Heterozygous; partial reduction in TRPA1 channel activity slightly blunts the burning sensation from isothiocyanate compounds.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; lower TRPA1 channel responsiveness produces notably diminished wasabi and mustard oil pungency.

description: TRPA1 encodes a calcium-permeable ion channel activated by reactive electrophiles including allyl isothiocyanate (the active compound in wasabi and horseradish) and cinnamaldehyde. Channel variants alter threshold and intensity of the resulting burning/pungent sensation. This variant has been associated with differential chemosensory pain thresholds.

sources.name: Jaquemar D et al. (2011) - TRPA1 mediates formalin-induced pain and is modulated by genetic variation

notes: Sensitivity to isothiocyanates is relevant for food preference and spice tolerance. TRPA1 also mediates responses to cold and environmental irritants. Individual variation is influenced by multiple TRPA1 variants beyond this locus.

AA.description: Normal TOMM40/APOE region expression, population-average aging trajectory

AG.description: Intermediate effect on mitochondrial import and aging-related biomarkers

GG.description: Altered TOMM40 expression, associated with faster cognitive decline and biological aging markers

description: TOMM40 (translocase of outer mitochondrial membrane 40) variant near APOE affects mitochondrial protein import efficiency. Associated with biological aging rate and cognitive decline.
notes: TOMM40 is adjacent to APOE on 19q13. Debate continues whether TOMM40 has independent effects beyond APOE linkage. TOMM40 poly-T repeat length correlates with age of Alzheimer's onset.

CC.description: Proline variant, stronger cell cycle arrest and DNA repair, slower apoptosis

CG.description: Heterozygous, balanced apoptosis and repair capacity

GG.description: Arginine variant, more efficient apoptosis, potentially better tumor suppression but faster aging

description: TP53 codon 72 polymorphism (Arg72Pro) affects p53 protein function, balancing apoptotic efficiency versus DNA repair capacity. Modifies cancer susceptibility and aging-related phenotypes.
notes: Arg72 has 15x stronger apoptotic activity than Pro72, but Pro72 is better at cell cycle arrest and repair. Population frequencies vary by latitude — Pro72 is more common near the equator.

AA.description: Homozygous reference (Val762); typical PARP1 automodification activity enables efficient single-strand break repair and chromatin remodeling after genotoxic stress.

AG.description: Heterozygous (Val/Ala); partially reduced PARP1 catalytic efficiency moderately slows base excision repair, with intermediate accumulation of DNA damage under oxidative stress.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate (Ala762); lower PARP1 automodification rate reduces repair efficiency, associated with greater residual DNA damage after oxidative or radiation stress.

description: PARP1 encodes poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1, a nuclear enzyme that detects single-strand DNA breaks and orchestrates base excision repair by poly-ADP-ribosylating histones and repair factors. The Val762Ala missense variant (A>G on the reference strand) reduces PARP1 autoacetylation efficiency, impairing the speed of single-strand break repair and increasing susceptibility to oxidative DNA damage accumulation.

sources.name: Lockett KL et al. (2007) - PARP1 Val762Ala polymorphism and susceptibility to prostate cancer

notes: PARP1 inhibitors are an approved cancer therapy (olaparib, niraparib). Val762Ala has been associated with cancer risk modulation in multiple studies. DNA repair capacity declines with age regardless of genotype. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

AA.description: Full XPC function, efficient global genomic nucleotide excision repair

AC.description: Intermediate XPC activity, slightly reduced UV damage recognition

CC.description: Lower XPC efficiency, impaired recognition of UV-induced DNA damage, increased skin aging

description: XPC Ala499Val variant affects the global genome nucleotide excision repair (GG-NER) damage sensor. XPC initiates repair of UV-induced cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers and 6-4 photoproducts.
notes: XPC is the primary UV damage sensor for transcriptionally silent DNA. Homozygous XPC loss causes xeroderma pigmentosum group C. Common variants modestly affect skin cancer risk and photoaging.

AA.description: Increased nevus (mole) count and elevated melanoma susceptibility

AG.description: Moderate effect on mole count and melanoma risk

GG.description: Fewer nevi, baseline melanoma risk from this variant

description: MTAP 9p21 variant affects melanocyte proliferation and nevus formation. Nevus count is the strongest single risk factor for melanoma, and this variant modulates both.
notes: MTAP is adjacent to CDKN2A, the familial melanoma gene. Total nevus count >100 increases melanoma risk ~7x. This variant explains ~1% of nevus count variation but has strong epidemiological relevance.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical TERC expression supports normal telomerase activity and average telomere length maintenance across somatic cell divisions.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate TERC expression and telomerase activity produces moderately altered telomere length trajectory, associated with average biological aging rate.
