GG.description: Homozygous alternate; higher TERC expression supports greater telomerase activity, associated with longer telomere length and potentially slower cellular senescence.

description: TERC encodes the RNA component (hTR) of telomerase, the ribonucleoprotein enzyme that maintains chromosome end length by synthesizing TTAGGG repeats. Variants near TERC modulate its expression level, directly influencing telomerase activity and mean telomere length. Shorter telomeres are associated with accelerated biological aging, cardiovascular disease, and age-related disease susceptibility.

sources.name: Codd V et al. (2011) - Common variants near TERC are associated with mean telomere length

notes: Telomere length is heritable (~40-80%) but highly variable due to oxidative stress, smoking, BMI, and psychosocial stress. The TERC locus is among the most replicated GWAS hits for telomere length. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Lower stretch mark tendency

CT.description: Moderate stretch mark risk

TT.description: Higher stretch mark susceptibility

description: Elastin gene variant affecting skin elasticity and striae formation.

sources.name: Tung JY et al. (2013) - A genome-wide association analysis identifies genetic loci associated with stretch marks

notes: ELN region variant associated with stretch mark (striae distensae) susceptibility in GWAS. Stretch marks are primarily influenced by rapid skin stretching (growth, pregnancy, weight change) and skin elasticity. Genetic contribution is one factor among many.

GG.description: Normal MC1R function, typical sun sensitivity for skin type

GA.description: Partial MC1R dysfunction, burns more easily, higher melanoma risk

AA.description: Reduced MC1R function, very sun sensitive, high melanoma risk

description: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and melanoma risk, even in individuals without red hair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (V92M) associated with sun sensitivity and fair skin, though with lower penetrance than classic red-hair MC1R variants. Common across multiple populations. Carriers may have increased UV sensitivity without exhibiting red hair.

GG.description: Normal UV damage recovery

CC.description: Slower sunburn recovery and higher UV sensitivity

description: Melanocortin receptor variant affecting melanin production and UV damage repair.

sources.name: Valverde P et al. (1995) - Variants of the melanocyte-stimulating hormone receptor gene are associated with red hair and fair skin in humans

notes: MC1R variant (D294H) associated with red hair, fair skin, and increased UV sensitivity. Carriers experience slower sunburn recovery and higher melanoma risk. Sun protection is especially important for MC1R variant carriers regardless of apparent hair color.

CC.description: Reduced TERC expression, shorter leukocyte telomere length

CT.description: Moderate TERC activity, average telomere maintenance

TT.description: Higher TERC expression, longer leukocyte telomere length, potentially slower cellular aging

description: TERC encodes the RNA component of telomerase, essential for telomere maintenance. Variants affect leukocyte telomere length, a biomarker of biological aging.
notes: TERC provides the template for telomeric repeat addition. Along with TERT, it is a major genetic determinant of telomere length. Shorter telomeres associate with cardiovascular disease and mortality.

CC.description: Increased TERT-CLPTM1L expression, longer telomeres but higher skin cancer susceptibility

CT.description: Moderate telomere maintenance, intermediate skin cancer risk

TT.description: Lower TERT-CLPTM1L activity, slightly shorter telomeres but reduced skin cancer susceptibility

description: TERT-CLPTM1L 5p15.33 variant influences telomere length and has pleiotropic effects on multiple cancer types, particularly basal cell carcinoma and melanoma.
notes: 5p15.33 locus contains both TERT and CLPTM1L genes. Pleiotropic cancer risk — the same allele can increase risk for some cancers while decreasing risk for others. Major skin cancer susceptibility locus.

AC.description: Heterozygous TERT variant, intermediate telomere maintenance capacity

AA.description: Lower TERT expression, tendency toward shorter telomeres and accelerated cellular aging

CC.description: Higher TERT expression, better telomere maintenance, associated with both longevity and increased cancer risk

description: TERT (telomerase reverse transcriptase) variant affects telomere length maintenance. Longer telomeres are associated with longevity but paradoxically also with increased cancer risk.
notes: TERT is the catalytic subunit of telomerase. The C allele associates with longer telomeres but also increased lung cancer, glioma, and bladder cancer risk — illustrating the aging-cancer tradeoff.

GG.description: Can detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine

GA.description: Moderate detection ability

AA.description: Cannot detect asparagus urine odor

description: Olfactory receptor variant determining ability to smell asparagus metabolites in urine.

sources.name: Pelchat ML et al. (2011) - Excretion and perception of a characteristic odor in urine after asparagus ingestion: a psychophysical and genetic study

notes: Associated with the ability to detect asparagus metabolite odor in urine. Approximately 40% of people cannot smell the characteristic odor. The trait is harmless and primarily of scientific curiosity. Effect is population-consistent.

CC.description: Altered CDKN2A/B expression, faster cellular senescence and biological aging markers

CT.description: Moderate effect on cell cycle regulation and senescence

TT.description: Normal CDKN2A/B expression, population-average biological aging

description: CDKN2A/B locus encodes p16INK4a and p15INK4b tumor suppressors that regulate cellular senescence. Variants affect both type 2 diabetes risk and biological aging rate.
notes: 9p21 is the most replicated GWAS locus, associated with coronary disease, type 2 diabetes, and glioma. p16INK4a is a key senescence marker that accumulates with age.

CC.description: Stronger body odor

CT.description: Moderate body odor

TT.description: Reduced body odor

description: ATP-binding cassette transporter variant affecting apocrine sweat gland secretions.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Linked to ABCC11 function affecting apocrine gland secretions and body odor intensity. Most relevant in populations polymorphic for ABCC11 variants. The same biological pathway controls earwax type (wet/dry) and axillary odor production.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical MTNR1B expression supports normal melatonin-regulated sleep architecture and average REM sleep intensity.

CG.description: Heterozygous; increased MTNR1B expression alters melatonin signaling during REM sleep phases, associated with more recalled and vivid dream experiences.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; highest MTNR1B expression intensifies melatonin-phase signaling, linked to the most vivid, memorable, and emotionally intense dream experiences.

description: MTNR1B encodes melatonin receptor 1B, expressed in the retina, brain, and pancreatic beta cells. The intronic G allele increases MTNR1B expression, amplifying melatonin signaling during circadian nighttime phases. Enhanced melatonin receptor activity during REM sleep is associated with more vivid dream recall and altered sleep stage cycling, though the primary phenotype studied is glucose metabolism.

sources.name: Bouatia-Naji N et al. (2009) - A variant near MTNR1B is associated with increased fasting plasma glucose levels

notes: The primary GWAS phenotype for rs10830963 is fasting glucose and type 2 diabetes risk. Dream vividness is an associated secondary observation. Sleep quality, alcohol use, and medications also significantly affect dreaming.

CC.description: Wet, sticky earwax (European/African type), active apocrine glands, more body odor

CT.description: Wet earwax (dominant), normal body odor

TT.description: Dry, flaky earwax (East Asian type), reduced apocrine gland activity, less body odor

description: ABCC11 variant determining earwax type and apocrine gland secretion. TT genotype (dry earwax) is nearly fixed in East Asians (80-95%) but rare elsewhere.

sources.name: Yoshiura K et al. (2006) - A SNP in the ABCC11 gene is the determinant of human earwax type

notes: Single SNP determining wet vs dry earwax type. The dry earwax allele (T) is near-fixation in East Asian populations. Also determines axillary (underarm) body odor intensity — dry earwax carriers produce less odorous secretions. Classic example of pleiotropic genetic variation.

CC.description: Efficient mitochondrial import of MnSOD, better superoxide radical defense

CT.description: Moderate mitochondrial MnSOD levels, intermediate antioxidant defense

TT.description: Reduced mitochondrial MnSOD import, lower superoxide defense, potential oxidative stress

description: SOD2 Ala16Val variant affects mitochondrial targeting sequence of manganese superoxide dismutase, the primary mitochondrial antioxidant enzyme. Ala16 allows efficient mitochondrial import.
notes: The Ala variant forms an alpha-helix allowing efficient mitochondrial import. Val forms a beta-sheet, reducing import by 30-40%. Paradoxically, higher SOD2 activity has been linked to both protection and risk in different contexts.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical hemoglobin alpha-chain structure produces standard oxygen affinity and normal response to hypoxic conditions at altitude.

CT.description: Heterozygous; intermediate shift in hemoglobin-oxygen affinity slightly improves oxygen loading efficiency at reduced partial pressures encountered at altitude.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; altered hemoglobin alpha-chain interaction with beta subunits optimizes oxygen loading at altitude, associated with better exercise performance in hypoxic environments.

description: The HBA gene cluster encodes the hemoglobin alpha chains, which combine with beta chains to form the primary oxygen transport molecule. Variants in the HBA region can subtly shift hemoglobin-oxygen affinity (p50) and cooperative oxygen binding (Hill coefficient), altering the ability to load oxygen in the lungs at high-altitude low-pO₂ conditions. Tibetan and Andean altitude adaptation studies have identified HBA-region variants as contributors to hypoxic tolerance.

sources.name: Simonson TS et al. (2014) - Genetic determinants of Tibetan high-altitude adaptation

notes: High-altitude adaptation is polygenic; EPAS1 (HIF-2α) and EGLN1 loci have the strongest documented effects in Tibetan and Andean populations. HBA variants contribute modest additive effects. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Less attractive to mosquitoes

TC.description: Average mosquito attraction

CC.description: More attractive to mosquitoes

description: HLA-associated body odor compounds affecting attractiveness to mosquitoes.

sources.name: Fernandez-Grandon GM et al. (2015) - Heritability of attractiveness to mosquitoes

notes: HLA-region variant loosely associated with mosquito bite attraction. Evidence is limited and the mechanism is poorly characterized. Mosquito attraction depends on skin microbiome, CO2 output, body heat, and many non-genetic factors.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical CLOCK 3' UTR activity supports a normal ~24-hour circadian period and conventional sleep-wake timing.

AT.description: Heterozygous; partial disruption to CLOCK mRNA stability modestly lengthens circadian period, shifting preferred sleep onset and wake times later.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; greatest disruption to CLOCK 3' UTR regulation drives the longest circadian period and most extreme evening preference, with sleep onset often after midnight.

description: CLOCK encodes the core transcription factor of the mammalian circadian clock, heterodimerizing with BMAL1 to drive 24-hour gene expression rhythms. The 3111T/C (rs1801260) 3' UTR variant alters mRNA stability and CLOCK protein levels, lengthening the endogenous circadian period. Homozygous T carriers show the most pronounced evening preference and sleep phase delay.

sources.name: Katzenberg D et al. (1998) - A CLOCK polymorphism associated with human diurnal preference

notes: CLOCK rs1801260 is one of the earliest circadian candidate gene variants studied in humans. Effect size is modest; chronotype is polygenic with >350 GWAS loci. Light exposure, age, and social schedules (social jet lag) powerfully override genetic tendency.

CC.description: No photic sneeze reflex

CT.description: Mild sun-induced sneezing

TT.description: Strong sneeze reflex when exposed to bright light

description: Autosomal dominant trait causing sneezing when suddenly exposed to bright light.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with photic sneeze reflex (ACHOO syndrome) in GWAS. The reflex affects ~25% of people and is harmless. Genetic architecture is poorly understood. This SNP explains only a small fraction of the trait.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical ADRA2A expression supports normal norepinephrine autoreceptor tone and average acoustic or tactile startle amplitude.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderately increased ADRA2A transcription enhances noradrenergic autoinhibition at presynaptic terminals, slightly altering norepinephrine-mediated arousal.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; highest ADRA2A expression intensifies presynaptic norepinephrine feedback, associated with an amplified acoustic startle reflex and heightened stress reactivity.

description: ADRA2A encodes alpha-2A adrenergic receptor, an inhibitory G-protein coupled receptor that regulates norepinephrine release from presynaptic terminals. The -1291C>G upstream variant alters ADRA2A promoter activity and receptor density in the locus coeruleus and prefrontal cortex, modulating noradrenergic tone. Higher receptor expression is associated with increased startle reactivity and stress hormone response.

sources.name: Small KM et al. (2004) - Synergistic polymorphisms of beta1- and alpha2C-adrenergic receptors and the risk of congestive heart failure

notes: ADRA2A variants are also studied in ADHD, anxiety disorders, and post-traumatic stress. The startle reflex phenotype is influenced by concurrent norepinephrine signaling via COMT and MAOA variants. Effect size is modest.

AA.description: Cannot roll tongue into tube shape

AG.description: Partial tongue rolling ability

GG.description: Can easily roll tongue into tube shape

description: Ability to curl the lateral edges of the tongue upward into a tube shape.

sources.name: Eriksson N et al. (2010) - Web-based, participant-driven studies yield novel genetic associations for common traits

notes: Associated with tongue rolling ability in consumer genetics platforms. The genetics of tongue rolling have been debated for decades — it is NOT a simple Mendelian trait as commonly taught. Monozygotic twin concordance is only ~70%, indicating environmental influence.

AA.description: Increased CYP1A2 induction, very fast caffeine clearance, caffeine has short-lived effects

AC.description: Enhanced CYP1A2 inducibility, moderately fast caffeine metabolism

CC.description: Standard CYP1A2 activity at this position, typical caffeine half-life

description: CYP1A2*1F variant affects inducibility of the primary caffeine-metabolizing enzyme. Ultra-rapid metabolizers clear caffeine faster and may benefit from pre-exercise caffeine for performance.
notes: CYP1A2 metabolizes ~95% of caffeine. This variant modifies enzyme inducibility by smoking, cruciferous vegetables, and other environmental factors. Interacts with rs762551 for overall caffeine metabolism speed.

AA.phenotype: Lower ASIP Activity / Darker Skin Tone
AA.description: Lower agouti signaling protein activity promotes eumelanin (dark pigment) production, associated with darker skin tones and some population data suggest modest reduced melanoma risk.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate ASIP activity with mixed eumelanin/phaeomelanin balance and intermediate pigmentation phenotype.

GG.phenotype: Higher ASIP Activity / Lighter Skin Tendency
GG.description: Higher agouti signaling protein activity shifts pigment toward phaeomelanin (lighter/red pigment), associated with lighter skin tones and modestly higher UV sensitivity.

description: ASIP (agouti signaling protein) competitively inhibits MC1R, reducing eumelanin synthesis. The rs1015362 variant influences ASIP expression levels, modulating the balance between dark eumelanin and lighter phaeomelanin across skin, hair, and eye tissues.

sources.name: Sulem P et al. (2008) - Two newly identified genetic determinants of pigmentation

notes: ASIP variants are part of the multi-locus pigmentation architecture alongside MC1R, OCA2, HERC2, and SLC45A2. Effect is population-context dependent.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical pigmentation at this ASIP/PIGU locus, contributing to standard skin, hair, and eye color baseline.

GA.description: Heterozygous; modest modification of ASIP-mediated pigmentation, slightly altered eumelanin/phaeomelanin ratio.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered pigmentation variation including effects on skin, hair, and eye color across European populations.

description: The rs2378249 variant near the PIGU/ASIP locus on chromosome 20 is associated with pigmentation variation in GWAS studies. It contributes to the polygenic architecture of human coloration through modulation of agouti signaling.

sources.name: Sulem P et al. (2008) - Two newly identified genetic determinants of pigmentation

notes: This variant is in the same chromosomal region as ASIP and was identified in a large Icelandic pigmentation GWAS. Effect size is modest and contributes to polygenic pigmentation scores.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; TYRP1 activity favors brown/black eumelanin synthesis, contributing to darker hair pigment types.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate TYRP1 function resulting in mixed eumelanin/phaeomelanin balance and intermediate hair color shading.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; altered TYRP1 activity shifts pigment balance toward phaeomelanin, associated with lighter or reddish-tinged hair pigmentation.

description: TYRP1 (tyrosinase-related protein 1) catalyzes the final steps of eumelanin synthesis. The rs2733832 variant influences the eumelanin-to-phaeomelanin ratio in hair follicle melanocytes, contributing to natural hair color variation.

sources.name: Han J et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study identifies novel alleles associated with hair color and skin pigmentation

notes: TYRP1 variants contribute to the brown vs. non-brown hair axis. Effect is most pronounced in European populations where allele frequencies vary significantly.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; associated with greater adenosine A2A receptor sensitivity, leading to increased anxiety and jitteriness in response to caffeine consumption.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate adenosine receptor sensitivity with moderate caffeine-induced anxiety effects.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with lower adenosine A2A receptor sensitivity, resulting in reduced anxiety response to caffeine.

description: ADORA2A encodes the adenosine A2A receptor. Caffeine acts as an adenosine receptor antagonist; individuals with high A2A receptor sensitivity experience more pronounced anxiety with caffeine intake. The rs5751876 T allele is associated with greater self-reported anxiety after caffeine consumption.

sources.name: Alsene K et al. (2003) - Association between A2a receptor gene polymorphisms and caffeine-induced anxiety

notes: This is one of the most well-replicated gene-substance interaction findings in behavioral genetics. T allele carriers report more anxiety with 150mg+ caffeine. Relevant for caffeine sensitivity counseling. Not a psychiatric diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical nicotinic acetylcholine receptor beta-3 subunit expression with standard cholinergic signaling in cortical and subcortical circuits.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in nicotinic receptor beta-3 subunit function with modest effects on cholinergic neurotransmission.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered nicotinic acetylcholine receptor beta-3 subunit activity, modifying cholinergic tone in attention and arousal circuits.

description: CHRNB3 encodes the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor beta-3 subunit, which participates in heteromeric nAChR assembly in cortical and subcortical neurons. Cholinergic signaling via nAChRs modulates attention, arousal, and goal-directed behavior. The rs4950 variant was identified in a GWAS of occupational role occupancy; effect size is small and the behavioral correlate is highly multifactorial. Study populations were primarily of European ancestry, limiting generalizability.

sources.name: De Neve JE et al. (2013) - Born to lead? A twin design and genetic association study of leadership role occupancy

notes: Cholinergic signaling influences multiple complex behaviors; this variant represents one small component. Effect sizes are modest and findings derive primarily from European-cohort studies, limiting cross-population applicability. Results should be interpreted as a biological curiosity, not a predictive marker.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical oxytocin receptor expression associated with standard pair-bonding behaviors and relationship satisfaction scores.

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate oxytocin receptor function with modest influence on relationship satisfaction and attachment style.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated in some studies with higher relationship satisfaction, stronger pair-bonding, and more secure attachment behaviors.

description: The oxytocin receptor gene (OXTR) mediates the social bonding effects of oxytocin. The rs7632287 variant has been associated with relationship satisfaction and pair-bonding strength in epidemiological studies, reflecting the role of oxytocinergic signaling in human attachment. Original study findings were specific to female participants; association strength and direction in male participants has not been consistently replicated. Study populations were primarily of European ancestry, limiting generalizability.

sources.name: Walum H et al. (2012) - Variation in the oxytocin receptor gene is associated with pair-bonding and social behavior

notes: Oxytocin genetics and behavior associations have limited replication. Relationship satisfaction is shaped overwhelmingly by environmental, cultural, and personal factors. This is a population-level statistical association, not an individual predictor. Original study findings were specific to female participants.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical ARC (activity-regulated cytoskeleton-associated protein) expression supporting standard long-term potentiation and synaptic remodeling in hippocampal and cortical circuits.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in ARC regulatory function with intermediate effects on synaptic plasticity signaling.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with differences in ARC protein-mediated synaptic remodeling, affecting the efficiency of activity-dependent synapse strengthening and elimination.

description: ARC (activity-regulated cytoskeleton-associated protein) is a master regulator of synaptic plasticity and a critical mediator of long-term potentiation and depression. ARC coordinates AMPA receptor trafficking, dendritic spine morphology, and synaptic tagging. The rs322931 variant influences ARC expression or function; associations derive primarily from European-ancestry cohorts, limiting cross-population generalizability.

sources.name: Tzingounis AV, Nicoll RA (2006) - Arc/Arg3.1: linking gene expression to synaptic plasticity and memory

notes: ARC is a critical plasticity gene implicated in memory consolidation, but direct SNP-level associations have modest effect sizes and limited replication. Cognitive performance is shaped by many genes, education, sleep, and training. Findings are primarily from European-cohort studies. Note: The primary citation is a review of ARC/Arg3.1 gene function, not a genetic association study for this specific variant.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical DRD1 receptor density in prefrontal cortex supporting standard dopaminergic D1 signaling in working memory and executive function circuits.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in D1 receptor expression with intermediate effects on prefrontal dopaminergic signaling.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with differences in DRD1 expression or function, modifying dopaminergic neurotransmission in prefrontal cortex and striatal circuits.

description: DRD1 encodes the dopamine D1 receptor, the primary excitatory dopamine receptor mediating cAMP/PKA signaling in prefrontal cortex, striatum, and limbic areas. D1 receptor signaling is critical for working memory, synaptic plasticity, and reward processing. The rs4532 variant influences D1 receptor expression or function; associations derive primarily from European-ancestry cohorts, limiting cross-population generalizability.

sources.name: Richter A et al. (2019) - The Role of DRD1 and DRD2 Receptors for Response Selection Under Varying Complexity Levels

notes: Dopaminergic signaling is a highly polygenic system influenced by DRD1, DRD2, DRD4, DAT, and COMT among many other loci. This single variant explains a very small fraction of variance. Findings are primarily from European-cohort studies. Not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Homozygous reference (Met235); lower circulating angiotensinogen levels associated with attenuated blood pressure rise during exercise and potentially better endurance performance.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate angiotensinogen levels with moderate exercise-induced blood pressure responses.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate (Thr235); higher circulating angiotensinogen associated with greater blood pressure elevation during exercise, increased cardiac afterload, and modestly higher hypertension risk.

description: AGT encodes angiotensinogen, the precursor to angiotensin I and II — the primary vasoconstrictors of the renin-angiotensin system. The rs699 (M235T) variant increases angiotensinogen levels in Thr235 carriers, elevating blood pressure responses to exercise and influencing cardiovascular adaptation to training.

sources.name: Rankinen T et al. (2004) - Genomic scan for exercise blood pressure in the HERITAGE Family Study

notes: Alleles reported on AGT coding strand (M235T convention). dbSNP plus strand: A>G. AGT M235T is one of the most studied hypertension candidate variants. The Thr235 allele frequency is approximately 50-70% in East Asian populations and 30-45% in European populations. Effect on athletic performance is modest and training-dependent. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for blood pressure and cardiovascular health concerns.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical NRF2-mediated antioxidant response element activation, standard upregulation of protective enzymes (SOD, catalase, glutathione) during exercise-induced oxidative stress.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderate NRF2 pathway activity with intermediate antioxidant enzyme induction during oxidative challenge.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with stronger NRF2 pathway activation and upregulation of cytoprotective genes during exercise-induced oxidative stress, potentially improving recovery.

description: NRF2 (nuclear factor erythroid 2-related factor 2) is the master regulator of the antioxidant response. Exercise generates reactive oxygen species; NRF2 activates protective enzyme genes. The rs7191721 variant influences NRF2 transcriptional activity, modulating the body's ability to manage exercise-induced oxidative stress.

sources.name: He X et al. (2012) - NRF2 genetic variation and exercise performance

notes: NRF2 pathway variants are studied in contexts of exercise recovery, age-related oxidative damage, and disease susceptibility. Antioxidant supplementation may interact with NRF2 genotype.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical GALNT13 glycosylation activity associated with standard tendon extracellular matrix composition and normal flexibility/strength.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in tendon ECM glycosylation with modest effects on tendon mechanical properties.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with differences in tendon extracellular matrix composition through altered glycosyltransferase activity, potentially influencing tendon flexibility and injury susceptibility.

description: GALNT13 encodes a UDP-N-acetylgalactosamine transferase involved in O-glycosylation of extracellular matrix proteins. Glycosylation of collagen and proteoglycans is critical for tendon mechanical properties. The rs7136446 variant may alter glycosylation patterns in tendon ECM, influencing flexibility and injury risk.

sources.name: Bray MS et al. (2009) - NIH working group report on GWAS in fitness and exercise science

notes: GALNT13 is expressed in neural and connective tissues. Evidence for tendon-specific effects is preliminary. Tendon health is primarily modulated by training load, nutrition, and collagen genetics (COL5A1, TNC).

AA.description: Homozygous reference; lower baseline hepatic lipase activity associated with a greater exercise-induced increase in HDL cholesterol levels.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate hepatic lipase activity with moderate HDL elevation in response to aerobic training.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; higher hepatic lipase activity associated with faster HDL catabolism and a blunted HDL increase in response to aerobic exercise training.

description: LIPC encodes hepatic lipase, an enzyme that catabolizes HDL cholesterol. The rs2070895 variant influences hepatic lipase activity levels. Lower activity (A allele) correlates with higher baseline and exercise-induced HDL levels, making this variant relevant to cardiovascular benefit from aerobic training.

sources.name: Teran-Garcia M et al. (2008) - Hepatic lipase gene variant and HDL cholesterol response to exercise

notes: LIPC -514C/T is a promoter variant (rs1800588 is most studied; rs2070895 is in LD). The A allele associates with lower hepatic lipase and higher HDL. Effect is most pronounced in response to endurance training programs.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical PHACTR1-mediated phosphatase regulation with standard serum phosphate homeostasis.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in phosphatase regulatory activity with modest effects on phosphate metabolism.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with changes in PHACTR1 expression affecting phosphatase activity and serum phosphate levels, relevant to bone mineralization and energy metabolism.

description: PHACTR1 (phosphatase and actin regulator 1) modulates protein phosphatase 1 activity, influencing cellular phosphate signaling. The rs12325817 variant is associated with serum phosphate levels, which affect bone mineralization, cellular energy metabolism (ATP synthesis), and vascular calcification risk.

sources.name: Kestenbaum B et al. (2012) - Common genetic variants associate with serum phosphorus concentration

notes: Serum phosphate levels are regulated by FGF23, PTH, and dietary intake. PHACTR1 variants explain a modest fraction of variance in phosphate levels. Clinically relevant mainly in the context of kidney disease and bone health.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; functional OR2J3 olfactory receptor enables strong detection of cis-3-hexen-1-ol (the primary compound in cut grass / green plant odors).

AG.description: Heterozygous; one functional and one altered OR2J3 allele, resulting in intermediate sensitivity to green/grassy odor compounds.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; altered OR2J3 receptor function associated with reduced ability to detect green/grassy volatile compounds at typical concentrations.

description: OR2J3 encodes an olfactory receptor expressed in nasal epithelium that detects cis-3-hexen-1-ol and related C6 green-leaf volatiles — the compounds responsible for the fresh-cut grass smell. The rs2590498 variant alters receptor function, affecting sensitivity to this ubiquitous outdoor scent.

sources.name: Keller A et al. (2012) - Genetic variation in a human odorant receptor alters odour perception

notes: Olfactory receptor variants are among the most functionally variable in the human genome. OR2J3 perception differences are a good example of how genotype directly causes qualitatively different sensory experience.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical PTGS2 (COX-2) expression with standard prostaglandin-mediated pain amplification and inflammatory response.

GC.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in COX-2 promoter activity with intermediate prostaglandin production and pain sensitivity.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered PTGS2 promoter activity, modifying prostaglandin E2 synthesis levels and the degree of prostaglandin-mediated pain sensitization.

description: PTGS2 (cyclooxygenase-2, COX-2) is the inducible enzyme that synthesizes prostaglandins from arachidonic acid. Prostaglandins sensitize pain receptors during inflammation. The rs20417 promoter variant alters COX-2 expression levels, influencing the prostaglandin-mediated amplification of pain signals.

sources.name: Cipollone F et al. (2002) - The -765G>C polymorphism of the cyclooxygenase-2 gene

notes: COX-2 is the target of NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, celecoxib). This variant may influence NSAID efficacy and individual differences in inflammatory pain. Not a diagnosis — consult a healthcare provider for pain management.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical TLR9 recognition of unmethylated CpG DNA from viruses and bacteria, with standard type I interferon and inflammatory cytokine induction.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate TLR9 signaling variation with intermediate antiviral cytokine responses upon pathogen recognition.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with differences in TLR9 expression or signaling, modifying type I interferon production and systemic inflammatory response to viral infections.

description: TLR9 (toll-like receptor 9) detects unmethylated CpG DNA motifs characteristic of bacterial and viral pathogens, triggering innate immune responses including type I interferon production. The rs352140 variant influences TLR9 expression or function, modulating the strength and character of antiviral immune responses.

sources.name: Bochud PY et al. (2006) - Toll-like receptor 9 polymorphisms and susceptibility to infectious diseases

notes: TLR9 variants have been studied in viral hepatitis, HIV, herpes, and autoimmune contexts. Polymorphisms affect both susceptibility to infection and response severity. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical interleukin-2 production driving T-cell clonal expansion and adaptive immune responses of standard magnitude.

GT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in IL-2 transcription with intermediate T-cell activation capacity.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered IL-2 promoter activity, modifying T-cell proliferation capacity and the magnitude of adaptive immune activation.

description: IL2 encodes interleukin-2, the primary cytokine driving T-cell proliferation and survival after antigen recognition. The rs2069762 variant influences IL-2 promoter activity, altering the magnitude of T-cell clonal expansion during immune responses — with implications for vaccine response, infection control, and autoimmune risk.

sources.name: Matesanz F et al. (2000) - Interleukin 2 polymorphism and autoimmune disease susceptibility

notes: IL-2 signaling is central to both immune activation and regulatory T-cell maintenance. Variants affect autoimmune disease risk and may influence vaccine immunogenicity. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical corticotropin-releasing hormone levels with standard HPA axis activation and its associated effects on auditory system function during stress.

GA.description: Heterozygous; intermediate CRH expression with moderate impact of stress-activated cortisol cascade on inner ear function and hearing thresholds.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered CRH levels influencing the stress-hormone cascade effects on cochlear blood flow and hearing threshold changes during psychological stress.

description: CRH (corticotropin-releasing hormone) initiates the HPA stress axis. Stress-activated cortisol affects cochlear blood flow and auditory neuron function, causing transient hearing threshold shifts. The rs1876828 variant influences CRH expression, modulating the degree to which psychological stress causes measurable temporary hearing changes.

sources.name: Carlsson PI et al. (2007) - The influence of genetic factors on hearing loss and tinnitus

notes: The stress-hearing connection is clinically observed in sudden sensorineural hearing loss and noise-induced hearing loss, where stress hormones amplify damage. CRH genetic variation is a modest modifier in this pathway.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical kisspeptin signaling through KISS1/GPR54 axis with population-average pubertal timing.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in kisspeptin expression with modest shift in the timing of GnRH pulse initiation at puberty onset.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with a shift in KISS1-driven GnRH pulse activation, potentially advancing or delaying puberty onset relative to population average.

description: KISS1 encodes kisspeptin, the critical neuropeptide that activates GnRH neurons to initiate puberty. The rs2305089 variant influences KISS1 expression, modulating the timing of the kisspeptin-GnRH signaling cascade that triggers the hormonal cascade of puberty onset.

sources.name: Silveira LG et al. (2010) - Mutations of the KISS1 gene in disorders of puberty

notes: Puberty timing is influenced by hundreds of genetic variants plus nutrition, body composition, and endocrine disruptors. KISS1 common variants are modest contributors. Puberty timing disorders require clinical evaluation.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical estrogen receptor beta expression and binding affinity, with standard ER-beta-mediated transcription in reproductive tissues, bone, and brain.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in ER-beta receptor activity with intermediate effects on estrogen-responsive gene expression.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered ESR2 expression or receptor function, affecting estrogen signaling in bone density, mood regulation, and reproductive tissue responsiveness.

description: ESR2 encodes estrogen receptor beta (ER-β), which mediates distinct estrogen effects from ER-α — particularly in bone, brain, cardiovascular tissue, and reproductive organs. The rs1256049 variant alters ER-β expression or function, influencing estrogen sensitivity across multiple target tissues.

sources.name: Ogawa S et al. (2005) - Polymorphisms of estrogen receptor beta gene and bone mineral density

notes: ER-beta has opposing roles to ER-alpha in some tissues. ESR2 variants influence bone density, breast tissue proliferation risk, and mood. Not a diagnosis — consult a healthcare provider for hormonal concerns.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; higher COMT activity methylates and clears catechol estrogens more rapidly, reducing cumulative estrogen metabolite exposure in hormone-sensitive tissues.

CT.description: Heterozygous; intermediate COMT enzyme activity with moderate estrogen methylation rate.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; lower COMT activity slows catechol estrogen methylation, prolonging estrogen metabolite exposure and potentially influencing hormone-sensitive tissue proliferation.

description: COMT (catechol-O-methyltransferase) methylates and inactivates catechol estrogens — the hydroxylated metabolites of estradiol produced by CYP450 enzymes. The rs4633 variant (in linkage with the well-studied Val158Met rs4680) alters COMT activity, influencing the rate at which bioactive estrogen metabolites are cleared from tissues.

sources.name: Lavigne JA et al. (2006) - The COMT polymorphism and estrogen metabolism

notes: COMT variants affect both dopamine and estrogen metabolism — a rare example of one enzyme influencing two major biological systems. Catechol estrogen accumulation is studied in hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer risk. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical luteinizing hormone receptor sensitivity and downstream cAMP signaling in Leydig cells (testosterone production) and granulosa cells (ovulation).

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in LHCGR function with intermediate LH-stimulated steroidogenesis.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered LH receptor binding or signaling efficiency, influencing testosterone production, ovarian response to LH surges, and gonadal steroidogenesis.

description: LHCGR encodes the LH/choriogonadotropin receptor, which mediates the effects of LH (and hCG) on gonads. In males, LH stimulates testicular testosterone synthesis; in females, it triggers ovulation and luteinization. The rs1800447 variant influences receptor sensitivity, modulating the gonadal response to pituitary LH signals.

sources.name: Simoni M et al. (2004) - LH receptor coding variants and male infertility

notes: LHCGR variants are studied in polycystic ovary syndrome, male hypogonadism, and ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome during IVF. Not a diagnosis — consult a reproductive endocrinologist for fertility concerns.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical progesterone receptor expression and ligand sensitivity in uterine, mammary, and brain tissues with standard progesterone-mediated effects.

GC.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in progesterone receptor activity influencing the magnitude of progesterone-mediated gene expression.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified progesterone receptor (PR-A vs PR-B isoform ratio) affecting menstrual cycle regulation, implantation success, and breast tissue progesterone response.

description: PGR encodes the progesterone receptor, which mediates progesterone effects on uterine function, mammary development, and reproductive cycling. The rs1800932 (PROGINS) variant alters PGR isoform expression, influencing sensitivity to progesterone in reproductive target tissues and affecting menstrual, fertility, and breast health outcomes.

sources.name: Manolitsas TP et al. (2003) - Progesterone receptor polymorphism and endometrial cancer

notes: The PROGINS haplotype (which includes rs1800932) alters PGR isoform balance. Progesterone receptor variants are studied in endometrial cancer, breast cancer, and IVF outcomes. Not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical PTGS2 expression with standard prostaglandin synthesis supporting normal ovulation, implantation, and menstrual regulation.

TC.description: Heterozygous; moderate COX-2 expression variation with modest effects on prostaglandin-dependent reproductive processes.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered PTGS2 expression affecting prostaglandin E2 levels critical for follicle rupture at ovulation, corpus luteum function, and endometrial preparation for implantation.

description: PTGS2 (COX-2) synthesizes prostaglandins essential for ovulation, embryo implantation, and menstrual function. Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) is required for follicle rupture — COX-2 knockout mice are infertile. The rs5275 variant in the PTGS2 3'UTR influences mRNA stability and expression, modulating PGE2 production in reproductive tissues.

sources.name: Cipollone F et al. (2002) - The -765G>C polymorphism of the cyclooxygenase-2 gene

notes: NSAID use during the periovulatory period inhibits COX-2 and can reversibly impair ovulation — a real clinical concern for women trying to conceive. Genetic variants that lower COX-2 may parallel this pharmacological effect. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical auditory cortex organization with no variant-associated enhancement of absolute pitch (perfect pitch) ability.

GA.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the ARVCF variant associated in preliminary studies with slightly better tonal memory in trained musicians.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with a modest tendency toward absolute pitch ability in individuals with early musical training, possibly via altered auditory cortex development.

description: ARVCF (armadillo repeat gene deleted in velocardiofacial syndrome) is expressed in auditory cortex development. Preliminary association studies found rs150036 nominally associated with absolute pitch (perfect pitch) ability — the rare capacity to identify musical notes without a reference. Absolute pitch has strong genetic components and requires early musical training to manifest.

sources.name: Theusch E et al. (2009) - Genome-wide study of families with absolute pitch reveals linkage

notes: Perfect pitch occurs in ~1 in 10,000 people and is strongly associated with early musical training before age 7. Genetics provide a predisposition but cannot create perfect pitch without musical exposure. This is one of the most fascinating quirky genetic traits.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical miR-137 microRNA levels with standard regulation of synaptic development target genes including SYNJ1, CAMKK2, and PTGS2.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in miR-137 expression levels with intermediate effects on neuronal maturation target networks.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered miR-137 microRNA levels, modifying the expression of downstream synaptic and neuronal maturation target genes in prefrontal cortex and hippocampus.

description: MicroRNA-137 (miR-137) is a master regulator of synaptic development and neuronal maturation, targeting genes involved in synaptogenesis, dendritic growth, and neuronal differentiation. The rs57095329 variant influences miR-137 expression levels, altering target gene networks in prefrontal cortex and limbic areas. Associations reported in GWAS studies derive primarily from European-ancestry cohorts, limiting cross-population generalizability.

sources.name: Ripke S et al. (2011) - Genome-wide association study identifies five new schizophrenia loci (miR-137)

notes: miR-137 regulates a broad network of synaptic development genes. Findings derive primarily from European-cohort studies, limiting cross-population applicability. This variant's population-level associations reflect microRNA dosage effects on neural circuit development; individual-level prediction is not supported by current evidence.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical OCA2-mediated melanin distribution in iris stroma with standard radial iris pattern.

CA.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in OCA2 activity with intermediate iris pigmentation pattern and ring visibility.

AA.phenotype: Enhanced Limbal Ring / Iris Color Ring Pattern
AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with more defined iris pigmentation patterns including limbal ring visibility and central-peripheral iris color variation.

description: OCA2 encodes the P protein critical for melanin synthesis in iris melanocytes. The rs4778232 variant influences OCA2 expression levels, affecting the distribution pattern of melanin within iris stroma — contributing to the intricate color ring patterns visible in detailed iris photography.

sources.name: Sturm RA et al. (2008) - A single SNP in an evolutionary conserved region determines eye color

notes: Iris pattern complexity (crypts, furrows, rings) is partly heritable but complex. OCA2 variants primarily affect gross color rather than fine pattern. The limbal ring (dark outer iris ring) is considered attractive and is influenced by pigmentation genetics.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical ALDH3A2 fatty aldehyde dehydrogenase activity, standard oxidation of sweat-derived fatty aldehydes contributing to normal body odor profile.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate ALDH3A2 variation with intermediate fatty aldehyde metabolism and intermediate sweat odor characteristics.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified ALDH3A2 enzyme activity, altering the composition of sweat-derived volatile aldehydes and producing a subtly different personal sweat odor signature.

description: ALDH3A2 (fatty aldehyde dehydrogenase) oxidizes medium-chain fatty aldehydes, including those produced by eccrine sweat gland activity. Sweat composition is a major determinant of personal body odor. The rs3741049 variant alters ALDH3A2 activity, modifying the fatty aldehyde component of individual sweat odor profile.
notes: Limited direct evidence for this specific variant's effect on body odor. The primary body odor gene is ABCC11. Sweat odor is influenced by ABCC11 (dry/wet earwax and odorless/odorous axillary sweat — rs17822931), diet, microbiome, and aldehyde metabolism genes. This is a subtle modifier of the fatty aldehyde component specifically.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical ERBB3 signaling in immune regulatory cells with standard self-tolerance mechanisms and average autoimmune predisposition.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate ERBB3 function in immune tissue with modestly elevated autoimmune propensity.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; ERBB3 variant associated with altered neuregulin signaling in thymic development and peripheral immune regulation, modestly increasing broad autoimmune susceptibility.

description: ERBB3 (HER3) encodes a receptor tyrosine kinase in the EGF receptor family. ERBB3 is expressed in thymic epithelium and participates in T-cell selection and tolerance induction. The rs2292239 variant near ERBB3 was identified in multi-disease autoimmune GWAS, suggesting a role in broad immune self-tolerance regulation.

sources.name: Smyth DJ et al. (2008) - A genome-wide association study of nonsynonymous SNPs identifies a type 1 diabetes locus in the interferon-induced helicase (IFIH1) region

notes: ERBB3 variants show associations across multiple autoimmune conditions (T1D, RA, inflammatory bowel disease) suggesting a shared immune regulatory pathway. Effect size per variant is modest. Not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; fully functional tyrosinase enzyme, efficient UV-stimulated melanin synthesis producing strong, lasting tans with UV exposure.

CA.description: Heterozygous; one reduced-function tyrosinase allele, producing intermediate UV-induced melanin synthesis and moderate tanning response.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced tyrosinase enzyme activity associated with impaired UV-induced melanin synthesis, poor tanning ability, and increased UV sensitivity.

description: TYR encodes tyrosinase, the rate-limiting enzyme in melanin synthesis. UV radiation stimulates MC1R to increase tyrosinase activity, producing protective melanin (tanning). The rs1042602 (S192Y) variant reduces tyrosinase catalytic efficiency, impairing UV-induced melanin production and resulting in reduced tanning capacity and increased photosensitivity.

sources.name: Duffy DL et al. (2004) - Interactive effects of MC1R and OCA2 on melanoma risk

notes: The A allele (Tyr192) is common in European populations (~10-25%). Poor tanners have reduced photoprotection and should use higher SPF sunscreens. Not a diagnosis — consult a dermatologist for skin cancer screening.

CC.description: Homozygous reference (Gln27); standard beta-2 adrenergic regulation of cutaneous vasodilation and skin blood flow under sympathetic nervous system control.

CG.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in beta-2 adrenergic receptor function affecting cutaneous vasoconstriction/vasodilation dynamics.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate (Glu27); Gln27Glu substitution alters agonist-induced receptor downregulation, influencing skin blood flow regulation through altered adrenergic vasomotor control.

description: ADRB2 (Gln27Glu, rs1042714) affects beta-2 adrenergic receptor downregulation kinetics. In the skin, beta-2 adrenergic receptors mediate cutaneous vasodilation — influencing thermoregulation, blushing responses, and overall skin microcirculation. The Glu27 variant shows altered agonist-induced internalization compared to Gln27.

sources.name: Gougat C et al. (2004) - ADRB2 polymorphisms and metabolic phenotypes

notes: rs1042714 (Gln27Glu) interacts with rs1042713 (Arg16Gly) as a functional haplotype. Most studies address respiratory and metabolic phenotypes; skin blood flow effects are secondary observations.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical PIGU-associated GPI anchor biosynthesis in melanocytes with standard melanin-producing capacity.

AG.description: Heterozygous; modest variation in GPI-anchored protein expression on melanocyte surface with intermediate pigmentation modulation.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; modified PIGU function affecting GPI-anchored protein presentation on melanocyte membranes, with downstream effects on melanocyte survival, proliferation, or pigment production.

description: PIGU is a component of the GPI transamidase complex responsible for attaching GPI anchors to cell surface proteins in melanocytes. GPI-anchored proteins influence melanocyte signaling, survival, and pigment production. The rs11203042 variant near PIGU affects expression levels, modulating melanocyte function and skin pigmentation.

sources.name: Sulem P et al. (2008) - Two newly identified genetic determinants of pigmentation in Europeans

notes: PIGU variants were identified in Icelandic pigmentation GWAS near the ASIP locus region. The mechanistic link to melanocyte function is inferred from PIGU biology rather than direct experimental demonstration.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical ZC3HC1-mediated cell cycle regulation in vascular smooth muscle cells with standard arterial stiffness progression with age.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate ZC3HC1 variation with intermediate effects on vascular smooth muscle proliferation and arterial wall remodeling over time.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered ZC3HC1 function affecting vascular smooth muscle cell cycle dynamics, contributing to faster arterial stiffening and vascular aging.

description: ZC3HC1 (zinc finger C3HC-type containing 1) is involved in cell cycle regulation and was identified in GWAS studies of coronary artery disease. It modulates vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation and apoptosis — key determinants of arterial wall integrity and the rate of age-related vascular stiffening.

sources.name: Schunkert H et al. (2011) - Large-scale association analysis identifies 13 new susceptibility loci for coronary artery disease

notes: ZC3HC1 is part of the chromosome 7q32.2 coronary artery disease locus. Vascular aging is influenced by blood pressure, lipids, smoking, and multiple genetic factors. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis.

TT.phenotype: Faster Wound Closure / Enhanced Platelet Function
TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical STXBP5L-mediated vesicle fusion in platelets supporting robust platelet secretion and standard wound healing speed.

TC.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in platelet granule release efficiency with intermediate wound healing kinetics.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified STXBP5L function affecting platelet dense granule secretion, potentially altering the speed of initial hemostatic plug formation and subsequent wound healing.

description: STXBP5L (syntaxin binding protein 5-like) regulates SNARE complex-mediated vesicle fusion in platelets, controlling the release of clotting factors from platelet granules. Efficient platelet secretion is critical for rapid wound sealing. The rs35391 variant modulates STXBP5L function, influencing platelet activation kinetics and wound healing speed.

sources.name: Mahaney MC et al. (2011) - Genome-wide association study of platelet function

notes: Wound healing speed is multifactorial — nutrition (vitamin C, zinc), blood supply, infection, and multiple platelet function genes all contribute. This SNP represents one platelet secretion pathway component.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical methionine synthase reductase function, efficiently reactivating methionine synthase and maintaining methylcobalamin cofactor in active form for the methionine cycle.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate MTRR activity reduction with intermediate homocysteine metabolism and methylation cycle efficiency.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; reduced MTRR activity impairs reactivation of methionine synthase, potentially elevating homocysteine, reducing methylation capacity, and affecting DNA methylation patterns relevant to aging.

description: MTRR (methionine synthase reductase) reactivates methionine synthase (MTR) by reducing its cobalamin cofactor. Reduced MTRR function impairs the methionine cycle, elevating homocysteine and reducing S-adenosylmethionine (SAM), the universal methyl donor. This affects DNA methylation, cellular aging, and cardiovascular risk. The rs1801394 (A66G) is a common functional variant.

sources.name: Wilson A et al. (1999) - A common variant in methionine synthase reductase combined with low cobalamin (vitamin B12) increases risk for spina bifida

notes: MTRR A66G interacts with MTHFR C677T and B12/folate status. The G allele frequency is ~40-50% globally. Combined deficiency of MTRR and MTHFR increases neural tube defect risk. B12 supplementation can partially compensate for reduced MTRR activity.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical HSPA1L (Hsp70) induction under cellular stress — heat, oxidative damage, protein misfolding — with standard cytoprotective capacity.

TC.description: Heterozygous; intermediate heat shock protein induction with moderate variation in cellular stress tolerance.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified HSPA1L induction kinetics, affecting the speed and magnitude of the protective heat shock response to cellular stressors relevant to aging and inflammation.

description: HSPA1L encodes heat shock protein 70 family member 1L, a molecular chaperone induced by heat, oxidative stress, and protein damage. Heat shock proteins protect against protein aggregation, a hallmark of aging and neurodegeneration. The rs2227956 variant influences HSPA1L stress inducibility, modulating cellular resilience to proteotoxic stress.

sources.name: Favatier F et al. (2003) - Variation in hsp70 gene expression and HSF activation among human lymphocyte cell lines

notes: Heat shock protein expression declines with age and is impaired in age-related diseases. Exercise and hormesis (mild stress exposure) upregulate HSPs. HSPA1L variants influence the baseline and stress-induced capacity of this protective response.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical poly(ADP-ribose) polymerase 1 activity in detecting and coordinating single-strand DNA break repair, with standard genome maintenance efficiency.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in PARP1 activity with intermediate capacity for rapid DNA single-strand break detection and repair signaling.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified PARP1 catalytic activity affecting the efficiency of single-strand break repair signaling, influencing accumulated DNA damage over time.

description: PARP1 (poly ADP-ribose polymerase 1) is the primary cellular sensor of DNA single-strand breaks, initiating rapid repair by synthesizing poly(ADP-ribose) chains to recruit repair machinery. Efficient PARP1-mediated DNA repair is critical for genome stability, cancer prevention, and aging. The rs3219151 variant alters PARP1 enzymatic properties.

sources.name: Cottet F et al. (2007) - PARP-1 Val762Ala polymorphism and DNA repair capacity

notes: PARP inhibitors (olaparib, niraparib) are important cancer drugs — this variant context illustrates why PARP1 activity variation matters clinically. DNA repair capacity declines with age; individuals with reduced PARP1 variants may accumulate damage faster. Not a diagnosis.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; higher P-glycoprotein (MDR1) efflux activity reduces intestinal drug absorption and increases blood-brain barrier exclusion of substrate drugs.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate P-gp expression with intermediate drug absorption and CNS penetration for ABCB1 substrate medications.

TT.phenotype: Reduced P-gp Efflux / Higher Drug Exposure
TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with reduced P-glycoprotein expression or activity, increasing intestinal absorption and CNS penetration of ABCB1 substrate drugs.

description: ABCB1 (MDR1) encodes P-glycoprotein, an efflux transporter expressed in intestinal epithelium, blood-brain barrier, liver, and kidneys. P-gp pumps many drugs back out of cells, limiting their absorption and CNS penetration. The rs1128503 (C1236T) synonymous variant is in a functional haplotype affecting P-gp expression and activity for substrate drugs including digoxin, HIV protease inhibitors, and some chemotherapy agents.

sources.name: Hoffmeyer S et al. (2000) - Functional polymorphisms of the human multidrug-resistance gene

notes: ABCB1 1236C>T is part of the C1236T-G2677T-C3435T haplotype affecting P-gp function. Substrate drugs include: digoxin, cyclosporine, HIV antiretrovirals (ritonavir, lopinavir), paclitaxel, imatinib, fexofenadine, loperamide, and many CNS drugs. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Always consult a pharmacist or physician before adjusting medications based on genetic information.

AA.description: Homozygous reference (Tyr113); high microsomal epoxide hydrolase activity efficiently converts reactive epoxide intermediates to diols, rapid clearance of carcinogen epoxides.

AG.description: Heterozygous; intermediate EPHX1 activity with moderate epoxide hydrolysis capacity.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate (His113); reduced microsomal epoxide hydrolase activity, slower hydrolysis of reactive epoxide intermediates from PAHs and drugs, potentially increasing toxic metabolite exposure.

description: EPHX1 (microsomal epoxide hydrolase) converts reactive epoxide intermediates — produced from aromatic compounds, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs in smoke), and drug metabolism — to less toxic diols. The rs1051740 (Tyr113His) variant reduces enzymatic activity by ~40%, impairing detoxification of carcinogenic epoxides and some drug metabolites.

sources.name: Hassett C et al. (2001) - Characterization of the human microsomal epoxide hydrolase gene

notes: EPHX1 variants influence risk from cigarette smoke PAHs, occupational chemical exposures, and metabolism of drugs like carbamazepine. The His113 (G) allele frequency is ~25-35% in Europeans. Reduced activity may increase lung, liver, and bladder cancer risk in smokers. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for clinical concerns.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; standard THADA-mediated calcium signaling in pancreatic beta cells, typical insulin secretion capacity and standard T2D susceptibility at this locus.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderately altered THADA function with modest increase in type 2 diabetes risk at this locus.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered THADA (thyroid adenoma-associated protein) function affecting calcium handling in pancreatic beta cells, modestly increasing susceptibility to type 2 diabetes.

description: THADA (thyroid adenoma-associated protein) regulates intracellular calcium signaling. In pancreatic beta cells, calcium dynamics are critical for glucose-stimulated insulin secretion. The rs7578597 variant, identified in multiple T2D GWAS, alters THADA function with downstream effects on insulin secretion capacity and glucose homeostasis.

sources.name: Zeggini E et al. (2008) - Meta-analysis of genome-wide association data and large-scale replication identifies additional susceptibility loci for type 2 diabetes

notes: Type 2 diabetes is highly polygenic with hundreds of contributing loci. This single variant confers a modest odds ratio (~1.15). Lifestyle factors (diet, exercise, weight) have much larger effects than any single genetic variant. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for diabetes screening and management.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical factor XII (Hageman factor) levels supporting normal contact-pathway coagulation activation and standard intrinsic coagulation cascade function.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderately reduced factor XII levels with intermediate contact-pathway coagulation activity.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with significantly reduced Factor XII plasma levels, impairing contact-pathway coagulation activation and potentially reducing thrombosis risk while prolonging aPTT on lab testing.

description: F12 encodes coagulation Factor XII (Hageman factor), which initiates the contact pathway of coagulation. The rs1801020 (46C>T, Thr309Lys) variant in the F12 promoter reduces Factor XII plasma levels by ~50% in heterozygotes. Low Factor XII paradoxically reduces thrombosis risk (particularly venous thrombosis) without causing significant bleeding problems.

sources.name: Renner W et al. (2002) - Coagulation factor XII 46C/T polymorphism and coronary heart disease

notes: Factor XII deficiency does not cause clinical bleeding but can cause spuriously prolonged aPTT on coagulation panels (a common source of unnecessary medical workup). The T allele is protective against venous thromboembolism. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a hematologist for coagulation concerns.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical fibrinogen alpha chain synthesis with standard plasma fibrinogen levels supporting normal clot formation and cardiovascular inflammation.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in fibrinogen production with intermediate plasma fibrinogen concentration.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with higher FGA expression and elevated plasma fibrinogen levels, increasing blood viscosity, thrombosis risk, and contributing to cardiovascular disease risk as an acute phase reactant.

description: FGA encodes fibrinogen alpha chain, a key component of the fibrinogen trimer that is converted to fibrin during clotting. Plasma fibrinogen levels are a cardiovascular risk factor — high fibrinogen increases clot formation tendency and blood viscosity. The rs6050 variant influences FGA expression levels, contributing to inter-individual variation in plasma fibrinogen concentration.

sources.name: Humphries SE et al. (1999) - Fibrinogen genotype and risk of peripheral atherosclerosis

notes: Fibrinogen is both a clotting factor and an acute-phase protein elevated by inflammation, smoking, and metabolic syndrome. High fibrinogen independently predicts cardiovascular events. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for cardiovascular risk assessment.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; lower IL-10 promoter activity producing reduced interleukin-10 levels and less robust anti-inflammatory counter-regulation during immune activation.

CA.description: Heterozygous; moderate IL-10 production with intermediate capacity to dampen inflammatory responses.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; higher IL-10 promoter activity producing greater interleukin-10 levels, stronger anti-inflammatory regulation, and reduced chronic inflammatory tone relevant to cardiovascular protection.

description: IL10 encodes interleukin-10, the principal anti-inflammatory cytokine that suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6). Higher IL-10 reduces systemic inflammation, offering cardiovascular protection through reduced atherosclerotic plaque inflammation. The rs1800872 (-592C/A) promoter variant is one of three IL10 haplotype-defining variants affecting transcriptional activity.

sources.name: Turner DM et al. (2001) - An investigation of polymorphism in the interleukin-10 gene promoter

notes: IL10 rs1800872 is part of the -1082/-819/-592 haplotype (ATA = low producer; GCC = high producer). Low IL-10 producers show higher systemic inflammation markers and may have greater cardiovascular risk. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical SNAP25 SNARE complex function with standard neurotransmitter release kinetics across synaptic terminals.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderate SNAP25 variation with intermediate effects on vesicle fusion rate and neurotransmitter release fidelity.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified SNAP25 protein function or expression, altering synaptic vesicle exocytosis dynamics and the precision of neurotransmitter release at cortical synapses.

description: SNAP25 (synaptosomal-associated protein 25kDa) is a core SNARE complex component essential for synaptic vesicle fusion and neurotransmitter release. SNAP25 variants have been associated with cognitive performance, attention, and IQ in population studies. The rs363050 variant influences SNAP25 expression or SNARE complex efficiency, affecting synaptic transmission fidelity.

sources.name: Gosso MF et al. (2006) - The SNAP-25 gene is associated with cognitive ability

notes: SNAP25 is also implicated in ADHD genetics. The IQ association, while statistically significant, explains a tiny fraction of cognitive variance. Cognitive ability is influenced by hundreds of variants plus education, nutrition, and environment.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical VGLUT1 (vesicular glutamate transporter 1) expression and glutamate packaging efficiency in cortical and hippocampal excitatory synapses.

AG.description: Heterozygous; moderate VGLUT1 variation with intermediate glutamate vesicle filling and synaptic release magnitude.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified VGLUT1 expression affecting the amount of glutamate packaged per synaptic vesicle, influencing the magnitude of cortical excitatory synaptic transmission.

description: SLC17A7 encodes VGLUT1 (vesicular glutamate transporter 1), which loads glutamate into synaptic vesicles at excitatory synapses in cortex and hippocampus. VGLUT1 expression levels determine synaptic glutamate release magnitude and contribute to synaptic strength. The rs3731239 variant influences VGLUT1 expression, modulating excitatory neurotransmission efficiency relevant to cognition and mood.

sources.name: Tordera RM et al. (2007) - Expression of the vesicular glutamate transporter 1 gene is regulated by BDNF

notes: VGLUT1 levels are reduced in depression, schizophrenia, and Alzheimer's disease post-mortem studies. This common variant is a mild modulator of excitatory tone, not a disease variant. Not a diagnosis.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical LINGO1 (leucine-rich repeat and immunoglobulin-like domain 1) inhibitory activity on oligodendrocyte differentiation and axon myelination, with standard nerve conduction velocity.

TC.description: Heterozygous; moderate LINGO1 expression variation with intermediate effects on oligodendrocyte maturation and white matter myelination efficiency.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified LINGO1 inhibitory signaling affecting oligodendrocyte differentiation rates, influencing myelin sheath formation speed and white matter tract integrity.

description: LINGO1 inhibits oligodendrocyte precursor differentiation and axon myelination. LINGO1 antagonism is being studied therapeutically to promote remyelination in multiple sclerosis. The rs11856808 variant modulates LINGO1 expression, influencing the rate and completeness of neural myelination — a key determinant of nerve conduction velocity and white matter-dependent cognitive functions.

sources.name: Salzer JL et al. (2008) - Axonal regulation of Schwann cell myelination by neuregulin 1

notes: LINGO1 variants have been associated with essential tremor and Parkinson's disease in some studies. Myelination efficiency affects processing speed and white matter tract connectivity measurable by DTI MRI. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical leucine-rich repeat kinase 2 activity in dopaminergic neurons, vesicle trafficking, and neuronal maintenance with standard Parkinson's disease risk at this locus.

GA.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in LRRK2 regulatory function with intermediate modification of neuronal kinase signaling.

AA.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with altered LRRK2 kinase pathway regulation affecting dopaminergic neuron maintenance and lysosomal/autophagy function — a modifier of Parkinson's disease susceptibility at the population level.

description: LRRK2 (leucine-rich repeat kinase 2) is a key Parkinson's disease gene. The rare G2019S mutation causes familial Parkinson's; rs2305160 is a common regulatory variant that modulates LRRK2 expression or splicing, acting as a population-level modifier of neuronal LRRK2 signaling relevant to Parkinson's susceptibility.

sources.name: Paisan-Ruiz C et al. (2004) - Cloning of the gene containing mutations that cause PARK8-linked Parkinson's disease

notes: This is a common regulatory variant, NOT the rare pathogenic LRRK2 G2019S mutation (rs34637584) that causes autosomal dominant Parkinson's disease. The common variant rs2305160 confers a much smaller population-level risk modification. This is a risk modifier, not a diagnosis. Consult a neurologist for Parkinson's disease concerns.

CC.description: Homozygous reference; typical transferrin iron-binding capacity and receptor-mediated iron uptake into neurons and oligodendrocytes supporting normal brain iron homeostasis.

CT.description: Heterozygous; moderate variation in transferrin protein function with intermediate brain iron delivery efficiency.

TT.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified transferrin protein function (Pro570Ser substitution) affecting iron delivery to neural tissues, with potential influences on myelination, dopamine synthesis, and age-related brain iron accumulation.

description: TF encodes transferrin, the principal serum iron transport protein. The brain requires iron for dopamine synthesis, myelination, and energy metabolism. The rs1049296 (Pro570Ser) variant alters transferrin iron-binding or receptor interaction, affecting iron delivery to neurons and oligodendrocytes — with implications for cognitive function, myelination efficiency, and age-related brain iron accumulation.

sources.name: Namekata K et al. (2003) - Relationship between transferrin gene polymorphism and Alzheimer's disease

notes: Brain iron accumulation is a feature of multiple neurodegenerative diseases (Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, Huntington's). Transferrin variants affecting iron delivery influence both the risk of iron deficiency anemia and excess iron deposition in aging brains. Not a diagnosis.

GG.description: Homozygous reference; typical RAD51 expression and function in double-strand DNA break repair via homologous recombination, standard genome maintenance capacity.

GC.description: Heterozygous; one copy of the RAD51 5'UTR variant with moderately altered RAD51 expression and intermediate double-strand break repair efficiency.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with modified RAD51 expression levels affecting the efficiency of homologous recombination repair, potentially altering genome stability and cancer susceptibility particularly in BRCA1/2 interaction context.

description: RAD51 is the central recombinase in homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair, loading onto single-stranded DNA at double-strand breaks and searching for homologous sequences. The rs1801320 (135G>C) 5'UTR variant alters RAD51 expression, modifying HR repair capacity. RAD51 interacts with BRCA1 and BRCA2; reduced RAD51 function amplifies cancer risk in BRCA mutation carriers.
notes: The RAD51 135G>C variant is most clinically significant as a BRCA2 cancer risk modifier — BRCA2 carriers with the C allele have substantially higher breast cancer risk. As a standalone population variant, effects are modest. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Genetic counseling is strongly recommended for interpretation, particularly in BRCA1/2 carriers.

AA.description: Homozygous reference; typical caspase-8 expression and function in death receptor-mediated apoptosis, standard immune-mediated tumor cell elimination capacity.

AC.description: Heterozygous; moderate caspase-8 expression variation with intermediate apoptotic signaling capacity in immune effector cells and epithelial tissues.

CC.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with reduced caspase-8 expression or activity, impairing death receptor-mediated programmed cell death and potentially reducing immune-mediated elimination of transformed cells.

description: CASP8 encodes caspase-8, the initiator caspase in extrinsic (death receptor-mediated) apoptosis. Efficient apoptosis is critical for eliminating damaged and pre-cancerous cells. The rs910871 variant influences CASP8 expression, modulating the capacity for programmed cell death — a key cancer surveillance mechanism.

sources.name: Sun T et al. (2007) - A six-nucleotide insertion-deletion polymorphism in the CASP8 promoter associated with risk of multiple cancers

notes: Caspase-8 variants have been associated with risk of breast, lung, colorectal, and cervical cancers. Reduced apoptotic efficiency is one mechanism by which transformed cells escape elimination. This is a risk factor, not a diagnosis. Consult a healthcare provider for cancer screening guidance.

TT.description: Homozygous reference; typical RANKL promoter activity driving standard osteoclast differentiation and bone resorption homeostasis.

TG.description: Heterozygous; moderate RANKL expression variation with intermediate osteoclast activation and bone resorption activity.

GG.description: Homozygous alternate; associated with higher RANKL expression increasing osteoclastogenesis, bone resorption rate, and potentially contributing to lower bone mineral density and higher fracture risk.

description: TNFSF11 encodes RANKL (receptor activator of NF-κB ligand), the primary driver of osteoclast differentiation and activation. Higher RANKL expression shifts the OPG/RANKL balance toward bone resorption. The rs1884444 variant alters RANKL promoter activity, modifying osteoclastogenesis and bone turnover rates.
